DeepEP SM count default changed via config rename. Parent code used `set_deepep_num_sms(config.moe_deepep_num_sms)` where `moe_deepep_num_sms` had a hard default of 20. The diff replaces this with `moe_flex_dispatcher_num_sms`, falling back to 20 only when it is None. This is preserved for DeepEP, BUT for the HybridEP path the semantics changed: parent used `moe_hybridep_num_sms` (default None) for `num_sms_dispatch_api`/`num_sms_combine_api`; the diff now feeds `moe_flex_dispatcher_num_sms` into those. If the unified `moe_flex_dispatcher_num_sms` default differs from the old per-backend defaults (e.g. it defaults to None where HybridEP relied on None-means-auto, or to a fixed number that caps SMs lower than before), the dispatch/combine comm kernels can be launched with fewer SMs than previously, exposing all-to-all comm latency (gpu-util/bubble). Verify the new field's default matches both prior backend defaults.
findings.suggested_benchmark: MoE all-to-all dispatch/combine throughput with HybridEP and DeepEP backends, comparing num_sms actually used before vs after the config rename

findings.claim: New NCCL EP hot path introduces a device-to-host sync every forward. In `_NCCLEPManager.get_permuted_hidden_states_by_experts` (dynamic-shape path), `num_valid = int(self.tokens_per_expert.sum().item())` calls `.item()` on a device tensor, forcing a CUDA sync + DtoH copy on every MoE layer forward. This serializes the GPU with the host and can create pipeline bubbles, especially at scale with many MoE layers. DeepEP/HybridEP paths were designed to avoid such per-iteration syncs on the critical path. The `static_shape=True` path avoids it, but it is gated behind sm100+ and is not the default.
findings.suggested_benchmark: Per-layer MoE forward latency with ncclep dynamic-shape (static_shape=False) measuring GPU idle time from tokens_per_expert.sum().item() sync

static rule 'full-cuda-sync-in-overlap-path' matched antipattern: Calling the global torch.cuda.synchronize() (full device barrier) to order work against a side stream, which serializes all compute/comm and defeats overlap; stream.wait_stream should be used instead. (matched: 'torch.cuda.synchronize()')

In the experimental FSDP hot path, `DBuffer.rendezvous(mesh_axis)` calls `symm_mem.rendezvous(self.local_buffer, group=...)` on every `unshard_parameters()` (per forward) and every `reduce_gradients()` (per backward). The buffer here (`_unsharded_model_weight.local_buffer` / `partial_grad.local_buffer`) has its storage resized (release_storage/reallocate_storage) each step, and for the reduce path `partial_grad` is a freshly-allocated DBuffer per call. `symm_mem.rendezvous` is a synchronizing/registration step that in this codebase's own inference usage (megatron/core/inference/symmetric_memory.py) is done exactly once at buffer creation and cached, never per-collective. Doing rendezvous once per microbatch-step introduces a repeated, per-step registration/handshake on the collective critical path that the inference code deliberately amortizes. This can add latency and cross-rank sync bubbles to every all-gather and reduce-scatter.
findings.suggested_benchmark: Profile per-step forward+backward wall time and NCCL kernel launch gaps for a multi-microbatch, multi-step FSDP run with use_symm_mem=True vs False; specifically measure time attributed to symm_mem.rendezvous per step (it should ideally be amortized to once).

This PR extends the fine-grained activation-offloading feature to be compatible with (and integrate into) TE CUDA graph capture/replay, adding a dedicated _cuda_graph_stream/_cuda_graph_event, a replay-mode delayed-commit path (delay_offload_until_cuda_graph -> push_offload_groups/flush_delayed_groups), and new tuning knobs (activation_offload_fraction, delta_offload_bytes_across_pp_ranks). These are all offload-management (D2H/H2D transfer) mechanisms — the same class as the retrieved historical regressions where offload copies trade transfer bubbles for VRAM. The new knobs default to non-aggressive values (fraction=1.0, delta=0), and offloading is only active behind --fine-grained-activation-offloading, so this is not a change-of-default regression like the historical c59ac8b case (cpu_offloading=True by default). No new unconditional/hot-path offload was introduced.
findings.suggested_benchmark: MoE + EP + TE CUDA graph training step: measure throughput and per-step CPU launch overhead with --offload-modules core_attn attn_proj expert_fc1 and --delay-offload-until-cuda-graph on/off; verify flush_delayed_groups D2H work is actually hidden in the post-replay EP A2A sync gap and not serialized on the compute stream.

The new event-wait scan in `_ParamAndGradBucketGroup.start_grad_sync` iterates over every bucket and every param on each grad-sync call, doing a `getattr(param, '_cudagraph_wgrad_ready_event', None)` per param plus set membership bookkeeping. `start_grad_sync` is a per-iteration/backward hot path invoked for every bucket group each step. When partial/local CUDA graphs are NOT in use (the common case), `_cudagraph_wgrad_ready_event` is never set, so this nested loop is pure overhead added to the reduction hot path with no benefit. Consider gating the entire scan behind a cheap flag (e.g. only run when local cuda graphs are enabled) rather than scanning all params unconditionally.
findings.suggested_benchmark: Micro-benchmark start_grad_sync over many buckets/params with local CUDA graphs disabled; compare per-step grad-reduce dispatch time before/after.

This revert re-introduces an unconditional `torch.cuda.empty_cache()` call inside `save_checkpoint_and_time()` (megatron/training/training.py, immediately after the `free_overlap_buffers` loop). `empty_cache()` returns all cached free blocks to the CUDA driver and forces a device synchronize, so every checkpoint save now pays a stall plus the cost of re-growing the caching allocator's pool afterward. Because this runs on the checkpoint-save path (not per-iter), the impact is bounded, but with frequent/async checkpointing the periodic empty_cache defragmentation cost and post-save allocator re-warm can measurably slow the iterations following a save. This is the classic allocator-config knob mechanism, here applied unconditionally rather than behind an opt-in flag.
findings.suggested_benchmark: Measure iteration time immediately before/after a checkpoint save (with and without the empty_cache workaround) to quantify allocator re-warm + sync overhead, and end-to-end throughput with periodic async checkpointing enabled.

findings.claim: 此改动触及 offload-management（memory-footprint），该类问题历史上主要在 [TP-aware (num_attention_heads_per_partition); dynamic inference engine, tp1pp1 test cases; tensor_model_parallel_size passed to context, only active when cpu_offloading=True (CPU activation/weight offloading path, TE-backed)] 配置下显现；The diff extends fine-grained activation offloading to a new module granularity ("fused_group_mlp"), adds TE non-offloadable tensor detection, and adjusts CPU-p

The new `fused_group_mlp` offload group (OffloadTensorGroup with use_cpu_pool=False, DiffLine ~361 in fine_grained_activation_offload.py) offloads the *entire* fused grouped-MLP input to host memory via off_interface/group_commit in TEGroupedMLP.forward (experts.py ~611-629). This is a hot MoE forward path; every forward pushes `permuted_local_hidden_states` D2H and reloads it H2D before backward. Because the fused_group_mlp group cannot use the CPU pool (shapes not known ahead of time), each iteration re-allocates a fresh pinned CPU buffer instead of reusing pooled buffers, adding transfer bubbles and allocation churn on the offload/reload streams. This matches the historical offload-management mechanism (moving activations across device/host to reclaim VRAM, incurring transfer bubbles). Verify overlap coverage: if the whole fused MLP input reload does not overlap enough compute (single large group per layer), it can stall the compute stream.
findings.suggested_benchmark: MoE fine-grained offload throughput with --offload-modules fused_group_mlp vs baseline expert_fc1/moe_act; measure step time and reload-stream idle bubbles.

此改动触及 deferred-free-peak（memory-management），该类问题历史上主要在 [pipeline model parallel (pp>1) required; requires gradient_accumulation_fusion; interacts with sequence_parallel/TP in the wgrad all-gather path, requires world_size(parallelization_group) > 1 (fully-parallel dist-checkpoint load, typically DP group), distributed checkpoint fully-parallel load across a parallelization ProcessGroup (e.g. data-parallel replicas)] 配置下显现；This diff removes a checkpoint-time workaround that freed overlap param-gather buffers and called torch.cuda.empty_cache() to make GPU headroom for async D2H ch

The number of concurrently created asyncio worker tasks changed from `num_workers = self.parallel_generation_tasks // groups_per_worker` to `self.parallel_generation_tasks` full tasks (`tasks = [asyncio.create_task(generate_task()) for _ in range(self.parallel_generation_tasks)]`). Combined with the new `get_rl_parallel_generation_tasks(args)` which multiplies `(rl_generation_lag + 1)` by `grpo_prompts_per_step` (and by `grpo_group_size` for R granularity), the effective parallel_generation_tasks can be far larger than the old flat default of 512. In R/G granularity the semaphore is per-rollout / released early, so many more concurrent in-flight agenerate() calls (and downstream reward computations) can be launched simultaneously than before, increasing peak memory / event-loop pressure and the httpx connection limit (`concurrency_limit` now scales with the larger value). Verify the intended in-flight cap in real configs.
findings.suggested_benchmark: Run gpt_grpo throughput test with R/G submission granularity and large grpo_prompts_per_step; measure peak GPU/host memory and number of simultaneous in-flight agenerate() requests vs. parent.

findings.claim: The diff introduces an opt-in activation-recompute path for the GDN output norm. When config.recompute_granularity=='selective' and 'gdn_norm_out' is in recompute_modules, `_gated_norm_and_a2a` (the RMSNorm + reshape/transpose/contiguous + HP->CP all-to-all) is wrapped in tensor_parallel.CheckpointWithoutOutput().checkpoint(...) and its output is discarded via discard_output_and_register_recompute(out). This is the classic recompute tradeoff: it saves the stored norm_out activation at the cost of recomputing the norm AND re-running the tensor_a2a_hp2cp all-to-all collective(s) during the backward pass. Because the recomputed region contains a CP all-to-all (and in the thd/packed case a loop of per-sequence all-to-alls plus a torch.cat), the backward pass will incur an extra round of CP communication + concat per GDN layer, adding latency/bubble on the CP group. This is expected/intended behavior (memory-for-compute+comm), but reviewers should be aware that unlike a pure pointwise recompute, this region includes a collective, so the recompute cost is higher than a typical norm recompute.
findings.suggested_benchmark: Compare GDN layer fwd+bwd step time and peak memory with recompute_modules=['gdn_norm_out'] vs baseline, especially at CP>1 and with thd packed sequences, to quantify the extra all-to-all cost in backward.

findings.claim: The recompute path is gated behind explicit opt-in (recompute_granularity=='selective' and 'gdn_norm_out' in recompute_modules), and defaults to False (self.recompute_norm_out=False). The default code path is functionally unchanged (the original inline norm+a2a is now just moved into the _gated_norm_and_a2a closure and called directly). Therefore no regression is introduced for existing/default configs; the only cost lands on users who explicitly enable this feature. No unconditional recompute or hidden sync was added.

This diff FIXES rather than introduces the historically-recorded buffer-sizing hazard. Historical case #11 in dynamic_inference_functional_tests.md records that MambaSlotAllocator.intermediate_ssm_out/intermediate_conv_out are sized (num_mamba_layers x MAX_INTERMEDIATE_OFFSETS_PER_REQUEST(=3) x max_requests) and can balloon to ~98 GiB at default max_requests=256, exceeding H100 capacity. In the parent, _allocate_mamba_cache computed max_slots = total_bytes // per_slot_bytes and allocated the scratch (intermediate_*) buffers ON TOP of that durable cache, so real GPU usage silently exceeded prefix_caching_mamba_gb and could OOM at startup. The diff now reserves scratch_bytes = MAX_INTERMEDIATE_OFFSETS_PER_REQUEST * self.max_requests * per_slot_bytes from the budget first (durable max_slots = (total_bytes - scratch_bytes) // per_slot_bytes) and raises a ValueError when the budget cannot fit scratch + 1 durable slot. This directly addresses the recorded over-allocation. No new perf regression introduced here.
findings.suggested_benchmark: Startup memory footprint of MambaSlotAllocator across max_requests in {16,64,256} confirming total durable+scratch <= prefix_caching_mamba_gb and no OOM.

findings.claim: The new memory-only-mode warning branch (is_hybrid_model + enable_prefix_caching but no prefix_caching_mamba_gb) only logs; it does not change hot-path behavior. It correctly documents that Mamba state caching / prefill skipping are disabled so prefix-caching latency benefit is forgone. This is a diagnostic aid, not a regression, and is only hit once at construction (cold path).

This diff ADDS the delay-wgrad `backward_dw()` and `set_for_recompute_input_layernorm()` methods to `FusedMLASelfAttention`, which previously had neither (they lived only on the base `MLASelfAttention`, referencing `linear_q_down_proj`/`linear_kv_down_proj` that don't exist on the fused variant). static rule 'tensor-item-in-forward-arg' matched antipattern: Calling .item() on a device tensor to pass a Python scalar into a kernel/op on the per-forward hot path forces a device-to-host sync and stalls the GPU pipeline. (matched: 'cu_seqlens[-1].item())')

`_build_thd_cp_a2a_perm(cu_seqlens, self.cp_size, seq_len)` is invoked on every GDN forward (THD + cp_size>1 path) and rebuilds several device tensors from scratch each call: `torch.arange(t_global)`, `torch.bucketize`, `torch.diff`, `torch.where`, plus the scatter `inv[idx] = positions`. The resulting permutation (`thd_cp_a2a_idx` / `thd_cp_a2a_inv`) is a pure function of (cu_seqlens, cp_size, t_global), which are stable across microbatches with a fixed packing layout. Unlike the sibling helper `_build_head_perm_for_split_sections`, which is memoized with `@lru_cache(maxsize=8)`, this per-sequence permutation helper is NOT cached, so the small index-tensor construction and its op launches repeat every step. This is the same mechanism as the historical get_seq_chunk_ids_for_reordering case (small chunk-id tensor rebuilt per attention call). Recommend memoizing keyed on (cu_seqlens content / a hashable signature, cp_size, t_global), e.g. an lru_cache on a tuple-ified cu_seqlens or a module-level dict.
findings.suggested_benchmark: Microbenchmark GDN forward with THD packed sequences and cp_size=2/4, fixed cu_seqlens across N steps; compare wall time / CPU op-launch count with vs without memoizing _build_thd_cp_a2a_perm.

findings.claim: In BridgeCommunicator.__init__ the new line `self.bridge_pg = self._get_or_create_bridge_pg(bridge_ranks)` calls dist.new_group(ranks, backend='nccl') (bridge_communicator.py:_get_or_create_bridge_pg). dist.new_group() is a COLLECTIVE that must be entered by *every* rank in the default (WORLD) process group with identical ordered arguments, even ranks not in the target subgroup. BridgeCommunicator objects are constructed via MultiModulePipelineCommunicator._build_bridge_comms(), which loops over the full topology and instantiates one BridgeCommunicator per (src,dest) module edge on *all* ranks. But bridge_ranks = sorted(set(src_tp_leaders)|set(dest_tp_leaders)) is computed from the two grids' leader sets and is identical across all ranks for a given edge, and the pre-existing dist.barrier() at the end of __init__ is entered by everyone too, so ordering is consistent — this matches the historical mismatched-collective-config pattern (new_group called with a rank subset) only if any rank ever short-circuits the constructor before reaching _get_or_create_bridge_pg. Verify that no early-return/exception path (e.g. grids the rank is not part of) skips this new_group call while other ranks reach it; a divergent skip would deadlock (all ranks must call new_group).
findings.suggested_benchmark: Multi-module (>=3 modules) init on N ranks where some ranks belong to none of a given edge's grids; assert init completes without hang and get_process_group_ranks(bridge_pg) matches on all callers.

此改动触及 fused-backend-swap（kernel-efficiency），该类问题历史上主要在 [tensor-parallel with --sequence-parallel required when tp_size>1; MoE and MTP explicitly unsupported (asserts), MoE (token-wise weighted SwiGLU path, weighted_bias_swiglu_impl), inference dynamic batching (TP per-partition heads used)] 配置下显现；该 diff 强制禁用 Transformer Engine 版本的 cross entropy loss fusion，要求改用 native 实现（在 config/arguments 加断言，并把功能测试配置从 impl='te' 改为 'native'）。这本质上是把一个融合内核后端换成另一种实现——即在保持

This diff swaps the cross-entropy fused backend from TransformerEngine's specialized `te_parallel_cross_entropy` (parallel_cross_entropy) kernel to the `native` `fused_vocab_parallel_cross_entropy` path, and hard-forbids `te` via new asserts in both model_parallel_config.py (post_init) and arguments.py (validate_args). It also flips the release/benchmark GB200 and DeepSeek-V3 proxy configs from `--cross-entropy-fusion-impl te` to `native`. The `te` branch in language_module.compute_language_model_loss dispatched to TE's fused vocab-parallel CE kernel (passing the TP group for the reduction), which on the affected hardware (GB200 release configs) was the intended fast path. Forcing `native` may reduce last-stage throughput where the TE kernel was faster. This is the inverse direction of the historical fused-backend-swap case (adopting TE for throughput); here the specialized fused backend is being removed. The change is explicitly justified as a stability fix (TE CE fusion disabled 'due to stability issues'), so any throughput delta is an accepted tradeoff, not an unintended regression. Note that `native` is still a fused implementation, so the loss step is not falling back to the unfused vocab_parallel_cross_entropy.
findings.suggested_benchmark: End-to-end training step throughput on gpt3_15b_8t_release_gb200 and deepseekv3_proxy_flex release configs, comparing cross_entropy_fusion_impl='te' vs 'native' for the last-pipeline-stage loss computation.

findings.claim: In TEFusedMLPWithGroupedLinear.forward (hot path, called every microbatch/layer), two tensors are allocated on every call: `tokens_per_expert = torch.full((1,), total_tokens, ...)` and `scales = torch.ones(total_tokens, ...)`. The `scales` tensor scales with token count (batch*seq) and is a fresh device allocation each forward, adding per-call allocation + kernel launch overhead that does not exist in the base TEFusedMLP.forward (which just calls `self._fused_impl[0](hidden_states)`). These are recomputed identically each step (given constant total_tokens) and are not cached, unlike the properly-cached `_fused_impl` and `_recipe`.
findings.suggested_benchmark: Microbench TEFusedMLPWithGroupedLinear.forward vs TEFusedMLP.forward at fixed shape, measuring per-call allocation/latency of tokens_per_expert/scales creation.

findings.claim: 此改动触及 low-precision-state（memory-footprint），该类问题历史上主要在 [interacts with CP/SP/TP padding, distributed optimizer (use_distributed_optimizer), distributed optimizer (ZeRO-style DP param sharding); test uses TP=4, DP] 配置下显现；The diff introduces fp8_param/fp4_param toggles and fp8_model_init contexts that cast initialized parameters to FP8/FP4 and control the precision of the weight

The default CUDA-graph token-count distribution is switched from the previous dense LINEAR grid ([1,2,4] + range(8,256,8) + range(256,max+1,16)) to EXPONENTIAL, which captures only ~log2(max_tokens) sizes (e.g. [128,64,32,16,8,4,2,1]). For DECODE-only graphs, a real active batch is padded up to the nearest captured token_count; with exponential spacing the gaps at mid/high range are large (e.g. 65..128 requests all pad up to the 128 graph), so decode steps waste compute on padding relative to the old dense grid. Because EXPONENTIAL is now the module default (sizing_distribution defaults to EXPONENTIAL in both _calculate_cuda_graph_token_counts and generate_cuda_graph_batch_dimensions_list, and InferenceConfig.cuda_graph_sizing_distribution defaults to EXPONENTIAL), existing users get this coarser coverage without opting in. The added smallest-decode-graph mitigation (1x/2x lcm(spec_unit,tp_size)) only backfills the two smallest shapes, not the mid-range gaps.
findings.suggested_benchmark: Decode-only per-step latency at active batch sizes just above each exponential boundary (e.g. 65, 129) comparing EXPONENTIAL vs LINEAR distribution; measure padded_active_sample_count and tok/s.

findings.claim: In mixed prefill+decode mode under the new EXPONENTIAL default, the inner loop now iterates over a geometric P-grid `for prefill_req_count in p_values` (p_values = [1,2,4,...,max_requests]) for EVERY prefill token count, whereas the parent captured a single mixed dimension per token count (fixed cuda_graph_mixed_prefill_request_count). This multiplies the number of captured mixed graphs by ~log2(max_requests), increasing graph-capture time at startup and the CUDA-graph mempool memory reserved for capture. Confirm the added graphs are actually matched/used at runtime; otherwise this is capture work + reserved pool memory with little payoff.
findings.suggested_benchmark: Measure graph-capture wall time and pool_reserved_bytes (via the new _cuda_graph_mempool_bytes) for EXPONENTIAL vs LINEAR at large max_requests; count total captured mixed graphs.

The new helper must allocate a persistent 1-e

The new `_set_skip_fp8_weight_update_tensor` helper (cuda_graphs.py, ~line 71) correctly preserves the persistent-tensor semantics that the historical `boolean-guard-misfire`/skip-fp8-weight-update regression cared about: on the TE>=2.15 path it allocates the 1-element CUDA scalar exactly once (guarded by `if qstate.skip_fp8_weight_update_tensor is None`) and thereafter uses `.fill_(skip)` in place, so `data_ptr()` stays stable across cudagraph replays and no per-microbatch GPU allocation/H2D copy is introduced. This is the correct fix pattern, not a regression. The only latent concern: this is a hot per-microbatch path (called from `_CudagraphReplayNode.forward` at ~line 636), and the added `hasattr(FP8GlobalStateManager, 'set_skip_fp8_weight_update_tensor')` / attribute lookups run on every applicable microbatch. That overhead is negligible (pure Python attribute checks, no allocation), so no material throughput regression is introduced by this diff.

此改动触及 cudagraph-enablement（compilation），该类问题历史上主要在 [inference; expert-parallel path has special handling (decode_only_cuda_graphs / EP all-reduce guard against NCCL hang), expert parallel (EP>1) combined with tensor + data parallel; MoE dynamic inference, distributed optimizer with fp8-param-gather; tp=2 in tests] 配置下显现；The commit adds conditional param.grad dereferencing logic specifically to support full-iteration (FWD-BWD) CUDA graphability in Megatron-FSDP, and exposes a --

The new `megatron_fsdp_cuda_graph_mode` path in ParamAndGradBuffer._reset_buckets skips dereferencing `param.grad` / `param.decoupled_grad` / `dist_main_grad[name]._local_tensor` to keep references valid for CUDA graph replay. This is guarded by `if not self.ddp_config.megatron_fsdp_cuda_graph_mode`, so the default (mode=False) path is unchanged and there is no regression for the common case. When the flag IS enabled AND the gradient shard is not a view of the FSDP sharded grad buffer (i.e. a casted-copy is created because main_params_dtype != main_grads_dtype and use_decoupled_grad is off), the retained casted-copy persistently increases memory utilization, as the docstring itself warns. This is an opt-in CUDA-graph-enablement trade-off, not a broad regression, but callers who enable cuda_graph_impl without decoupled/matched grad dtypes will pay a persistent memory cost.
findings.suggested_benchmark: Peak GPU memory (torch.cuda.max_memory_allocated) with cuda_graph_impl=full_iteration + megatron_fsdp_main_params_dtype=fp32/main_grads_dtype=bf16 (mismatched, non-decoupled) vs decoupled/matched-dtype config.

findings.claim: This PR adds an opt-in config `megatron_fsdp_enable_fine_grained_param_gather` (default False) that, when set, forces the fine-grained per-module parameter all-gather hook path in Megatron-FSDP (mcore_fsdp_adapter.py line ~202 ORs it into `enable_fine_grained_param_gather_hook`). Per its own docstring, this mode 'increases the overlap between parameter all-gather and forward computation, at the cost of more frequent communication calls' and only unshards rowwise params of recompute modules to save MXFP8 memory. This is exactly the memory-vs-communication knob choice described by the reshard-tradeoff historical pattern: coalesced (bucketed) gathers give fewer/larger collectives (better throughput) whereas fine-grained per-module gathers save memory at the cost of many smaller collectives. Because it is opt-in and defaults to False, it does NOT introduce a regression on the default path; the tradeoff is only paid when a user explicitly enables the flag. Reported as a suggestion so reviewers confirm the throughput cost of the extra collective calls is acceptable when the flag is on with mxfp8/recompute.
findings.suggested_benchmark: MXFP8 FSDP training with fine-grained activation recompute: compare throughput and peak memory with --megatron-fsdp-enable-fine-grained-param-gather on vs off to quantify the comm-call-count vs memory tradeoff.

This PR flips the MSC feature default from enabled-when-installed to disabled-by-default. In msc_utils.py the flag construction changes from `MultiStorageClientFeature = _FeatureFlag(_msc_available)` to `_FeatureFlag(default=False)`, and in arguments.py the CLI toggle changes from `--disable-msc` (store_false, default enabled) to `--enable-msc` (store_true, default False). This is the classic config-toggle-perf-feature pattern: a static default value now routes to a different code path than before for any deployment where the multi-storage-client library is installed and paths were being served via MSC (regular filesystem paths and msc:// URLs). Previously such installs got MSC automatically; now, unless the operator adds --enable-msc, MSC is silently off. For MSC users this is a functional/perf behavior change on the checkpoint/data I/O path. Note this is the intended behavior of the PR (opt-in), so it is a deliberate default flip rather than an accidental regression, but it does change the perf-relevant path for existing MSC deployments that relied on auto-enable.
findings.suggested_benchmark: Checkpoint save/load throughput to an object-store / MSC-backed path with the multistorageclient package installed, comparing pre-PR (auto-enabled) vs post-PR default (disabled) without passing --enable-msc.

findings.claim: static rule 'cuda-stream-in-init' matched antipattern: Allocating a fresh torch.cuda.Stream() inside __init__ (or per-object construction), so N objects create N CUDA streams instead of sharing one. (matched: 'torch.cuda.Stream()')

The default of `cuda_graph_use_single_mempool` is flipped from False to True in TransformerConfig (transformer_config.py:894). This flag is not limited to the new full-iteration/optimizer shared-pool path — it is also consumed by the pre-existing `local` cuda graph implementation (megatron/core/transformer/cuda_graphs.py: CudaGraphManager.global_mempool at lines ~1413-1571). Per the *removed* docstring, when single-mempool is True for the `local` impl, 'all cudagraphs may only be used once per step' and cannot be reused across microbatches, which 'may greatly increase the number of cudagraphs created when the number of microbatches is high.' Flipping the default silently changes behavior for existing `local` cuda-graph users who never opted in, potentially inflating graph count / capture time / memory when num_microbatches is high. The new docstring narrows the description to only the full_iteration path, hiding this cross-cutting effect.
findings.suggested_benchmark: Enable cuda_graph_impl='local' with high num_microbatches (e.g. 16-32) and compare graph-capture count, capture time, and peak reserved memory between cuda_graph_use_single_mempool=False vs True.

In combined_forward_backward_step (per-microbatch hot path), the EP-overlap+FSDP code path re-runs find_megatron_fsdp(f_model) and then loops over ALL layers calling layer_plan.set_fsdp_reshard_hooks(...) on every forward call. This wiring is re-done for each microbatch rather than once at setup. Each set_fsdp_reshard_hooks call also does isinstance checks and re-registers lambdas via set_post_forward_hook/set_post_backward_hook. For deep MoE models with many layers × many microbatches this adds per-step Python overhead on the critical path that co-schedules comm and compute; if the hook wiring is not idempotently cheap it can perturb the overlap timing the schedule relies on.

HybridStack.forward now routes through checkpointed_forward when config.recompute_granularity=='full' and self.training, wrapping (chunks of) layers in tensor_parallel.checkpoint / te_checkpoint. This is the classic activation-recompute mechanism (matched historical MTP/TransformerBlock cases): it trades extra recomputation FLOPs during backward for reduced stored activations. The overhead is opt-in (only when recompute is configured), so it is expected/intended rather than a regression; but note that under 'block' method with recompute_num_layers set, previously HybridStack ignored recompute entirely (parent forward had NO recompute branch), meaning any hybrid config that set recompute_granularity=full will now actually pay the recompute compute cost that it silently did not before. Users who had that config set (and were relying on it being a no-op) will see a throughput drop from the added backward recompute.
findings.suggested_benchmark: Hybrid model train step throughput with recompute_granularity=full, method in {block,uniform}, comparing parent (no-op) vs this commit.

In NVLSAllGatherVDispatcher.dispatch_preprocess, the shared-expert forward is launched on SharedExpertMLP.stream reading the input `hidden_states` tensor (`apply_module(self.shared_experts)(hidden_states)`), while the main stream continues into `token_dispatch` where the same `hidden_states` view is consumed by the AGV/permute path. The side stream is only synchronized on entry (`stream.wait_stream(current_stream())`) but the input `hidden_states` tensor is NOT protected with `record_stream(stream)` (nor cloned) before the main stream may free or overwrite it. This is exactly the historical dual-stream shared-experts race (#26440/344a0017c) where the shared-expert compute on the side stream can race with the main stream's reuse/free of the input buffer, causing correctness issues or CUDA-caching-allocator reuse hazards that manifest as GPU bubbles/stalls when the allocator has to hold the buffer.
findings.suggested_benchmark: NVLS inference decode with moe_shared_expert_overlap enabled; verify shared-expert output correctness under repeated allocator churn and measure GPU util vs the serial path

findings.claim: The new `--inference-cuda-graph-all-prefills` flag widens the prefill/mixed CUDA-graph token budget from `max_requests * (num_speculative_tokens + 1)` up to the full `max_tokens`. When enabled together with non-decode CG capture, the graph token-count list grows to include large token-count shapes (e.g. the added test shows counts jumping from decode-capped [80,72,...] to [1024, 512, 80, ...]). Each extra shape is a full prefill/mixed CUDA graph capture, sharply increasing warmup capture time and, more importantly, capture-time GPU memory (graph pools). The PR itself acknowledges this risk: the updated unit test comment says 'capturing mixed graphs across the full range will OOM' and forces `use_cuda_graphs_for_non_decode_steps=False` in `test_dynamic_bs` to avoid OOM. This is the wasted-compilation/over-capture side of the historical cudagraph-enablement mechanism — capture is possible but capturing far more prefill shapes than needed costs warmup time and memory. Default remains False, so this is opt-in, limiting severity.
findings.suggested_benchmark: Measure warmup capture wall-time and peak GPU memory with --inference-cuda-graph-all-prefills on vs off at large max_tokens (e.g. max_tokens=16384, max_requests=256), and confirm no OOM at expected max_tokens.

findings.claim: MoELayer moved token-dispatcher selection out of the `train()` override (invoked once per train/eval mode switch) into `forward()`, so the `hasattr(self, "_inference_token_dispatcher")` check plus dispatcher/shared_expert_overlap reassignment now executes on every MoE layer, every forward step, instead of once. This is the classic move of one-time setup onto the hot path. In practice the work is cheap (an attribute lookup + a couple of assignments and a classmethod bool read), so the per-step cost is small, but it is strictly redundant recompute since the value only changes when the engine transitions between active/inactive. Consider caching and only reassigning when InferenceMode state actually flips.
findings.suggested_benchmark: Microbench MoELayer.forward with config.transformer_impl=='inference_optimized' comparing per-step overhead before/after the dispatcher-selection block; and end-to-end MoE inference decode step latency.

When repeated layer mode is on,

findings.claim:
static rule 'device-cumsum-into-zeros-buffer' matched antipattern: Computing a small prefix-sum on-device via torch.cumsum(..., out=...) into a freshly torch.zeros-allocated GPU buffer, incurring an extra kernel launch and allocation per step instead of computing the tiny cumulative sum on CPU and doing a single async H2D copy. (matched: 'torch.cumsum(
            self.request_query_lengths[prefill_idxs : self.total_request_count],
            dim=0,
      ')

static rule 'tensor-from-pylist-on-device' matched antipattern: Constructing a tensor from a Python list literal with a non-CPU device forces a CPU materialization plus a host-to-device copy on every call; use torch.zeros/ones/full/empty directly on the device instead. (matched: 'torch.tensor([42], device=')

static rule 'async-busy-poll-sleep-zero' matched antipattern: An async loop that unconditionally does work every iteration and only yields via asyncio.sleep(0), busy-polling the event loop and burning CPU instead of waiting on an event/condition. (matched: 'await asyncio.sleep(0)')

The new `disable_ep_consensus` branch in `run_engine_with_coordinator` replaces the previous sleep-based idle behavior with a busy spin: when `local_pending == 0` it unconditionally calls `controller.dummy_forward()` (a real GPU forward, followed by `step_end_event.synchronize()`) every loop iteration and only yields with `await asyncio.sleep(0)`. Unlike the parent path which used `await asyncio.sleep(0.02)` on idle iterations, this consumes a CUDA-graph forward + a blocking CUDA sync per loop with no backoff, burning GPU/CPU while there is no work. This is exactly the historical 'busy poll loop that only yields with sleep(0)' pattern (idle throughput/CPU-burn regression). The PR's own unit test asserts `idle_calls > 0` within 0.2s, confirming the intended spin. Impact is gated behind the `--inference-disable-ep-consensus` flag (default False), and the comment notes it is only needed to avoid EP>1 deadlock, but the flag doc even recommends its use at ep_world_size==1 where the dummy_forward is pure waste.
findings.suggested_benchmark: Idle-engine CPU/GPU utilization and dummy_forward call rate over a fixed idle window with --inference-disable-ep-consensus enabled vs disabled (EP=1).

findings.claim: static rule 'tensor-item-in-forward-arg' matched antipattern: Calling .item() on a device tensor to pass a Python scalar into a kernel/op on the per-forward hot path forces a device-to-host sync and stalls the GPU pipeline. (matched: 'sync_tensor[0].item())')

static rule 'tensor-to-cpu-forces-d2h-sync' matched antipattern: Moving a GPU tensor to CPU via .to('cpu')/.cpu() on a hot path forces a blocking device→host copy and CUDA synchronization. (matched: '.cpu()')

The diff removes the empirical @triton.autotune over 24 configs on the fused-MoE GEMM (_fused_moe_kernel) and replaces it with a hand-written static heuristic _get_default_config(M,E,top_k). This is exactly the historical failure mode: swapping autotuned/looked-up launch geometry for a fixed heuristic keyed only on a coarse token-count hint. Because BLOCK_SIZE_N/K, GROUP_SIZE_M, num_warps, num_stages are now chosen without measuring the actual (N,K) weight shapes or the device, the picked tile may be suboptimal (or over-large) for shapes that the autotuner previously handled well — mirroring PR #11589 (large tile picked generically) and the get_moe_configs()/JSON-lookup regressions where a generic default heuristic underperformed the per-(E,N,device) tuned config. Notably num_stages is capped at 3 for M>32 while the old configs used up to 5, and num_warps<=4 for M<=128; on some (N,K) these were the autotuner's winners.
findings.suggested_benchmark: Sweep vllm_fused_moe latency across decode (M=1..128) and prefill (M=512..4096) token counts and representative (E,N,K) shapes, comparing the new _get_default_config heuristic against the removed autotuned config set on target GPUs (H100/A100/L4).

findings.claim: The GEMM grid is now sized host-side from num_tokens_hint (grid_size_fc1/fc2 = num_pid_m_hint * num_pid_n) rather than from num_sms or actual num_tokens_post_padded. When the actual padded token count exceeds the hint (prefill spikes, or a decode step with more tokens than hinted), each CTA must stride over multiple tiles via the outer tl.range, reducing parallelism and adding latency on those steps. The hint is a per-step guess; underestimation directly serializes GEMM work.
findings.suggested_benchmark: Measure fused-MoE GEMM latency when actual token count exceeds num_tokens_hint (e.g. hint sized for decode but a prefill/large batch arrives) vs. an oversubscribed num_sms grid.

findings.claim: 此改动触及 buffer-sizing-layout（memory-management），该类问题历史上主要在 [MoE / expert-parallel, GroupedMLP with num_local_experts>=1, MoE with expert parallelism; recommended when num_local_experts > 1; dropless training (moe_expert_capacity_factor is None), MoE (EP/TP), affects both AllGather and AllToAll dispatchers and Grouped/TEGrouped MLP] 配置下显现；核心改动是让 NVLS MoE dispatcher 的对称内存缓冲区根据实际 tensor 尺寸（shape×dtype element_size）动态计算 size_mb，而不是使用固定默认（并把默认从 256MB 提到 512MB）。这直接改变了 symmetric-memory 缓冲区的容量/布局分配策略，属于

vllm_fused_moe selects BLOCK_SIZE_M host-side via _select_block_size_m(effective_tokens), where effective_tokens = num_tokens_hint if provided, else max_tokens (the worst-case, always-max buffer size). Because hidden_states is always allocated at max_tokens for CUDA-graph safety, if num_tokens_hint is not plumbed through (see experts.py dispatch which historically did not pass a hint for TORCH/FLASHINFER paths), decode iterations with few valid tokens will always pick BLOCK_SIZE_M=128 (max_tokens>512 branch). This mirrors the historical fused-MoE tile-heuristic mismatch: a too-large BLOCK_SIZE_M for tiny effective batch sizes causes excessive padding in the indirection table (each of num_local_experts blocks rounded up to 128 rows) and wasted GEMM tiles, hurting decode latency. The docstring itself notes the hint exists precisely to avoid 'using the worst-case buffer size'.
findings.suggested_benchmark: Decode-shaped MoE microbench (small valid_tokens, max_tokens>>valid): compare vllm_fused_moe latency/throughput with num_tokens_hint=None (falls to max_tokens -> BLOCK_SIZE_M=128) vs. hint=batch_size*ep_size, measuring padding waste and kernel time.

It rebinds the router probs to the live router o

This diff is a correctness fix for partial CUDA graph replay + HybridEP: it rebinds '_comm_manager.token_probs' to the live router output so the router.weight backward DDP hook fires, and refactors token-dispatcher attr save/restore into helper methods (_resolve_token_dispatcher_attr / _restore_token_dispatcher_attrs). These changes operate on the per-layer forward path but do not add asymptotic work, extra buffer copies, or block CUDA graph capture; the number of restored attrs (2-4 tensors from cudagraph_attrs) and the per-layer dict iteration are unchanged from parent. The historical cudagraph-enablement/incompatibility pattern is NOT triggered because capture is not being disabled and no unsafe construct is introduced.

此改动触及 redundant-load-startup-work（io-startup），该类问题历史上主要在 [Megatron-FSDP with nccl_ub (NCCL user buffers) + fsdp_double_buffer required, large-scale / high data-parallel (many ranks); many dataset file prefixes (hundreds to thousands), virtual pipeline parallelism (VPP) with pipeline parallelism] 配置下显现；该 diff 用 NullTokenizer 替代需要加载 tokenizer 模型文件的路径，明确目的是在预训练/预分词数据场景下消除每个 rank 对共享文件系统（如 Lustre）的冗余 tokenizer 文件加载，从而减少 startup/初始化阶段的 I/O。这落在启动阶段冗余加载工作（redundant-

The HybridEP SM-count config default is changed from a concrete tuned value to None, altering the effective fast-path parameterization. Parent: `moe_hybridep_num_sms: int = 16` (docstring: '16 SMs can generally achieve good bandwidth'). This diff makes it `Optional[int] = None`. In `_HybridEPManager` (token_dispatcher.py:1073-1075) this `None` flows down through `hybrid_ep_dispatch` into `init_hybrid_ep_buffer`, which now OMITS the `num_sms_dispatch_api`/`num_sms_combine_api` kwargs entirely when None (fused_a2a.py: `if num_sms_dispatch_api is not None: kwargs[...]`). Previously HybridEPDispatch.forward defaulted these to 24, and init always passed them explicitly. So the new default silently hands SM count to DeepEP's internal default rather than Megatron's known-good 16 (or the old 24). If DeepEP's internal default differs from the previously-tuned value, this can change achieved comm bandwidth / kernel occupancy for the HybridEP dispatch/combine on every MoE layer forward+backward — a hot path executed each microbatch. This matches the 'default value itself, not logic, is wrong/changed' boundary of the config-default class (cf. the CompilationConfig Optional default-changed-to-None historical case).
findings.suggested_benchmark: MoE HybridEP dispatch/combine microbenchmark comparing num_sms_dispatch_api/combine_api = None (DeepEP default) vs explicit 16 vs 24; measure all-to-all bandwidth and end-to-end MoE layer step time on NVL topology.

findings.claim: This diff INTRODUCES the permute/unpermute fusion into HybridEP dispatch/combine (fuse_permute_dispatch / fuse_unpermute_combine), gated by `moe_permute_fusion_into_hybridep` which defaults to False. This is a beneficial fusion (merges permute + a2a into one kernel path) but it is OFF by default, so no throughput gain is realized unless the flag is explicitly enabled. Additionally the runtime fallback in HybridEPDispatch.forward uses `inspect.signature(...)` + a warnings.warn when the installed DeepEP lacks `fuse_permute_dispatch`; inspect.signature is relatively expensive but is only hit on the fused path and re-evaluated per forward call — consider caching the capability check once rather than per-call to avoid repeated reflection overhead on the hot dispatch path.
findings.suggested_benchmark: Enable moe_permute_fusion_into_hybridep=True and compare MoE layer step time vs the unfused HybridEP path; also profile the per-call inspect.signature capability check cost on the fused dispatch path.

findings.claim: `_dynamic_step_sample_bookkeeping` and `_get_log_probs_flags` now read sampling metadata via `context.active_request_metadata[label][:active_request_count].tolist()` / `.any()`. Per the metadata redesign in inference_request.py, all metadata now lives on GPU (the previous per-field `on_gpu`/CPU-pinned distinction was removed), and `build_active_slices` copies them with `non_blocking=True`. Calling `.tolist()` (temperature/top_k/top_p) and `.any().item()`-style reductions on these GPU tensors forces a device->host copy of a GPU tensor that was just written non_blocking, inducing a blocking D2H sync on the hot per-step sampling path. Previously these were CPU-pinned tensors specifically to make the D2H copies cheap/async; removing the pinned-CPU staging means `.tolist()` now syncs on raw GPU tensors. This matches the blocking-d2h-sync historical mechanism (host reads of device values forcing a stall).
findings.suggested_benchmark: Per-step decode latency (batch of mixed sampling params) comparing pinned-CPU metadata staging vs GPU active_request_metadata + .tolist(), profiling cudaStreamSynchronize count per step.

findings.claim: static rule 'unconditional-device-copy-may-alias-cpu' matched antipattern: A pre-allocated device tensor is filled via an unconditional `.copy_(..., non_blocking=True)` from a CPU staging tensor without checking whether the target device is CPU, causing a redundant CPU→CPU copy (and duplicate allocation) when running on CPU. (matched: '.copy_(\n                self.request_metadata[label][padded_slice], non_blocking=True\n            )')

Since the kernel skips padding rows, those rows are no longer zero-initi

此改动触及 offload-management（memory-footprint），该类问题历史上主要在 [TP-aware (num_attention_heads_per_partition); dynamic inference engine, tp1pp1 test cases; tensor_model_parallel_size passed to context, only active when cpu_offloading=True (CPU activation/weight offloading path, TE-backed)] 配置下显现；This diff moves the optimizer/grad-buffer restore-from-CPU step from immediately after inference (get_environment_rollouts) to later in the data-iterator setup

This diff relocates the optimizer/grad-buffer onload (restore_grad_buffers() + optimizer.restore_from_cpu()) from immediately after get_environment_rollouts (inference) to later, inside get_grpo_data_iterator after logprobs computation, gated by the new optimizer_is_on_cpu flag which is fed from args.rl_offload_optimizer_during_inference. This is a deliberate scheduling change of a host<->device transfer (offload-management mechanism): the CPU->GPU restore now overlaps with / follows logprobs compute rather than blocking right after inference. It does NOT introduce a regression: the restore is still guarded by the same runtime feature flag (no boolean-guard-misfire), still runs exactly once per collection (no redundant-hot-path-work), and preserves data (no extra FLOPs). The primary risk to sanity-check is timing: because grad buffers are now restored later, GPU memory freed during inference stays freed longer, and logprobs computation now runs while the optimizer state is still on CPU — verify logprobs compute does not require the optimizer/grad buffers to be resident, otherwise it would trigger an unintended re-onload.
findings.suggested_benchmark: RL GRPO step wall-time with --rl-offload-optimizer-during-inference enabled: compare time-to-first-optimizer-step and peak VRAM before/after this commit; confirm restore_from_cpu is called exactly once and not implicitly triggered during logprobs compute.

findings.claim: 此改动触及 cudagraph-enablement（compilation），该类问题历史上主要在 [inference; expert-parallel path has special handling (decode_only_cuda_graphs / EP all-reduce guard against NCCL hang), expert parallel (EP>1) combined with tensor + data parallel; MoE dynamic inference, distributed optimizer with fp8-param-gather; tp=2 in tests] 配置下显现；This change moves the full_iteration_inference CUDA graph scope from HybridStack up to HybridModel so that the embedding and output layers are captured within t

This diff expands the full_iteration_inference CUDA graph scope from HybridStack (decoder) up to HybridModel, so that the embedding and output/LM-head layers are also captured inside the graph. HybridModel now inherits GraphableMegatronModule and owns the cudagraph_manager, with __call__/_should_call_local_cudagraph routing forward through the captured graph during inference decode. This is the intended cudagraph-enablement pattern (more of the iteration is captured, fewer eager kernel launches / less CPU-launch bubble per decode step) rather than a regression. No hot-path recompute or O(N) scan is introduced by the diff itself.
findings.suggested_benchmark: Dynamic/static inference decode latency + kernel-launch count for a hybrid (Mamba) model with cuda_graph_scope=full_iteration_inference, comparing decoder-scoped vs model-scoped capture.

It sw

This diff adds FlashAttention-4 (`flash_attn.cute.flash_attn_varlen_func`) as a new preferred backend for dynamic-batching inference (both prefill and decode). When HAVE_FA4 is True it is chosen ahead of FA3/FA2. This is a backend swap analogous to the historical `fused-backend-swap` (FA3-on-Hopper) case: it is gated behind a try/import so it is opt-in and falls back correctly. No obvious regression in the swap itself, but the new path is now unconditionally preferred whenever the `flash_attn.cute` module is importable — if the FA4 cute kernel is slower for certain shapes than FA3, this preference could regress latency. Worth a benchmark comparing FA4 vs FA3 decode/prefill throughput.
findings.suggested_benchmark: Dynamic-batching inference decode+prefill latency with FA4 present vs FA3 present, across small/large batch and seqlen.

In the MarlinExperts activation path (marlin_moe.py -> apply_moe_activation -> silu_and_mul_with_clamp in activation.py), the SILU + clamp_limit case previously always dispatched to the fused CUDA op `torch.ops._C.silu_and_mul_with_clamp`. After this diff, whenever `topk_ids` is threaded through (which the marlin path now always passes via `_fused_marlin_moe(..., topk_ids=topk_ids)` -> `activation_func(..., topk_ids=topk_ids, expert_map=expert_map)`), `silu_and_mul_with_clamp` routes to the new Triton `_swiglu_limit_pad_aware` kernel *instead of* the optimized fused CUDA kernel, even when there is no expert_map / no EP (single-device). The Triton pad-aware kernel is only clearly beneficial when there are actually many padded/unrouted rows to skip; for the common dense case it replaces a well-optimized CUDA op with a Triton kernel that computes the same math but with a serial row-loop bounded grid (see next finding). This is a gate that pushes a hot path off the fast fused kernel outside the scenario where the new kernel pays off.
findings.suggested_benchmark: Benchmark marlin MoE (e.g. GPTQ/AWQ int4 w4a16 SwiGLU model) activation step latency: SILU+clamp_limit with topk_ids passed (new Triton path) vs the parent silu_and_mul_with_clamp CUDA op, single-device (expert_map=None), across token counts 1..8192.

findings.claim: The new `_swiglu_limit_pad_aware` launches the Triton kernel with `grid = (min(num_tokens, 256), cdiv(hidden_size, 1024))`, and the kernel body loops `for row in tl.range(pid, num_tokens, row_stride)` with `row_stride = tl.num_programs(0) <= 256`. For prefill / large-batch MoE where `num_tokens = M*topk` can be tens of thousands, only 256 CTAs cover the token dimension, so each CTA serially iterates over `num_tokens/256` rows. This caps token-dimension parallelism and can make the activation slower than the fully-parallel fused CUDA op on the hot per-layer path when few rows are actually padded/skipped. The row cap (256) is not scaled with device SM count or occupancy.
findings.suggested_benchmark: Sweep num_tokens (M*topk) from 512 to 65536 for _swiglu_limit_pad_aware with a fully-routed topk_ids (no -1) and expert_map=None; compare against silu_and_mul_with_clamp CUDA op to find the crossover where the 256-row grid cap loses parallelism.

findings.claim: On s390x, _get_cpu_list() now remaps LogicalCPUInfo.numa_node to the book (or socket) ID rather than the real memory NUMA node. This synthetic numa_node then flows into CPUWorker.__init__ memory binding: `if cpu_core.numa_node in allowed_memory_nodes` will almost always be False on multi-book/drawer s390x (book IDs != memory-node IDs), so every rank falls back to `allowed_memory_nodes[0]` and calls init_cpu_memory_env([node0]) for ALL ranks. This binds all workers' memory to a single physical memory node regardless of where their CPUs (books/drawers) actually run, producing remote/cross-node memory access for every rank except the one local to node 0 — the classic NUMA memory-locality regression from the historical cpu-affinity-numa (memory) case where a topology mismatch caused wrong/absent memory binding.
findings.suggested_benchmark: On a multi-drawer/multi-book s390x host, run TP>1 CPU inference and compare per-rank memory-node locality (numastat / local vs remote page access) and decode throughput vs a topology where each rank binds memory to its own drawer.

findings.claim: In OMPProcessManager._get_autobind_cpu_ids, the s390x override `allowed_numa_nodes = sorted(set(cpu.numa_node for cpu in logical_cpu_list))` replaces the memory-node list with book/socket group IDs. Auto-binding then assigns rank->book, which is fine for thread placement, but combined with the cpu_worker.py fallback the memory node chosen for each rank no longer corresponds to the book its threads are pinned to, so CPU threads and their bound memory can end up on different physical nodes (remote memory). Confirm books map 1:1 to memory nodes on target hardware; otherwise thread/memory affinity are decoupled.
findings.suggested_benchmark: Multi-book s390x: verify that each rank's OMP-bound cores and its init_cpu_memory_env node reside on the same physical node; measure bandwidth-bound op latency for co-located vs decoupled placement.

The refactor packs K and V into the content dim, changing the standardized attention KV-cache layout from a 5-D [num_blocks, 2, block_size, num_kv_heads, head_size] to a 4-D [num_blocks, num_kv_heads, block_size, 2*head_size]. For the NIXL/MoRIIO/host-buffer transfer paths this changes physical strides: base_worker.py now allocates the CPU host xfer buffer as (B, N, H, 2*D) and *views* it as logical (B, H, N, 2*D) to fake NHD physical strides (kv_shape = tuple(kv_shape[i] for i in inv_order); permute_shape=True). If the packed logical tensor is non-contiguous under this view, downstream raw NIXL transfers/index_copy_ over such a view can silently fall onto strided (non-coalesced) memory paths. This is the same 'buffer's layout/stride order wrong at allocation' mechanism that caused the FlashInfer HND/NHD stride-order latency regressions in the retrieved cases. Verify the new (0,2,1,3) permute keeps the transferred region contiguous per block; a strided view forces element-wise gather instead of a single contiguous DMA per block.
findings.suggested_benchmark: NIXL disaggregated PD transfer throughput (device->host xfer buffer) with NHD device layout and enable_permute_local_kv=False, measuring bytes/s per block region before vs after the packed-KV refactor.

The new TileLang monitor wraps `JITImpl.__call__` (vllm/utils/jit_monitor.py `_call_with_monitor`), which is invoked on EVERY TileLang kernel call — not just on compilation. Unlike the Triton/CuTeDSL hooks that only fire during actual compile events, this wrapper runs `_tilelang_cache_miss_key(self, args, kwargs)` on every steady-state (cache-hit) invocation. That helper does `dict(kwargs)` copy + pop/update (`_tilelang_call_kwargs`), several getattr chains, and crucially calls `func.parse_args(*args, **call_kwargs)` to recompute the cache key, then re-does the cache lookup. TileLang's original `__call__` already computes the key and looks up the cache, so the monitor DUPLICATES the per-call key-computation on the hot path for every kernel execution during inference. Since the monitor is activated post-warmup (the real serving path), this adds constant per-invocation Python overhead (dict copy + parse_args + lookup) to every TileLang kernel dispatch even when nothing is compiling.
findings.suggested_benchmark: Microbench a TileLang JITImpl.__call__ cache-hit loop (e.g. 100k invocations of a fixed-shape TileLang kernel) with jit_monitor active vs inactive, measuring per-call latency delta from the extra parse_args + dict copy.

findings.claim: In `_tilelang_cache_miss_key`, when `jit_impl.mode == 'auto'` the wrapper calls `impl._infer_jit_mode(*args, **call_kwargs)` and mutates `impl.mode` / `func.set_mode(mode)` on EVERY call before parse_args. This adds mode inference work (and repeated state mutation) into the per-invocation path purely to decide whether to log, further inflating hot-path cost for auto-mode TileLang kernels.
findings.suggested_benchmark: Compare per-call cost of an 'auto'-mode TileLang kernel with monitor active vs inactive to isolate the _infer_jit_mode overhead on cache hits.

In fused_moe_mxfp8_native the previous regime-based block_m selection (_mxfp8_moe_tiles: prefill T>=1024 -> block_m=128/block_n=256, decode -> block_m=64/block_n=64) is replaced by a hardcoded block_m=64 for ALL regimes, and the new _select_cfg always returns BLOCK_N=128 regardless of M. For large-prefill MoE this shrinks the compute tile in both M (128->64) and N (256->128). The redesign relies on the new GROUP_SIZE_M=4 grid-swizzle to recover the A-row L2/MALL locality that the larger BLOCK_M previously provided. If the swizzle's L2-residency assumption doesn't hold on a given ROCm/triton build or larger N (weights evict A between N-tiles), prefill MoE GEMM could regress vs the parent's 128x256 tile. This is a plausible net-positive occupancy redesign but the prefill path is no longer covered by a large-M tile, so it is worth an explicit prefill-shape (large T) A/B benchmark.
findings.suggested_benchmark: Grouped-MXFP8 MoE GEMM latency at MiniMax-M3 prefill shapes (large T, e.g. T=4096, N=2I, K=6144) comparing parent block_m=128/block_n=256 vs new block_m=64/block_n=128 + GROUP_SIZE_M=4 swizzle on gfx950 triton 3.6 and 3.7.

findings.claim: 此改动触及 kernel-fusion（kernel-efficiency），该类问题历史上主要在 [MoE (expert parallelism / token dispatch), inference dynamic batching (non-static), Megatron-FSDP (requires __fsdp_param__), used with gradient_accumulation_fusion=True; TE activation offloading context mentioned] 配置下显现；This diff vectorizes scalar math functions (exp, tanh, erf) in the VSX/PowerPC CPU backend, replacing per-element std::exp/std::tanh/std::erf loops with SIMD po

This diff is a fix (not a regression) for exactly the historical mismatched-collective-config-style mechanism in the DBO/microbatch path. In the parent, `_capture_ubatches` computed the cudagraph key as `num_tokens = ubatch_metadata[0].num_tokens + ubatch_metadata[1].num_tokens` (only the first two ubatches), while the `__call__` lookup path computes `num_tokens = sum(ubatch_slice.num_tokens for ubatch_slice in ubatch_slices)` (all ubatches). When `parallel_config.num_ubatches > 2`, the capture-side key and lookup-side key disagree, so the captured FULL cudagraph is stored under the wrong (too-small) num_tokens key and is never found on the hot path — causing repeated capture/dispatch misses (and the barrier is sized for num_ubatches+1 threads, consistent with >2 ubatches). Changing to `sum(m.num_tokens for m in ubatch_metadata)` makes the capture key match the lookup key. The diff therefore REMOVES a latent perf/correctness bug rather than introducing one; the two barrier changes are comment-only.
findings.suggested_benchmark: Run DBO with num_ubatches>2 and CUDAGraphMode.FULL; assert self.cudagraphs is hit on subsequent steps (no re-capture / no PIECEWISE fallback) and measure per-step latency vs recapture.

findings.claim: 此改动触及 spin-wait-hang（concurrency-sync），该类问题历史上主要在 [pipeline-parallel + tensor/embedding groups (multi-rank inference); hang requires >1 pipeline stage where collectives desync, disaggregated prefill/decode with remote KV transfer, multi-GPU / distributed (message queue used per reader; docstring notes each GPU otherwise pins one thread at 100% CPU)] 配置下显现；The diff switches CUDA events used for synchronizing async output copy streams (and prepare-inputs event) from default spinning events to blocking (sleep) event
findings.suggested_benchmark: determinism-perf — stream/sync ordering; determinism harness exposes stalls

findings.claim: The new unified score buffer is reset with `unified_scores.fill_(_SCORE_SENTINEL)` on every `build()` call over the `[:num_tokens]` view of shape `[num_tokens, num_index_heads, MAX_K_TILES=8192]` float32. This is a full device memset of num_tokens*num_index_heads*8192*4 bytes each forward (e.g. num_tokens=2048, H=1 -> 64MB; larger with more heads), independent of the actual causal sequence length. The score kernels only write the causally-valid tiles (typically far fewer than 8192), so the vast majority of this -inf fill is on tiles that are never read by a token whose context is short. This is exactly the 'wasted zero/fill work on an over-sized buffer / bounded by a compile-time constant instead of actual seq_len' pattern — mirrors the historical Triton 2D-prefill case where num_blocks was cdiv(seq_len) but iterated ALL KV tiles up to full seq length. Consider sizing/filling only the needed tile range (cdiv(max_seq_len, PAGE_SIZE)) instead of the fixed MAX_K_TILES=8192.
findings.suggested_benchmark: Decode/prefill latency of MiniMax-M3 indexer build() at moderate context (e.g. 4k tokens) vs long context, isolating the unified_scores.fill_() memset time; compare against the parent (no unified buffer).

findings.claim: MAX_K_TILES is hardcoded to 8192 (== 1M tokens of context) as a cudagraph capture-time constant for the unified score buffer's innermost dim. The persistent buffer allocated in the builder __init__ is [max_num_batched_tokens, num_index_heads, 8192] fp32 — a large fixed allocation (e.g. 8192*1*8192*4 = 256MB for max_num_batched_tokens=8192, H=1; scales with heads). If actual max model context is far below 1M tokens, this over-allocates GPU memory and forces the per-forward fill_() to touch far more tiles than any token's causal range needs. Sizing MAX_K_TILES from the model's max context (cdiv(max_model_len, PAGE_SIZE), rounded to a cudagraph-safe constant) would cut both the resident allocation and the per-forward memset.
findings.suggested_benchmark: Peak GPU memory of MiniMax-M3 indexer builder init and per-forward memset cost at MAX_K_TILES=8192 vs a context-derived value.

findings.claim: 此改动触及 redundant-buffer-copy（memory-management），该类问题历史上主要在 [TP (low latency TP case mentioned) (issue-measured), MoE/expert-parallel models; sharded branch requires distributed optimizer (main_param_sharded), CUDA graphs enabled (share_cudagraph_io_buffers) with transformer decoder layers; PP/VPP chunks] 配置下显现；The diff eliminates avoidable copies of multimodal tensor payloads on the request-lowering hot path: (1) it replaces `bytes.clone()` in the msgpack RawView seri

The per-tile logprob stashing loop now runs inside the request-tiling `for start_req in range(0, num_decode, group)` loop. Inside it, `just_converged.nonzero(...)[0]` is iterated element-by-element with `local_idx.item()`, `slot.item()`, and `int(valid_canvas_len_np[...])` — each `.item()` is a blocking D2H sync per converged request. Because the outer loop can now execute multiple tiles per call (group < num_decode at high concurrency, which is exactly the target scenario), the same host-scalar loop is re-entered once per tile, multiplying the number of per-element `.item()` syncs versus the parent's single pass. This adds host overhead on the sampler hot path at high concurrency.
findings.suggested_benchmark: Diffusion sampler step with logprobs enabled at high concurrency (num_decode large enough that group<num_decode, so multiple tiles), measure per-step host CPU time / D2H sync count vs parent single-pass.

The bug being addressed is a "permanent hole" in stored blocks that "breaks prefix-reuse

In _warm_zero_kv_blocks_kernel, the warmup launch previously specialized the Triton kernel with num_warps=4, num_stages=3. But the production launch KVBlockZeroer.zero_block_ids (vllm/v1/worker/utils.py:212) launches _zero_kv_blocks_kernel WITHOUT specifying num_warps/num_stages, letting Triton autotune/default them. Triton bakes num_warps/num_stages into the JIT compilation cache key, so the warmed kernel variant (num_warps=4, num_stages=3) does NOT match the production variant (defaults). Removing these two kwargs from the warmup path is the CORRECT fix: it makes the warmup launch's compile signature identical to the production path, so the runtime hot-path zero_block_ids call reuses the pre-warmed kernel instead of triggering a JIT compilation stall on the first real step. This diff CLOSES a warmup-shape/spec gap rather than introducing one.
findings.suggested_benchmark: Measure first-step latency / JIT compile events for Qwen GDN zero-kv path: assert zero_block_ids() incurs no Triton compilation after qwen_triton_warmup().

findings.claim: The control-flow refactor of the zero-kv warmup gating changed semantics intentionally and does not appear to introduce a guard misfire. Previously: if runner-zeroer warmed -> done (skip kernel warmup even when zero_config present). Now: always compute warmed_zeroer, and if zero_config is not None run _warm_zero_kv_blocks_kernel regardless of whether the runner zeroer warmed; only log 'skip' when neither path applies (elif not warmed_zeroer). This is a broadening (both paths may now warm) which increases warmup coverage — a startup-time cost, not a hot-path regression. No fast-path is incorrectly excluded at runtime.

The primary behavioral change is passing `tune_max_num_tokens = max(moe_config.max_num_tokens * moe_config.dp_size, 8192)` to FlashInfer's trtllm MoE kernels. FlashInfer autotunes over token buckets up to `tune_max_num_tokens`; raising this ceiling (now scaled by dp_size, potentially far above the FlashInfer default of 8192) directly increases one-time autotuning cost and tuning workspace/memory. The docstring itself acknowledges 'overestimation may be dangerous, increasing tuning-cost and memory use.' For large DP deployments (e.g. dp_size * max_num_tokens >> 8192) this can materially inflate first-invocation/warmup latency and peak memory. This is intended (correctness of bucket coverage) but is a real perf/memory tradeoff worth benchmarking; the nvfp4 modular path caps it with min(bucket, _get_chunk_size()) but the bf16/fp8/nvfp4-monolithic paths do not.
findings.suggested_benchmark: Measure MoE warmup/autotuning time and peak GPU memory at dp_size=1 vs large dp_size for bf16/fp8/nvfp4 trtllm experts, comparing tune_max_num_tokens=8192 (old default) vs the new dp-scaled ceiling.

findings.claim: The new helper `fi_moe_largest_bucket(self.moe_config)` is invoked directly inside the MoE `apply`/`_invoke_kernel` bodies (trtllm_bf16_moe.py:149, trtllm_fp8_moe.py:255/426/483, trtllm_nvfp4_moe.py:325/488), i.e. once per MoE forward pass on the hot path. The function itself is cheap (`max(moe_config.max_num_tokens * moe_config.dp_size, 8192)`), reading two plain dataclass fields, so the per-call cost is negligible. However `moe_config.max_num_tokens`, `dp_size` and hence the returned bucket are static after init; the value could be computed once at __init__ and cached on the instance instead of being recomputed each forward. This is a minor recompute; note `dp_size` is a property (config.py:1365) rather than a stored field, adding a small attribute-dispatch each call.
findings.suggested_benchmark: Micro-bench a single trtllm MoE forward comparing per-call fi_moe_largest_bucket vs. a cached instance attribute; effect expected to be within noise.

findings.claim: In flashinfer_autotune, the condition that disables the persistent FlashInfer autotune cache was broadened from a narrow set of DeepEP a2a backends to *any* distributed run (`get_world_group().world_size > 1`). When `use_persistent_cache=False`, the code takes the branch where EVERY rank runs `fi_utils.autotune()` inside `_dummy_run` (full FlashInfer benchmarking on all ranks) instead of the persistent-cache branch where only rank 0 (leader) tunes with `tune_mode=True` and broadcasts the resulting tactics to the other ranks. As a result, all TP/PP ranks now redundantly perform the expensive autotune benchmarking at startup, and the on-disk persistent cache is no longer used to short-circuit tuning across restarts. This significantly increases warmup/startup time for any multi-GPU (world_size>1) deployment, not just DeepEP setups. This is a startup-only (cold) path, so it does not affect steady-state request latency, but it regresses initialization time for the common distributed case.
findings.suggested_benchmark: Measure engine startup / warmup wall-clock time (with VLLM_USE_FLASHINFER autotune enabled) on a >=2 GPU tensor-parallel run before vs after this change, and separately with a warm persistent cache across a second process launch.

findings.claim: In FlashInferB12xExperts.apply(), the parent code passed the caller-owned `output` tensor directly to the kernel (`output=output`) so results were written in place with zero extra copies. The new code calls `wrapper_output = wrapper.run(...)` (which allocates its own (M, K) output buffer internally) and then performs `output.copy_(wrapper_output)`. This introduces, on every MoE forward pass (a hot decode/prefill path), (a) an extra full-size (M, hidden_dim) device buffer allocation inside the wrapper and (b) an extra device-to-device memcpy to move it into the modular-kernel-owned `output`. Neither existed before. If B12xMoEWrapper.run exposes an output/out argument, it should be passed the pre-allocated `output` to keep apply() copy- and alloc-free (the class docstring/comments explicitly claim apply() is kept alloc-free).
findings.suggested_benchmark: Microbench FlashInferB12xExperts.apply() forward on SM120 with representative (M=1..512, hidden_dim large) shapes; compare wall-time and peak memory vs the in-place output=output path to quantify the copy+alloc overhead per step.

findings.claim: The newly added `_ensure_wrapper()` lazily constructs B12xMoEWrapper on first apply() and caches it on `self._wrapper` with an early `if self._wrapper is not None: return` guard. This is the CORRECT cached one-time-setup form (not the anti-pattern): the expensive `from flashinfer.fused_moe import B12xMoEWrapper` import and wrapper allocation run only once, and the per-call cost afterward is a single attribute check. No regression here; noted only to confirm the historical one-time-setup-on-hot-path pattern is NOT triggered. One caveat unrelated to perf: because the wrapper is created with use_cuda_graph=True on first apply(), the first call must occur outside/before CUDA graph capture, otherwise construction happens during capture.

findings.claim: 此改动触及 kernel-noop-skip（kernel-efficiency），该类问题历史上主要在 [tensor-parallel (per-partition head counts), affects any TP config, GQA config: applies when num_attention_heads_per_partition == num_query_groups_per_partition (standard MHA, ratio==1)] 配置下显现；This changes the Triton unified attention kernel to pass None instead of dummy pointers/strides when the per-token-head scale path (USE_PER_TOKEN_HEAD_SCALES) a

In the parent code, non-per-token-head modes passed real scalar strides (`ks_blk=ks_slot=ks_head=0`) and real tensor pointers (`k_scale_ptr=k`, `segm_output_ptr=out`), keeping the kernel argument types stable (integers / tensors) across all launches. The diff swaps these to Python `None` for the dead branches (`ks_blk=...=None`, `k_scale_ptr=None`, `segm_output_ptr=None`) and changes the kernel signature annotation from `tl.int64` to `int | None`. This is intentional and matches the stated goal (letting Triton skip materialising unused pointer/stride arguments and registers). The historical `warmup-shape-gap` risk is that mixing `None` vs concrete values for the SAME argument across call sites causes Triton's JIT to key on the None-ness and compile separate specializations. Here the None/non-None choice is deterministic per (use_per_token_head_scales, use_3d) constexpr configuration — the same configurations already fork the compiled kernel via constexpr toggles — so no NEW specialization axis is introduced and no extra runtime JIT stalls are expected. Net effect should be neutral-to-positive (fewer materialised args in the common non-quantized/2D path).
findings.suggested_benchmark: Microbenchmark unified_attention over the common non-quant 2D decode path before/after: measure first-call JIT/compile latency and steady-state kernel time; also count distinct compiled kernel variants (triton cache) to confirm no extra specialization is created by the None arguments.

HummingFP8ScaledMMLinearKernel is inserted at the FRONT of _POSSIBLE_WFP8A16_KERNELS[CUDA] (before MarlinFP8ScaledMMLinearKernel). Because choose_scaled_mm_linear_kernel iterates in priority order and returns the FIRST kernel whose is_supported()+can_implement() pass, and HummingFP8ScaledMMLinearKernel.is_supported() only checks CUDA+SM75 while can_implement() unconditionally returns (True,None), on ALL CUDA GPUs with humming-kernels installed the W-FP8-A16 path now silently switches from the previously-selected Marlin kernel to Humming under `auto` selection. If Humming is not universally faster than Marlin for W8A16 (e.g. on Hopper/Blackwell where Marlin/tuned kernels are well-optimized), this is a throughput regression forced by list placement, not a correctness fallback.
findings.suggested_benchmark: W-FP8-A16 (weight-only fp8) dense linear GEMM microbenchmark on H100/B200 comparing MarlinFP8ScaledMMLinearKernel vs HummingFP8ScaledMMLinearKernel across token counts (1..4096) and hidden sizes

`get_cudagraph_support` now returns `AttentionCGSupport.UNIFORM_BATCH` (instead of `UNIFORM_SINGLE_TOKEN_DECODE`) whenever `num_speculative_tokens > 0`. This is the correct direction to *enable* MTP/spec-decode CUDA graphs, but it silently downgrades the CUDA-graph capability class for the entire attention backend once any spec config is present. UNIFORM_BATCH is a strictly weaker guarantee than UNIFORM_SINGLE_TOKEN_DECODE for pure-decode capture: dispatch may fall back to eager/piecewise for standard single-token decode batches under a spec-enabled deployment, and the newly-added `build()` path unconditionally calls `hpc.assign_attention_decode_task(...)` on every build (a host-side kernel-scheduling call) which was previously absent — this per-step overhead runs on the hot metadata-build path and is not gated by whether spec-decode is active. Verify that non-MTP decode steps still hit full CUDA-graph replay and that assign_attention_decode_task is cheap / cudagraph-safe.
findings.suggested_benchmark: Decode-only throughput/latency with spec_config enabled vs disabled, comparing CUDA-graph replay rate and per-step build() cost (assign_attention_decode_task) for standard single-token decode batches.

findings.claim: This diff is the intended positive counterpart of the historical MTP-disabled pattern (rocm_aiter_fa / FA3 MLA forcing max_qo_len=1 / reorder_batch_threshold=1): it removes the hardcoded `max_seqlens=1` (now `decode_query_len`) and the static `torch.arange` qo_indptr in rope_norm.py, threading `attn_metadata.qo_indptr_decode` and `decode_query_len` through so multi-token decode requests can be batched. No regression here — flagged only to confirm the change matches the known-good enablement mechanism rather than reintroducing the query-length==1 restriction.
findings.suggested_benchmark: MTP/spec-decode acceptance-throughput on HPC_ATTN with num_speculative_tokens in {1,2,3} vs baseline decode.

This PR flips the default runtime path for a huge class of models: the `_should_use_v2_model_runner` helper now returns True for ANY dense (non-MoE) generate model via the new `or not model_config.is_moe` clause, whereas the parent only enabled V2 for an explicit allow-list of architectures (Qwen3/Llama/Mistral/DeepseekV2/Qwen2Moe/GraniteMoe). Combined with removing Qwen3/Llama/Mistral from DEFAULT_V2_MODEL_RUNNER_ARCHITECTURES, the effective default for essentially every dense CausalLM (Gemma2, arbitrary un-tested dense architectures, etc.) is now the V2 model runner unless explicitly overridden by VLLM_USE_V2_MODEL_RUNNER. This is a static default-value change that silently routes models onto a different execution path; any model where V2 is slower or falls back to a suboptimal path will regress with no user action. The blast radius is much larger than the historical allow-list toggle.

The new `_get_image_max_pixels` fallback (`ImageProcessingMixin.get_image_processor_dict(...)`) and `_get_video_max_pixels` (`BaseVideoProcessor.get_video_processor_dict(...)`) call HuggingFace's uncached config-loading helpers directly, rather than the vLLM `cached_*_from_config` / `lru_cache` wrappers used elsewhere in this file. Each invocation re-reads the preprocessor config (potentially hitting the hub/disk and re-parsing JSON). These are invoked from `get_mm_max_tokens_per_item`/`_get_video_max_pixels`, which run during startup metadata/profiling. This is init-time work only (not a per-step hot path), and it replaces a heavier full-processor init, so the net direction of the PR is an improvement. Recommend routing the config reads through an lru_cache to avoid repeated disk/hub JSON reads when these getters are called multiple times during startup.

The diff removes `tune_max_num_tokens=max(self.max_capture_size, 1)` from the `flashinfer_cutlass_fused_moe(...)` call (and the `self.max_capture_size` field). This argument is the token-count bound the FlashInfer CUTLASS autotuner uses to select/tune kernel configs. Dropping it means the kernel falls back to FlashInfer's default `tune_max_num_tokens` (which is small/unrelated to the actual capture size). If that default is smaller than the real max batch of tokens seen at runtime, the autotuner will pick tile/config choices tuned for a small token count and run them on much larger batches — a suboptimal-kernel-config regression that hurts prefill / large-batch MoE throughput. Note this is titled a 'Bugfix' correcting the bound, but the previous value `max(max_capture_size, 1)` was itself only the CUDA-graph capture size, not the true tuning bound; verify the removal (defaulting) does not under-tune large-token MoE runs on the FlashInfer CUTLASS path.
