findings.claim: static rule 'all-gather-then-local-shard-split' matched antipattern: All-gathering a tensor across the TP group and then immediately slicing/splitting it back down to only the local rank's shard, which throws away the just-gathered data and wastes the collective plus allocations. (matched: 'all_gather(')

In the new `_all_gather_batched_symm_mem` (cuda_communicator.py), every call enters `nccl_symm_mem_context(pynccl_comm)` and allocates fresh symmetric output tensors via `torch.empty` inside the context. Entering that context runs `_pool.snapshot()` and a full scan over all pool segments calling `register_comm_window_raw` for any unregistered address on every invocation. The PR's own docstrings (in `_get_symm_scratch`/`_all_gather_symm_mem`) explicitly state this per-call snapshot + window-registration scan costs ~0.5 ms and 'dwarfs the NVLS transfer itself', which is exactly why the single-tensor RS/AG paths were rewritten to use persistent pre-registered scratch. The batched list path was NOT given the same treatment, so it re-pays the snapshot/registration overhead per batched all-gather and freshly allocates symm output tensors each time instead of reusing role-keyed scratch. This is a redundant per-call setup cost on a collective path (all_gatherv with list inputs, e.g. MoE topk_ids gather via all2all.py / transformers/moe.py).
findings.suggested_benchmark: Microbench batched all_gatherv (list of small tensors, dim=0, uniform sizes) under VLLM_USE_NCCL_SYMM_MEM=1: compare per-call latency of _all_gather_batched_symm_mem vs the single-tensor persistent-scratch path; measure nccl_symm_mem_context enter cost (snapshot + register scan) per call.

findings.claim: `_forward_impl` in HpcRopeNorm executes `import hpc` on every forward call. This is the innermost per-layer, per-forward hot path: `HYV3Attention.forward` -> `HpcRopeNorm.forward_cuda` -> `torch.ops.vllm.hpc_rope_norm_forward` -> `hpc_rope_norm_forward` -> `rope_norm._forward_impl(...)`, which begins with `import hpc`. Although Python caches modules in sys.modules so subsequent imports are dict lookups, the import statement still incurs per-call interpreter/import-machinery overhead executed once per attention layer per decode/prefill step (dozens of layers x every token step). This mirrors the historical activation-CustomOp regression where a per-call `from vllm import _custom_ops as ops` inside forward() added avoidable per-call overhead. The `hpc` module should be imported once at module load (or bound as an instance attribute in __init__) rather than inside the hot forward.
findings.suggested_benchmark: Decode-step latency micro-benchmark of HpcRopeNorm._forward_impl comparing module-level `import hpc` vs per-call import, across N attention layers per token step.

findings.claim: In the decode branch of `_forward_impl`, `qo_indptr_decode = torch.arange(num_decode_reqs + 1, dtype=torch.int32, device=qkv.device)` is allocated and recomputed on every forward pass on the hot path. This is a deterministic monotonic ramp [0,1,...,num_decode_reqs] identical across all attention layers within a step and re-derivable from a pre-allocated buffer sliced by size. The vLLM ROCm AITER sparse MLA backend deliberately pre-allocates exactly this `qo_indptr = torch.arange(0, max_num_batched_tokens+1, ...)` once in the builder __init__ and slices `self.qo_indptr[:num_tokens+1]` per step (rocm_aiter_mla_sparse.py) to avoid the per-call device allocation/kernel launch. Here the arange is repeated per-layer (all layers in a step have the same num_decode_reqs), so it could be computed once in the metadata builder and reused.
findings.suggested_benchmark: Measure decode-step overhead of per-layer torch.arange device allocation vs a single pre-allocated qo_indptr buffer shared via HpcAttnMetadata, at high layer counts and small batch.

(matched: 'torch.tensor(\n                [1] * num_decodes + [window_size + q for q in query_lens[num_decodes:]],\n                d')

        [scratch.data_ptr()] * config.n_segs,

此改动触及 kernel-config-tuning（kernel-efficiency），该类问题历史上主要在 [TP8, TP4, MoE (OpenAI triton_kernels matmul_ogs)] 配置下显现；This commit adds a new Helion fused_qk_norm_rope kernel plus per-GPU tuned config JSONs (block_sizes, num_warps, num_stages, indexing, pid_type, etc.) keyed by

The new `_sync_dbo_comm_if_needed()` inserts a hard host-blocking `torch.cuda.current_stream().synchronize()` immediately after DeepEP HT `dispatch` (~line 172) and `combine` (~line 388) whenever `sync_dbo_comm and dbo_enabled()` (i.e. on ROCm DBO). DBO's whole purpose is to overlap these DeepEP dispatch/combine all2all collectives with the other ubatch's compute (via comm_stream/compute_stream + gpu events). A full stream synchronize blocks the CPU until all queued GPU work on the current stream completes, preventing the CPU from queuing the peer ubatch's compute while comm is in flight. This serializes comm against compute on the hot per-MoE-layer path, reintroducing the exact comm bubble DBO was built to hide. It is scoped to ROCm+DBO+deepep_high_throughput as a correctness (workspace-reuse) fix, but the performance cost is a lost overlap / GPU-util bubble.
findings.suggested_benchmark: ROCm DP+EP throughput/latency benchmark with --enable-dbo and --all2all-backend deepep_high_throughput vs. same run without the synchronize; measure MoE-layer GPU utilization / comm-compute overlap (e.g. rocprof timeline) and end-to-end tokens/s.

findings.claim: `_create_sm_control_context` now forces `comm_sms = 0` on the ROCm HT DBO path. With comm_sms==0 both `set_comm_sms` and `set_compute_sms` remain no-ops, so the SM/CU carve-out that lets communication and compute kernels run concurrently on disjoint CUs is disabled. This removes an overlap-tuning lever for DBO on ROCm (documented as an accuracy workaround), reducing potential compute/comm concurrency. Impact is lower than the stream synchronize and is intentional; noting it as a related overlap regression on the same gated path.
findings.suggested_benchmark: Compare DBO MoE throughput on ROCm with comm_sms=0 vs a nonzero VLLM_DBO_COMM_SMS carve-out to quantify concurrency loss.

findings.claim: The weightless RMSNorm path on XPU now allocates a fresh `torch.ones(x.shape[-1], device=x.device, dtype=x.dtype)` tensor on every call (both rms_norm and fused_add_rms_norm) instead of taking the native weightless code path that skips the per-channel multiply entirely. This runs on a hot path — models like gemma3n/gemma4/llama4/hrm_text/falcon-mamba construct `RMSNorm(..., has_weight=False)` which passes `weight=None`, and RMSNorm is invoked per layer per forward/decode step. Each call now (a) launches a device fill kernel to materialize the ones vector, and (b) forces the _C kernel to do an extra pointwise multiply by 1.0 that was previously elided. This is wasted allocation + wasted compute on a per-step norm, i.e. a redundant buffer creation/multiply that the prior code avoided.
findings.suggested_benchmark: benchmarks/kernels/ir/bench_ir_ops.py --ops rms_norm on XPU with a weightless (has_weight=False) RMSNorm layer, comparing native weightless path vs the torch.ones fallback

findings.claim: The decode attn_logits scratch is moved from a per-call torch.empty into the shared WorkspaceManager. The builder reservation (_reserve_attn_logits_workspace) sizes it with q_num_heads = self.num_heads * self.dcp_world_size and B = max_num_seqs, and max_splits = _compute_num_kv_splits(max_model_len, num_compute_units()). The runtime forward_mqa uses B = q.shape[0], q_num_heads = q.shape[1], num_kv_splits = _compute_num_kv_splits(attn_metadata.max_seq_len, self._sm_count). If any runtime dimension exceeds the reserved one (e.g. q_num_heads at runtime > num_heads*dcp_world_size, or a decode batch where B > max_num_seqs due to spec-decode/MTP query expansion, or max_seq_len == max_model_len giving equal splits but larger B*heads), get_simultaneous must grow the buffer — but the workspace is locked after warmup, so the grow path raises AssertionError on the decode hot path. This is the exact buffer-sizing-layout risk: buffer size/scope wrong at reservation time relative to actual runtime worst case.
findings.suggested_benchmark: Decode with spec-decode/MTP (multiple query tokens per seq) at max_num_seqs and max_model_len context to confirm reserved (B, q_num_heads, max_splits, lse_dim) covers runtime shape and the locked workspace never has to grow.

findings.claim: 此改动触及 cudagraph-enablement（compilation），该类问题历史上主要在 [inference; expert-parallel path has special handling (decode_only_cuda_graphs / EP all-reduce guard against NCCL hang), expert parallel (EP>1) combined with tensor + data parallel; MoE dynamic inference, distributed optimizer with fp8-param-gather; tp=2 in tests] 配置下显现；This diff makes Gemma3ForConditionalGeneration implement SupportsEncoderCudaGraph and adds the full set of encoder CUDA graph hooks (config, budget range, item

This diff FIXES (not introduces) a scheme-selection performance regression matching the fused-backend-swap pattern. Before this change, `_is_wNa8O8_int` returned true when `is_static_int8_in OR is_static_int8_out`. That meant a standard W8A8 int-quantized checkpoint (static/dynamic input activations, NO output activations) incorrectly matched WNA8O8Int, dispatching to the fake-quant WNA8O8Int path instead of the true int8 GEMM path (CompressedTensorsW8A8Int8). The change to `is_static_int8_in AND is_static_int8_out` restores the correct dispatch so W8A8 int8 models use the optimized int8 gemm kernel; WNA8O8Int now only matches when BOTH input and output activations are present. This is the same class of issue as historically dispatching to the wrong/less-optimal quantized matmul backend.
findings.suggested_benchmark: Load a W8A8 channel/tensor int-quantized compressed-tensors checkpoint (no output_activations) and assert the selected scheme is CompressedTensorsW8A8Int8; benchmark end-to-end throughput vs the WNA8O8Int fake-quant path.

findings.claim: This is a revert (#46958) of the '[KV Offload] Use background thread for mmap / cpu_tensors pinning' optimization (#45850). It reintroduces SYNCHRONOUS, blocking host-memory pinning during CPUOffloadingWorker.__init__: (1) CPU tensors are now allocated with pin_memory=pin_memory (pinned at allocation even on CUDA/ROCm) instead of the parent's `pin_memory=PIN_MEMORY and not current_platform.is_cuda_alike()`, and (2) pin_mmap_region(mmap_region) is called inline before building the handlers instead of on a background CPUTensorPinThread. The parent's own comment explicitly noted 'Pinning here would block worker initialization'. Reverting therefore restores a one-time worker-startup latency cost proportional to total KV offload buffer size (cudaHostRegister / allocation-time pin over GBs of host memory). This is intentional: it is a [BugFix] revert, because pinning asynchronously in the background could race with the first async H2D/D2H transfers that assume pinned memory (cf. prior commit 'Ensure memory is pinned prior to async h2d copy'), yielding either unpinned/slow DMA or incorrect behavior. The slow (blocking) path is required for correctness, not a removable suboptimal gate.
findings.suggested_benchmark: Measure CPUOffloadingWorker.__init__ wall-clock (worker startup time) with large num_cpu_blocks / mmap_region on CUDA before vs after this commit; confirm the delta is a one-time init cost and that per-transfer throughput (transfer_async/get_finished) is unchanged (memory stays pinned).

kernel_warmup.py flips the FlashInfer autotune persistent-cache default from disabled (_FLASHINFER_USE_PERSISTENT_CACHE = False) to enabled (use_persistent_cache = True for all non-DeepEP backends). The removed TODO/comment explicitly documented WHY it was disabled: the persistent file cache had key collisions (e.g. use_8x4_sf_layout=True/False) that caused INVALID/suboptimal FlashInfer tactics to be selected at runtime. This was originally turned off by commit #43119 ('Temporarily disable using persistent cache for flashinfer autotune') to fix exactly such a regression. Re-enabling the persistent-cache path is entirely predicated on the coupled FlashInfer 0.6.13 bump actually fixing that cache-collision bug upstream. If 0.6.13 does not resolve the use_8x4_sf_layout collision, every non-DeepEP deployment will now load bad autotune tactics from the persistent cache and dispatch slower attention/MoE kernels — a throughput/latency regression on the hot inference path (Hopper/Blackwell). This is a static-default toggle change that routes runtime kernel selection down a path known-bad in the prior version.
findings.suggested_benchmark: On SM90/SM100, run a FP4/FP8 MoE + attention throughput benchmark (e.g. mixed prefill+decode) with flashinfer 0.6.13 comparing the persistent-cache-enabled autotune path vs the old all-ranks-tune path; verify chosen tactics for both use_8x4_sf_layout=True and False do not regress tokens/s vs parent commit.

The diff adds `Mxfp4MoeBackend.EMULATION` to the auto-selection priority list in `_get_priority_backends_for_gpt_oss()` (mxfp4.py:322). In the `runner_backend == 'auto'` path of `select_mxfp4_moe_backend`, backends are iterated in priority order and the first whose `is_supported_config` returns True is chosen. EMULATION is `OCP_MXQuantizationEmulationTritonExperts`, whose `_supports_quant_scheme` unconditionally returns True and which self-documents as 'may be slower than native quantized MoE' because it dequantizes both w1 and w2 weights on the fly on EVERY forward pass (see `apply` -> `_dequantize_weights`, a hot inference path). Previously emulation was opt-in only (`--moe-backend emulation`), so auto-selection could never land on it. Now, on hardware where the native fast backends (Marlin/AITER/TRTLLM/XPU) report unsupported for a given config, auto-selection can silently fall through to the emulation backend, incurring per-forward dequantization overhead instead of erroring/using a native path. This matches the historical 'fast kernel exists but gating logic now admits/prefers a slow path' pattern.
findings.suggested_benchmark: MXFP4 gpt-oss MoE decode throughput on MI300/MI250 (non-gfx950) comparing auto backend selection before vs after this change, to confirm emulation is not silently chosen over a native path

The parent code keyed `model_states` by calling `model_config.compute_hash()` directly (JSON serialization + SHA-256 over the whole ModelConfig). The new `EplbState.prepare_forward()` is invoked on EVERY forward pass (added to `vllm/v1/worker/gpu/model_runner.py`, `vllm/v1/worker/gpu_model_runner.py`, and every speculator/proposer path). If it had used `model_config.compute_hash()` directly for the per-forward dict lookup, this would place expensive JSON+SHA-256 serialization on the hot path. The PR correctly mitigates this by introducing `compute_hash_cached()` (memoizes by `id(config)`) and using it in `prepare_forward`. This dependency is the crux: the finding is that if the cached helper had NOT been used, this would be a classic redundant-hot-path-work regression. As implemented, the cache neutralizes it — verify the cache is actually consulted on all hot lookup paths and that no path still calls compute_hash() per forward.
findings.suggested_benchmark: Enable EPLB and measure per-forward CPU overhead of prepare_forward with vs without compute_hash_cached; assert compute_hash() is not called per forward step.

`with_gpu_sync_check` decorates the two hottest per-step worker entry points (`execute_model` and `sample_tokens` in vllm/v1/worker/gpu_worker.py). When `VLLM_GPU_SYNC_CHECK` is unset (the default), the decorator returns `fn` unchanged (`mode is None: return fn`), so there is zero default-path overhead — this is correctly gated and NOT a regression. When the env IS set, each call adds `torch.cuda.get_sync_debug_mode()` + two `torch.cuda.set_sync_debug_mode()` calls per step; but this is an explicit opt-in debug knob (config-toggle-perf-feature category), so the slowdown is intended and bounded to debug runs. No unconditional hot-path cost is introduced.
findings.suggested_benchmark: Steady-state decode throughput/latency with VLLM_GPU_SYNC_CHECK unset vs set, to confirm default path (return fn) is a true no-op and the debug path overhead is acceptable.

In rocm_aiter_fused_experts (the per-forward MoE expert compute path invoked from AiterExperts.apply on every decode/prefill step), the diff hoists `from aiter.ops.flydsl.moe_common import GateMode` out of the previous `try/except ImportError` block, so it now executes UNCONDITIONALLY on every call for all quant/activation configs — not just the `use_mxfp4_w4a16` case as before. Previously the import (and its cost) only ran inside the mxfp4-w4a16 branch. Although CPython caches loaded modules via sys.modules (so the marginal cost is a dict lookup + attribute bind rather than a full module load), it is now paid on every MoE forward for paths (SILU/GELU, fp8, etc.) that don't need GateMode at all. Recommend module-level import (top of file) so it is bound once, or keep it inside the branch that actually uses gate_mode.
findings.suggested_benchmark: Microbenchmark AiterExperts.apply / rocm_aiter_fused_experts throughput on ROCm across SILU and mxfp4 configs, comparing per-forward latency with the import at module scope vs inside the call.

The new fused kernel reuses try_get_optimal_moe_config with config_name(hidden_states.dtype) but there is no tuned config JSON for this brand-new kernel shape/dtype combination, so it will fall back to the generic get_default_config. As with the historical H20-3e / L4 cases, a mismatched default BLOCK_SIZE_M/N/K can (a) underperform vs a tuned config and (b) risk exceeding shared-memory on smaller GPUs (e.g. L4) because the dequant path materializes an extra [BLOCK_SIZE_N, BLOCK_SIZE_K] bf16 tile plus fp32 scale tiles inside the K-loop on top of the standard A/B tiles. The code comment itself acknowledges 'no support for tuning ... to be added'.
findings.suggested_benchmark: Run the new fused NVFP4 emulation kernel on an L4/small-SM GPU across batch-size buckets to verify default Triton config does not exceed shared memory and is not slower than the old dequant+TritonExperts path

findings.claim: This PR performs the intended fusion of NVFP4 weight dequantization into the Triton GEMM kernel (fused_moe_nvfp4_emulation_kernel), replacing the previous approach of fully materializing bf16-dequantized w1_dequant/w2_dequant tensors before calling TritonExperts. This is a legitimate optimization (removes large intermediate weight allocations and extra memory traffic) rather than a regression. The main residual concern is that per-block scale pointers (b_scale_ptrs) are recomputed from scratch inside every K iteration instead of being advanced incrementally like a_ptrs/b_ptrs, adding redundant integer address arithmetic in the hot K-loop; minor vs the GEMM cost but worth noting.
findings.suggested_benchmark: benchmark_moe.py on an NVFP4 MoE model (e.g. Qwen3-30B-A3B-NVFP4) comparing pre/post PR forward latency and peak memory

findings.claim: PyNvVideoCodecVideoBackendMixin caps concurrent GPU decoders at PYNVVIDEOCODEC_MAX_RETAINED_DECODERS = 1 (single ClassVar decoder slot guarded by _decoder_slot_cond). All concurrent video-decode requests in the renderer's ThreadPoolExecutor (renderer_num_workers threads) serialize on _borrow_decoder_slot() via _decoder_slot_cond.wait(). The GPU-decode fast path becomes single-flight regardless of how many API/renderer workers are running, creating a hard concurrency bottleneck and idle-GPU bubbles under multi-request video load.
findings.suggested_benchmark: Concurrent multi-request video decode throughput with backend='pynvvideocodec' vs 'opencv'/'pyav' under N>1 renderer_num_workers

findings.claim: With only one retained decoder slot shared across all requests, the intended decoder-reuse optimization (reconfigure_decoder instead of reconstructing SimpleDecoder) is defeated under concurrency and even sequentially when consecutive requests target different files. get_decoder() sees slot.source_path != file_path on nearly every borrow and calls reconfigure_decoder (or falls back to full _construct: CUVID parser + decoder + surface-pool allocation, the documented dominant per-request cost). Because decode_frames_pynvvideocodec borrows the slot TWICE per request (once in _read_source_metadata, once in _decode_to_pinned_host) with the same temp_path, interleaved requests thrash source_path back and forth, forcing repeated reconfigure/rebuild.
findings.suggested_benchmark: Per-request PyNvVideoCodec decode latency: alternating distinct video files vs repeated same file, measuring reconfigure_decoder / SimpleDecoder construction frequency

findings.claim: CodecQ3::recv (csrc/quickreduce/quick_reduce_impl.cuh) reconstructs values via a per-value scalar loop: an 8-iteration loop building int16_t qv[8] from bit shifts/masks, then packed_from_int16_pair<T>() which for bf16 goes through __float2bfloat16(static_cast<float>(int)) per lane. This is markedly more scalar/ALU-heavy than CodecQ4's decode, which unpacks 8 nibbles with 4 vectorized packed_add ops using kMask000F/kHalf2_1024 tricks and no per-element int->float conversion. This is exactly the extra pack/unpack overhead the authors themselves cite as the reason INT3 is TP2-only. Since recv runs on the hot all-reduce path (per atom, per block), on TP4/TP8 the decode cost can exceed the communication savings. The commit already gates INT3 to world_size==2 both in Python (quick_all_reduce.py) and via TWOSHOT_DISPATCH_TP2_ONLY / TP2-only instantiation, so the regression is contained; this is a note that the codec decode path is inherently slower than the fp16-bit-trick codecs.
findings.suggested_benchmark: tests/distributed/test_quick_all_reduce.py INT3 vs INT4 latency at TP2 across message sizes (1MB..64MB)

findings.claim: 此改动触及 prefix-state-caching（inference-serving），该类问题历史上主要在 [DCP/PCP asserted world_size==1 for hybrid/sliding-window paths, tensor-parallel (TP>1) with MLA; also PP-aware per comment, multi-engine / data-parallel or cross-restart (inconsistent lora_int_id across engines)] 配置下显现；This diff adds ROCm/AMD Triton kernels for MiniMax M3 sparse (block-sparse) attention: a lightning-indexer block-scoring + bitonic top-k kernel that selects top

The new RVV W4A8 GEMM inner loop (_dequant_gemm_accum_rvv) reloads the loop-invariant weight zero-points every K iteration. `gemm_accum_uint4_rvv` calls `__riscv_vle8_v_i8(qzeros_b + group*8, 8)` on each (m,k,group) inside the hot `for k in K` reduction loop, then subtracts. In the reference AVX512 small-M path (_dequant_gemm_accum_small_M) the zero-points are loaded ONCE into vzps[col] outside the K loop. Because qzeros_b depends only on `group` (not on m or k), this is a K×M×4 redundant vector load + subtract inside the hottest GEMM reduction loop, inflating memory traffic and instruction count.
findings.suggested_benchmark: Microbenchmark int4_scaled_mm_cpu on RISC-V with RVV enabled (M small, large K) comparing per-k qzeros reload vs hoisting qzeros/vsub out of the K loop.

findings.claim: load_uint4_as_int8_rvv issues a strided load (`__riscv_vlse8_v_u8` stride=vnni_size=4) plus vand/vsrl/vsub unpack work for every single K lane and every group. The VNNI4 weight layout packs 4 adjacent K lanes contiguously precisely so the AVX512 path can load one wide contiguous 256-bit chunk covering 4 K lanes at once (load_uint4_as_int8) and reuse it. The RVV path instead processes one K lane at a time with a strided/gather-style access and re-unpacks per k, forgoing the 4-way K packing reuse. This roughly 4x's the unpack/load instruction count relative to the layout's intent and gives a weak, non-contiguous memory access pattern in the hot loop.
findings.suggested_benchmark: Compare RVV int4 GEMM throughput unpacking one K-lane-at-a-time (strided vlse8) vs a variant that loads/unpacks all 4 VNNI4 K lanes contiguously per iteration.

The new swap_ab fast path in fused_moe_kernel is gated by enable_swap_ab() which requires exactly device capability 90 (Hopper) AND BLOCK_SIZE_M<64 AND BLOCK_SIZE_N>=64. Even within fp8_w8a8, this is intentionally narrow: it excludes SM100/SM120 (Blackwell) and any tuned config where BLOCK_SIZE_M happens to be 64+. This mirrors the historical suboptimal-kernel-gate pattern (fast kernel exists but gating logic rejects most invocations). It is a conservative gate rather than a regression, but for skinny-M decode shapes on non-Hopper GPUs the faster swapped layout will never be selected. Not a regression against parent (parent had no fast path); flagging as a coverage/gating observation.
findings.suggested_benchmark: Benchmark fp8_w8a8 fused_moe on H100 with small M (decode, M=1..32) vs the same on B200/L4 to confirm swap_ab only engages on SM90 and quantify the left-on-the-table speedup elsewhere.

此改动触及 correctness-forces-slow-path（kernel-efficiency），该类问题历史上主要在 [any (NCCL all-reduce path); TE attention path affected, tensor parallel (and pipeline parallel forward_step), tensor-parallel (async-TP, symmetric memory fused matmul+reduce-scatter)] 配置下显现；This diff extends the device-capability gate so that NVIDIA Thor (SM 11.x) architectures are recognized as supporting the CUTLASS FP8 grouped-GEMM MoE kernels —

The PR moves cudaHostRegister (host memory pinning) from synchronous worker init into a background thread (self.pin_thread), but the transfer hot path (submit_store/submit_load -> transfer_async) never waits for pin_thread to complete. Only shutdown() joins it. Consequently, any store/load transfers issued in the window between worker init and pin-thread completion run DMA against UNPINNED host memory, which the code's own warning acknowledges is 'slower (unpinned DMA)'. On CUDA/ROCm this is a real (bounded, transient) H2D/D2H bandwidth regression on the first offload/load operations after startup, whereas previously the memory was guaranteed pinned before any transfer could run. This is a latency/throughput trade to reduce startup latency; if a benchmark measures first-request offload/load latency it may show regression.
findings.suggested_benchmark: Measure H2D/D2H bandwidth of the first N offload/load transfers issued immediately after CPUOffloadingWorker init on CUDA, versus after pin_thread completes; compare to parent (always-pinned) behavior.

findings.claim: 此改动触及 suboptimal-kernel-gate（kernel-efficiency），该类问题历史上主要在 [ZeRO-Offload / CPU-Adam, Megatron-FSDP with gradient_accumulation_fusion enabled, TP1/PP1 in test config] 配置下显现；This diff widens the activation gate for the FlashInfer MXINT4 fused MoE backend so that standard gated SiLU (MoEActivation.SILU) now also routes into the fused

In FlashAttnMLASparseImpl.forward_mqa (hot per-decode-step path), `cu_seqlens_q = torch.arange(0, num_actual_toks + 1, dtype=torch.int32, device=q_rope.device)` allocates and populates a fresh device tensor on the GPU on every attention-layer call. Since decode always has query length 1 (max_seqlen_q=1), this cu_seqlens is a fixed arange [0..num_tokens] whose size only depends on batch shape; it can be precomputed once in the metadata builder (like req_id_per_token_buffer / query_start_loc already are) and sliced, avoiding a per-layer, per-step kernel launch + allocation across all decoder layers. The sibling FlashInfer/FlashMLA sparse backends do not construct such a tensor per call — they reuse query_start_loc / seq_lens from metadata.
findings.suggested_benchmark: Decode-only throughput on a Hopper sparse-MLA model (e.g. DeepSeek with index_topk) at moderate batch size; compare tokens/s with cu_seqlens_q precomputed in metadata vs allocated per forward_mqa call, scaled by number of MLA layers.

findings.claim: This diff is a legitimate optimization matching the redundant-hot-path-work pattern in the inverse (fixing it). Previously the lookup path rebuilt per-rank key metadata and re-serialized the full pool-key prefix for every (group, hash) candidate: `metadata_templates = [dataclasses.replace(db.metadata, tp_rank=tp, pp_rank=pp) for ...]` was executed per lookup call (per token_len), and each candidate key did a full `PoolKey(md, h_hex).to_string()` f-string interpolation of ~7 fields. The change hoists the stable prefix computation into `_init_lookup_key_prefixes()` (one-time init), so the hot loop only concatenates the precomputed prefix with the hash via `build_key_string`. This removes per-iteration dataclasses.replace + repeated prefix f-string formatting from the hot lookup path. No regression introduced.
findings.suggested_benchmark: Microbenchmark lookup() candidate-key construction over many (group,hash) candidates with TP*PP>1, comparing parent vs this commit

此改动触及 scheduler-work-distribution（parallelism-scheduling），该类问题历史上主要在 [data-parallel group >1 (FullyParallelSaveStrategyWrapper); affects distributed checkpoint save, data-parallel (with context-parallel) group; benefits scale with DP size >1, data-parallel / distributed checkpointing (parallelization_group across DP ranks, group_size>1)] 配置下显现；The diff changes the DP prefill throttling condition in the scheduler: instead of deferring only fresh prefills (num_computed_tokens == 0), it now defers any re

The DP prefill-throttling gate in Scheduler.schedule() was broadened from `request.num_computed_tokens == 0` to `num_computed_tokens < request.num_tokens - 1`. This changes which requests are treated as 'has local prefill compute' and therefore deferred on throttled (non-cadence-aligned) steps. The new condition now correctly defers partially-transferred remote-KV resumes (connector matched only part of the prompt, leaving local prefill), while still admitting fully-transferred resumes (single-token decode step). This is the intended fix and generally improves throttling accuracy, but it is a change in batch-composition assumptions: any request whose remaining tokens exceed one (e.g. chunked prefill mid-flight, or a resume with residual local prefill) will now `break` out of the scheduling loop on a throttled step where previously (with num_computed_tokens>0) it would have been scheduled. On DP setups this can add extra latency/TTFT for such requests by pushing their local prefill to the next cadence-aligned step. Confirm this is desired and that the cadence interval is short enough not to starve these resumed prefills.
findings.suggested_benchmark: DP disaggregated-prefill (P/D) decode-side throughput/TTFT with connector partial-match: measure added latency for remote-KV resumes that carry residual local prefill under throttle_prefills cadence.

In `ClientInner::send_to_engine` (hot path — invoked for every `EngineCoreRequestType::Add` request submission at client.rs:488, plus every abort/utility send), the diff replaces the inline `transport::send_message(...).await` with `self.handle.spawn(async move { ... }).await?`. Every outbound engine message now incurs an extra Tokio task allocation, a cross-runtime scheduling handoff onto the dedicated ZMQ runtime, and a `JoinHandle` await. This adds per-request scheduling latency and allocation overhead on the request-submission critical path. Because `send_message` internally clones `input_send` and grabs a socket, spawning-then-immediately-awaiting adds a task round-trip without concurrency benefit at the call site. Suggest confirming the spawn is actually required for cross-runtime correctness (the added `// TODO: is it still necessary` comment in multimodal.rs shows the authors themselves are unsure whether nested-runtime offloads are still needed) and, if so, measure the per-request latency delta versus inline send.
findings.suggested_benchmark: Micro-bench request submission throughput/latency (add_request → send_to_engine) at high concurrency comparing inline await vs handle.spawn().await; watch p99 submit latency and task-alloc rate.

findings.claim: This PR intentionally *loosens* the `check_moe_marlin_supports_layer` gate (adding `allow_tile_padding`), which is the inverse of the historical suboptimal-kernel-gate regression: previously the gate over-rejected the fast Marlin MoE kernel for tile-misaligned intermediate sizes and forced a fallback to slow WNA16 kernels. That is fixed here by zero-padding the intermediate size at weight-prep so the fast kernel is admitted. This is a net improvement, NOT a regression. The one caveat: the new gate uses `group_size <= 0 or intermediate_size_per_partition % group_size == 0`, and callers pass group_size that may still be None in channelwise WNA16 (the exact prior root cause). Verify callers pass -1 (not None) for channelwise so `group_size <= 0` short-circuits correctly, otherwise `% None` raises. compressed_tensors path constructs `group_size` explicitly; auto_awq/auto_gptq pass `self.group_size`.
findings.suggested_benchmark: MoE throughput for a WNA16/FP8 model whose intermediate_size_per_partition is not a multiple of 64 (e.g. TP-sharded odd intermediate), comparing Marlin-with-padding vs the WNA16 fallback.

findings.claim: All new `_pad_*` helpers (int_wna16.py, marlin_utils_fp8.py) and the scale/zero-point padding run inside weight-prep functions (_process_weights_marlin, _process_awq_weights_marlin, prepare_fp8_moe_layer_for_marlin, prepare_mxfp8_moe_layer_for_marlin), i.e. one-time load-time setup, not the forward hot path. The `torch.nn.functional.pad(...).contiguous()` copies are therefore not a recurring hot-path regression. No redundant-hot-path-work found.

The new FlashInferCuteDslNvFp4LinearKernel is prepended to the head of the CUDA NVFP4 auto-selection priority list (_POSSIBLE_NVFP4_KERNELS[PlatformEnum.CUDA]), placing it BEFORE the previously-default FlashInferCutlassNvFp4LinearKernel. Its is_supported() only requires is_device_capability_family(100) + has_flashinfer(), so on all SM100 devices auto mode now silently switches the default NVFP4 linear GEMM backend from cutlass to cute-dsl. Unlike a purely additive backend behind an opt-in flag, this changes the default hot path for every NVFP4 linear layer on SM100. If the cute-dsl kernel is not uniformly faster (or lacks autotuning parity) across all shapes/batch sizes served in production, this is a throughput/latency regression on the default path — the same failure mode as prior fused-backend-swap defaults that regressed some shapes. Recommend keeping cute-dsl opt-in (like b12x) or benchmarking it against cutlass across the full shape grid before making it the auto default.
findings.suggested_benchmark: NVFP4 linear GEMM latency/throughput on SM100 (B200) across representative M (batch*seq) and N/K shapes, comparing auto (now cute-dsl) vs flashinfer_cutlass, both with autotune on/off.

findings.claim: FlashInferCuteDslNvFp4LinearKernel does NOT override input_quant_key() and does not use the as_quantized_activation() pre-quantized-activation path, whereas the FlashInferCutlassNvFp4LinearKernel it displaces at the head of auto-selection DOES declare input_quant_key()=kNvfp4Dynamic and consumes a fused/pre-quantized activation when one is available (skipping its own scaled_fp4_quant). Because this cutedsl kernel is now the SM100 auto default, layers that previously received a QuantizedActivation from an activation-fusion producer (expose_input_quant_key) will no longer advertise a consumable key, so the fused input-quant is forced back into a separate inline scaled_fp4_quant call inside apply_weights on every forward. This re-introduces a redundant per-forward activation-quantization step on the hot path that the cutlass default had folded away — the same mechanism as the historical CUTLASS nvfp4 quantize-into-GEMM fusion case.
findings.suggested_benchmark: End-to-end NVFP4 model decode latency on SM100 with activation-quant fusion enabled, comparing cutlass (fused input-quant) vs cute-dsl (inline scaled_fp4_quant) defaults.

Potential stale-result / correctness-adjacent perf issue in the async lookup future lifecycle: in blocking mode (`non_block=False`, the default), `lookup()` submits to the single-worker executor and calls `future.result()`, which BLOCKS the scheduler thread just as before but now with an extra thread hop and serialization through a 1-worker pool. If multiple requests call blocking lookup in the same step, they serialize on the single executor worker AND block the scheduler thread on each `.result()`. Compared to the parent (direct synchronous `self.client.lookup` on the scheduler thread), the default (lookup_async=False) path now incurs executor submit + context-switch overhead per lookup with no overlap benefit. Verify the default path did not regress scheduler-step latency.
findings.suggested_benchmark: Micro-benchmark get_num_new_matched_tokens throughput with lookup_async=False (default) before/after: direct call vs executor.submit().result() to quantify the added thread-hop overhead on the hot scheduler path.

findings.claim: This PR reverts commit 2a47a9ff0 ("[DSV4 Perf] Optimize dsv4 cudagraph by reducing eager_break_during_capture, 26.8%~27.9% E2E TTFT improvement"). The optimization being reverted split the breakable-cudagraph capture path so that, during BreakableCUDAGraphCapture.is_active(), only the compressor overlapped with wq_b+kv_insert while the indexer ran without forcing a graph break at the attention boundary — reducing the number of eager breaks that fragment the captured cudagraph and thus cutting per-forward launch overhead. The revert re-decorates attention_impl with @eager_break_during_capture and forces the always-3-way execute_in_parallel form, reintroducing the extra eager break during capture. If the revert's justification (a correctness/hang bug) is not itself the reason, this restores the ~27% TTFT regression that the reverted commit had removed. Confirm the revert is motivated by correctness (e.g., the eager-break decorator being required for the breakable-cudagraph replay contract / in-place buffer semantics) rather than an accidental perf loss.
findings.suggested_benchmark: DSV4 (compress_ratio==4 / C4A indexer layers) E2E TTFT with VLLM_USE_BREAKABLE_CUDAGRAPH=1, before vs after this revert; also measure cudagraph num_eager_breaks per forward

findings.claim: The reverted branch relied on BreakableCUDAGraphCapture.is_active() as a runtime predicate to select a reduced-break overlap path (compressor overlap + inline indexer) vs the full 3-way execute_in_parallel. The revert removes this guard entirely and unconditionally re-adds @eager_break_during_capture on attention_impl plus the 3-way execute_in_parallel. This is the inverse of the historical boolean-guard-misfire pattern: rather than a predicate wrongly excluding the fast path, the revert removes the predicate that had been enabling the fewer-break fast path. Verify the removal does not disable the intended overlap on the non-capture (replay/eager) path — the 3-way execute_in_parallel with enable=aux_streams is not None still preserves overlap on CUDA, so functional overlap is retained; the loss is specifically the extra capture-time graph break.
findings.suggested_benchmark: Microbench attention_impl launch/overlap on C4A layer under breakable cudagraph capture

findings.claim: Revert removes DeepGEMM PDL enablement (`_apply_pdl(_dg, True)` gated by `is_arch_support_pdl()` in `_lazy_init`). On SM90+ (Hopper/Blackwell) DeepGEMM GEMMs run in the hot FP8 inference path; PDL lets a dependent kernel start its prologue before the predecessor fully drains, reducing inter-kernel launch latency. Disabling it re-introduces kernel-launch bubbles for every DeepGEMM invocation. This is a launch-knob (config) change, not an algorithm change; matches the historical kernel-config-tuning pattern (same kernel, tuned launch params).
findings.suggested_benchmark: DeepGEMM fp8_gemm_nt / m_grouped_fp8_gemm_nt throughput & end-to-end decode latency on SM90/SM100 with and without PDL

findings.claim: Revert strips `griddepcontrol.wait` / `griddepcontrol.launch_dependents` PMD instructions from `per_token_group_quant_8bit_packed_register_kernel` and reverts the launch from `cudaLaunchKernelEx` with `programmaticStreamSerializationAllowed=1` back to a plain `<<<grid,block,0,stream>>>` launch. This removes programmatic stream serialization / dependent-launch overlap for the per-token-group FP8 quant kernel that feeds DeepGEMM, adding launch latency between the quant and the consuming GEMM on SM90+. Same algorithm, launch-config only.
findings.suggested_benchmark: Microbenchmark per_token_group_quant + DeepGEMM chain latency on Hopper with/without programmatic launch

The new flashinfer_sparse_index_cache memoizes build_flashinfer_mixed_sparse_indices results keyed only by layer-type string ('swa_only'/'c128a'/'c4a'). But the SWA-only and C4A sparse-index build reads per-layer inputs from self.topk_indices_buffer, which is a SINGLE model-shared buffer (created once at nvidia/model.py:917, passed to every layer and to each layer's SparseAttnIndexer, which OVERWRITES it in-place per forward at sparse_attn_indexer.py:369). Consequently, in a step with multiple same-type layers whose indexer writes distinct topk indices into the shared buffer, the first layer's cache entry is reused by all subsequent same-type layers -- returning STALE sparse_indices that no longer reflect the current layer's topk selection. The diff mitigates this by leaving C4A uncached (cache_key=='c4a' never stored), but 'swa_only' IS cached and its prefill path also slices self.topk_indices_buffer[num_decode_tokens:num_tokens] (width 0, so effectively input-independent) -- so swa_only caching is likely safe. The real risk is if the assumption 'same-type layers produce identical mixed indices' does not hold across ~60 layers when indexer output differs per layer; verify that swa_only prefill_topk_indices width is truly always 0 and c128a's c128a_prefill_topk_indices/c128a_global_decode_topk_indices are computed once at metadata-build time (shared) rather than per-layer.
findings.suggested_benchmark: Run DSv4 FlashInfer sparse with >1 same-type layer and compare per-layer sparse_indices with and without the cache to confirm bit-identical outputs (correctness gate for the perf optimization).

findings.claim: flashinfer_sparse_index_cache lives on DeepseekSparseSWAMetadata, which is documented as a fresh instance per build() (per step). So the cache is correctly step-scoped and does NOT persist stale results across decode steps -- this is the safe design point. No cross-step staleness risk found. The optimization legitimately eliminates ~60x redundant build_flashinfer_mixed_sparse_indices calls per step for same-type layers, matching the claimed 2-4% TTFT gain.

findings.claim: In vllm/kernels/xpu_ops.py, the new weightless (weight is None) branch of fused_add_rms_norm redirects to the native PyTorch implementation and then performs two extra full-tensor device copies (x.copy_(output); x_residual.copy_(residual)) to emulate in-place semantics. Compared to the parent, which allocated a torch.ones weight and called the single fused _C kernel, this native path plus two extra elementwise copies is slower per call on XPU. This is a correctness-required fallback (weightless _C ops are CUDA-only, per the comment), and it only fires on the uncommon has_weight=False path, so impact is limited, but the double copy_ adds avoidable device traffic; it could be reduced by having native write in place. Not a hot-path regression for typical (has_weight=True) modules.
findings.suggested_benchmark: Benchmark RMSNorm(has_weight=False) forward on XPU (fused_add path) before/after: measure latency of native fallback + 2x copy_ vs prior fused kernel.

此改动触及 kernel-config-tuning（kernel-efficiency），该类问题历史上主要在 [TP8, TP4, MoE (OpenAI triton_kernels matmul_ogs)] 配置下显现；This diff adds VLLM_TRITON_FORCE_FIRST_CONFIG, which monkey-patches Triton's Autotuner.run to skip benchmarking and always pick the first valid config instead o

vllm/triton_utils/force_first_config.py monkey-patches triton.runtime.autotuner.Autotuner.run to skip benchmarking and pick the first *valid* config rather than the autotuned best config. This is mechanistically the inverse of the historical kernel-config-tuning cases (which selected better block sizes/num_warps for throughput/latency): when enabled it can pick a suboptimal launch config (e.g. an oversized/undersized BLOCK, wrong num_warps) and degrade kernel throughput/latency across all Triton kernels. HOWEVER, install() is only invoked when VLLM_TRITON_FORCE_FIRST_CONFIG is explicitly set to 1/true (env_override.py gate; envs.py default is '0'), so the default production path and its autotuning are completely unchanged. This is a documented determinism/benchmarking aid, not a change to the normal hot path — hence no regression is introduced for default users. Flagged only so reviewers are aware that enabling the flag trades peak kernel performance for autotune-variability elimination, and that the 'first valid config' is order-dependent on the kernel's config list (no perf ranking).
findings.suggested_benchmark: With VLLM_TRITON_FORCE_FIRST_CONFIG=1, benchmark a fused MoE / w8a8_block_fp8_matmul / flash-attn Triton kernel and compare kernel latency/throughput vs the default autotuned build to quantify the worst-case slowdown of first-valid-config selection.

This diff removes `AsyncMicrobatchTokenizer` and replaces the async encode/decode path with plain per-request `make_async(self._encode)` / `make_async(self._decode)` wrappers. In the parent, `get_async_tokenizer()` returned an `AsyncMicrobatchTokenizer` that coalesced concurrent encode/decode requests (up to max_batch_size=32 within batch_wait_timeout_s=0.002s) into a single batched tokenizer call (`self.tokenizer(prompts, **kwargs)` / `tokenizer.batch_decode(token_ids_list)`). The new code in `_tokenize_prompt_async`/`_detokenize_prompt_async` calls `tokenizer.encode(single_prompt)` / `tokenizer.decode(single_token_ids)` one item at a time. Since `tokenize_prompts_async` fans out `asyncio.gather(*(self.tokenize_prompt_async(prompt, params) for prompt in prompts))` and the front-end serves many concurrent requests, every request now pays the full per-call tokenizer/Python overhead individually with no batching. Additionally the default `renderer_num_workers=1` means these calls serialize through a single-worker thread pool, so removing micro-batching directly reduces tokenization throughput under concurrent load. This is precisely the mechanism the AsyncMicrobatchTokenizer was introduced to fix (per-request individual tokenization).
findings.suggested_benchmark: Concurrent OpenAI /tokenize and /v1/completions throughput benchmark with many simultaneous requests (e.g. serving benchmark at high concurrency with a fast model / short generation), measuring frontend tokenization QPS and per-request TTFT contribution, comparing batched vs per-request tokenization.

findings.claim: The rewritten fastsafetensors_weights_iterator now passes the ENTIRE hf_weights_files list to a single ParallelLoader and streams via a producer thread + queue, replacing the parent's per-batch loop (weight_files_sub_lists of size pg.size()) that called copy_files_to_device()/fb.close()/loader.close() after each batch. In the parent, GPU shard buffers were reclaimed after every pg.size()-sized batch (batch-scoped peak). Even with queue_size=0, the ParallelLoader's producer prepares/holds the next shard's device buffer while the consumer still holds the current shard, so peak load-time VRAM is ~(1 + queue_size) shard buffers plus whatever the producer keeps resident across the full file list. If ParallelLoader retains file handles / partial metadata / GPU buffers for the whole file set (rather than freeing per-shard like the old fb.close()), peak device memory during loading can be higher than the parent for multi-shard models, shrinking the loadable-model envelope on memory-tight GPUs. This is the classic deferred-free-peak pattern (buffer is freed, but later than before), matching the historical 'del state' shard-loop case.
findings.suggested_benchmark: Measure peak GPU memory (torch.cuda.max_memory_allocated) during fastsafetensors load of a multi-shard model with VLLM_FASTSAFETENSORS_QUEUE_SIZE=0 vs the parent commit, and with queue_size=1, to confirm the default preserves the non-pipelined footprint and quantify the extra shard-sized resident buffers.

In prepare_inputs (hot path, per forward step), the new block computes num_active_loras by building `req_ids = list(scheduler_output.num_scheduled_tokens.keys())` and then calling `get_num_active_loras_for_dispatch` -> `lora_state.get_activate_loras(req_ids)`, which iterates over all req_ids and constructs a set of LoRARequest objects on every step. This materializes a full list of req_ids and a fresh set each iteration purely to obtain a count (len). It only runs when self.lora_config is set, but for LoRA-enabled workloads it adds per-step O(num_reqs) allocation/iteration to the dispatch path. Consider tracking the active-LoRA count incrementally in LoraState (on add/remove) instead of recomputing per step.

In CPUAttentionMetadataBuilder.build(), the new encoder-only path allocates and zero-fills a fresh KV-cache buffer on every build call: `encoder_cache_tensor = torch.zeros((total_block_num, num_kv_heads, block_size, 2*head_dim), dtype=self.dtype)`. build() runs once per forward step, so this re-allocates and memsets a full [total_blocks, kv_heads, block_size, 2*head_dim] buffer each step (size scales with total token/block count). Previously the ENCODER/ENCODER_ONLY path used _run_sdpa_forward directly on the passed-in key/value tensors with NO extra cache allocation. This is a per-step allocation+zeroing on the hot metadata-build path — the buffer's scope is wrong (allocated per-step rather than reused/persisted), matching the buffer-sizing-layout mechanism.

Dynamic SD forces cudagraph_mode to PIECEWISE when the config uses full cudagraphs (VllmConfig._maybe_override_dynamic_sd_cudagraph_mode). This is a deliberate, documented tradeoff ('not compatible with full cuda graph so we force piece-wise cuda graph'). Note the perf implication: enabling Dynamic SD downgrades an otherwise full-cudagraph deployment to piecewise, which increases per-step CPU/launch overhead versus a full graph. This is gated strictly on uses_dynamic_speculative_decoding(), so it does NOT affect deployments that don't opt into num_speculative_tokens_per_batch_size. Historical warmup-shape-gap (decode query length = 1+K exceeding captured sizes) is not newly introduced here since K only shrinks from the static num_spec_tokens (min(vllm_num_speculative_tokens, K)), so no batch shape exceeds already-captured shapes.

The diff removes the `@support_torch_compile` class decorator from `DeepSeekV4MTP` (top of the class def). The intent appears to be to replace torch.compile-fused elementwise ops (enorm/hnorm masking, shared-head RMSNorm) with hand-written fused kernels imported from `deepseek_v4/common/ops` (`fused_mtp_input_rmsnorm`, `mtp_shared_head_rmsnorm`). If those custom fused ops do not cover the whole forward graph that torch.compile previously covered, dropping `@support_torch_compile` disables Inductor/vLLM graph compilation for the *entire* MTP model forward (attention block, e_proj/h_proj GEMMs, MoE, hc_head). That reverts a compiled graph back to eager per-op dispatch on the hot spec-decode step path, adding per-op Python/dispatch overhead and losing operator fusion/cudagraph capture that the decorator previously provided. This is a kernel-fusion-scoped regression risk: a couple of small ops are hand-fused but the whole-model graph fusion/compile is lost.
findings.suggested_benchmark: Spec-decode (MTP draft) latency/throughput for DeepSeek-V4 on XPU: measure per-step draft forward latency before vs after removing @support_torch_compile, at representative batch sizes.

findings.claim: For tile-misaligned (padded) layers, every forward now runs marlin_pad_dim on the activation (torch.nn.functional.pad along K) before the GEMM and marlin_unpad_output (output[..., :size_n].contiguous()) after it. Both allocate a new tensor and perform an extra memory-movement copy on the hot path for each padded layer, on top of the GEMM. The added .contiguous() slice-copy in marlin_unpad_output is explicitly flagged as avoidable in the code's own TODO (kernel could write un-padded columns directly). For models with several padded layers this is per-token, per-layer, per-forward overhead.
findings.suggested_benchmark: End-to-end decode throughput on a model whose shapes require Marlin tile padding vs the same shapes that are naturally aligned, isolating the pad/unpad copy cost.

findings.claim: apply_gptq_marlin_linear / apply_awq_marlin_linear / apply_fp4_marlin_linear / apply_fp8_marlin_linear / apply_mxfp8_marlin_linear all now call marlin_repacked_nk(weight, ...) on EVERY forward. This recovers (size_n, size_k) purely from static packed-weight shapes (qweight.size(0)*16, qweight.size(1)*pack_factor//16) — a constant per layer, yet it is recomputed every iteration on the hot path. Since it derives from tensor shapes and can produce a Python int comparison against size_k/size_n, it also feeds the subsequent `if padded_n == size_n` branch. This is init-shaped work placed on the hot path rather than cached at prepare-time (e.g. stored on the layer).
findings.suggested_benchmark: Micro-bench a padded W4A16/FP8 Marlin linear forward (many small M) comparing per-forward marlin_repacked_nk+pad vs a cached (padded_n,padded_k) path.

findings.claim: 此改动触及 kernel-config-tuning（kernel-efficiency），该类问题历史上主要在 [TP8, TP4, MoE (OpenAI triton_kernels matmul_ogs)] 配置下显现；该 commit 引入了新的 Helion kernel（per_token_group_fp8_quant）并附带针对 nvidia_b200/h100 的自动调优配置 JSON（block_sizes、num_warps、num_stages、indexing、l2_groupings 等）。这些配置直接决定了 k

release_device_memory_under_pressure(target_device) is invoked inside the per-module loop of process_weights_after_loading (one call per module, i.e. potentially hundreds/thousands of iterations for large models). On integrated (UMA) GPUs, whenever the release condition is met it calls torch.accelerator.synchronize(device) followed by torch.accelerator.empty_cache(). empty_cache() returns cached blocks to the driver via cudaFree, forcing a device-wide synchronize and dropping the caching allocator's reusable pool. Doing this repeatedly during weight loading means the very next module's process_weights_after_loading must re-cudaMalloc the memory it just returned, so the allocator ping-pongs (free -> re-malloc -> free) across modules. This can substantially slow weight loading on UMA devices (GH200/DGX Spark/Jetson) and add per-module synchronize stalls. Consider debouncing (release at most once per N modules or once at the end) or tracking cumulative releasable bytes rather than releasing greedily each iteration.
findings.suggested_benchmark: Load a large quantized model (e.g. compressed-tensors/GPTQ with many linear layers) on a UMA/integrated GPU under simulated memory pressure and measure process_weights_after_loading wall time with vs. without the per-module release call.

findings.claim: release_device_memory_under_pressure runs its guard checks unconditionally for every module during process_weights_after_loading. On non-integrated (discrete) GPUs the first guard (current_platform.is_integrated_gpu(device.index)) short-circuits cheaply, but it is still evaluated once per module, and on integrated GPUs each call additionally issues torch.accelerator.memory_reserved()/memory_allocated() queries plus a psutil.virtual_memory() syscall per module. psutil.virtual_memory() reads /proc/meminfo each call; performing it per-module across a full model is unnecessary syscall overhead. Prefer computing the integrated-GPU check once and gating the whole loop, and/or throttling the psutil read.
findings.suggested_benchmark: Micro-benchmark process_weights_after_loading module-loop overhead: count calls to release_device_memory_under_pressure and total psutil.virtual_memory()/memory_reserved() time for a many-layer model.

findings.claim: 此改动触及 prefix-state-caching（inference-serving），该类问题历史上主要在 [DCP/PCP asserted world_size==1 for hybrid/sliding-window paths, tensor-parallel (TP>1) with MLA; also PP-aware per comment, multi-engine / data-parallel or cross-restart (inconsistent lora_int_id across engines)] 配置下显现；This introduces a Marconi-style admission policy for the hybrid KV cache, adding shared-prefix detection (num_uncached_common_prefix_tokens) in HybridKVCacheCoo

The Marconi-style admission policy caps num_new_tokens to num_uncached_common_prefix_tokens (aligned down to block_size) in _mamba_block_aligned_split for hybrid/mamba models. When a shared uncached prefix >= block_size is detected, the scheduler deliberately schedules fewer tokens for this step so the common prefix lands on a cacheable block boundary and can be reused by later requests. This is a prefix-reuse admission heuristic (matches the prefix-state-caching family). Correctness/throughput is the intent, but note it artificially reduces the per-step prefill chunk (num_new_tokens = num_uncached_common_prefix_tokens // block_size * block_size). For a single request with no future prefix hits, this splits one big prefill into two chunks, adding an extra scheduler/forward iteration and slightly lowering prefill throughput. The heuristic is gated only on num_uncached_common_prefix_tokens >= block_size (any detected shared uncached prefix), not on whether future reuse is likely.
findings.suggested_benchmark: Hybrid (mamba+full attn) model with mamba_cache_mode=align: measure prefill TTFT/throughput for a workload with partially-shared prompts vs unique prompts, comparing with/without the admission cap.

findings.claim: 此改动触及 kernel-occupancy-redesign（kernel-efficiency），该类问题历史上主要在 [MoE (expert-parallel) with Multi-LoRA (multiple adapters, max_loras > 1), expert-parallel (expert_map path benefits most; benchmark uses ep_size 1 and 8), TP8+TP8, TP8+EP8, DP+EP (issue-measured)] 配置下显现；The diff replaces a one-block-per-group shared-memory tree reduction (with per-level __syncthreads and scalar loads) with a one-warp-per-group design using warp

In the fused hot path `_project_qkv_gate`, the effective GemmaRMSNorm weights are recomputed on every forward call via `self.q_norm.weight.float() + 1.0` and `self.k_norm.weight.float() + 1.0`. Each expression launches a dtype-cast kernel plus an add kernel and allocates a fresh (head_dim,) GPU tensor — 4 extra tiny elementwise kernels/allocations per full-attention layer per decode step. These are invariant weights that could be precomputed once at __init__ (e.g. a cached buffer) and passed in. This partially undoes the launch-reduction goal of the fusion and adds per-step overhead on the attention critical path.
findings.suggested_benchmark: Decode-step latency for Qwen3.5 text-only on CUDA with attn_output_gate: compare per-layer attention time with cached (weight+1) buffer vs recomputing self.q_norm.weight.float()+1.0 each forward, at small batch (bs=1) where kernel-launch overhead dominates.

此改动触及 encoder-execution-mode（inference-serving），该类问题历史上主要在 [supports PP (pipelining forward step paths retained), disaggregated encoder (EPD) instances, ViT with TP (num_heads_per_partition)] 配置下显现；The core change modifies how the Gemma3 multimodal encoder's max token count is computed for profiling — it now profiles the vision encoder against the pre-pool

This commit reverts the optimized warp-shuffle + vectorized-I/O implementation of silu_and_mul_per_block_quant_kernel back to the slower one-block-per-group design, reintroducing a known performance anti-pattern. Concretely, the parent (optimized) version used one warp per (token, group) with each lane owning EPT=group_size/32 contiguous elements via wide vector loads/stores (InVec/OutVec reinterpret_casts), a sync-free __shfl_xor_sync abs-max reduction, and no shared memory. The reverted code goes back to one thread per element with scalar loads (`token_input_gate[tid]`, `token_output[tid]`), a __shared__ float shared_max[group_size] log2(group_size) tree reduction with a __syncthreads() per level, plus an extra __syncthreads()+shared-memory broadcast of the scale. This is exactly the narrow per-element load/store + shared-memory tree-reduction pattern the historical kernel-occupancy-redesign cases (RMS-norm scalar->vectorized, INT8 quant scalar->vectorize_with_alignment) were rewritten to eliminate — only here the change runs in the regressing direction. The op is used in the FFN/MoE activation-quant hot path (triton_moe.py, act_quant_fusion.py), so this reintroduces a memory-latency-bound kernel (~2x off peak per the removed comment).

Launch config also reverts from a 128-thread block packing kWarpsPerBlock=4 groups (grid.y = ceil(num_groups/4)) with __launch_bounds__ back to dim3 block(group_size) and grid(num_tokens, num_groups). For group_size=64 this means 64-thread blocks, which halves warps-per-block versus the packed 128-thread design and can lower occupancy; combined with the reintroduced __syncthreads barriers this compounds the throughput loss on the activation-quant path.

此改动触及 memory-leak-unpruned-state（memory-management），该类问题历史上主要在 [affects per-rank KV/dynamic inference context; tensors sized by max_total_requests and max_kv_block_count, CUDA graphs / per-device (PP+VPP first/last layer gating), torch FSDP2 (TorchFullyShardedDataParallel)] 配置下显现；The change fixes LRUCache.touch: previously a KeyError path inserted a stale order key (key with no corresponding value) into the OrderedDict, which desyncs the

The change to LRUCache.touch() is a correctness/hang fix that is neutral-to-positive on performance. Old code used try/move_to_end/except-KeyError and, on a missing key, inserted a bare order key (self._LRUCache__order[key] = None) with no corresponding __data entry. Such stale order keys break popitem()/remove_oldest(): popitem iterates self.order to find an LRU key then calls self.pop(lru_key), but pop() checks `if key not in self` (i.e. __data) and returns default for the orphan key without removing it from __order, so eviction fails to make progress -> the reported MM-cache hang. The new `if key in self: move_to_end` avoids creating orphan keys and, on the hot path, replaces a possible exception-path dict insert with a single membership check, so it is strictly cheaper or equal. No regression introduced.
findings.suggested_benchmark: Microbenchmark LRUCache.touch() hit/miss loop plus a fill+evict cycle to confirm no throughput regression and that eviction terminates.

The new `_inflight_prefill_reserved_blocks()` is called once per candidate async-KV-load request inside the waiting-scheduling loop, and it iterates over the entire `self._inflight_prefills` set, invoking `_request_remaining_blocks()` -> `coordinator.get_num_blocks_to_allocate(...)` for every in-flight prefill on each call. If the loop admits K async loads in a single schedule() step while N prefills are already in flight, this becomes O(K*N) coordinator computations per step (a super-linear pattern as both in-flight prefills and pending async loads grow), even though nothing about the in-flight set changes within a single scheduling pass. The reservation sum could be computed once per schedule() step (and incrementally updated as loads are admitted) instead of re-summed from scratch on every candidate.
findings.suggested_benchmark: Disaggregated-prefill / async-KV-connector schedule() microbenchmark with many concurrent WAITING requests requesting async loads while a large number of prefills are in flight; measure per-step scheduler latency vs. inflight count.

findings.claim: `_request_remaining_blocks()` calls `coordinator.get_num_blocks_to_allocate()` with `apply_admission_cap=True` and full-ISL token counts for each in-flight prefill on the scheduler hot path. This per-request coordinator query is only meaningfully guarded by `if load_kv_async` in the waiting loop, so it is skipped when no async KV connector load is present; however when async loads are common (P/D disaggregation), the recomputation of each in-flight prefill's remaining full-sequence block requirement every candidate is redundant given the values are stable within a step. Consider caching per-request remaining-block counts and maintaining a running reserved-blocks total.
findings.suggested_benchmark: Scheduler step latency under P/D disaggregation with high async-load admission rate; compare re-summing reservation each candidate vs. incremental accounting.

In DeepEPV2PrepareAndFinalize._receiver, the prefill (do_expand=True) path builds recv_topk_idx via a Python for-loop over recv_expert_num_tokens (a host list): `for i, count in enumerate(recv_expert_num_tokens): recv_topk_idx[offset:offset+count].fill_(i + rank_expert_offset)`. This launches a per-expert GPU slice-fill kernel from the host inside the hot MoE prepare path, one dispatch per expert (up to num_experts, e.g. 256), each going through torch tensor `__setitem__`/`fill_` dispatch. In addition `total_tokens = sum(recv_expert_num_tokens)` iterates the host list. This is the classic host-scalar-loop-work pattern: work that is inherently per-expert-contiguous could be vectorized (e.g. torch.repeat_interleave of expert IDs on GPU) instead of a Python loop launching many tiny kernels. Because recv_expert_num_tokens comes from the do_cpu_sync path, the counts are host-known, so a single repeat_interleave/arange-based construction is possible.
findings.suggested_benchmark: Prefill MoE with deepep_v2 backend, num_experts=256, measure prepare() latency vs token count; compare Python per-expert fill loop against vectorized repeat_interleave construction of recv_topk_idx.

findings.claim: The diff adds freeze_gc_heap() + maybe_attach_gc_debug_callback() in the Worker's compile/warmup finalization path (after activate_triton_jit_monitor(), around line 727 of gpu_worker.py). This is a one-time init-shaped call (gc.collect(0/1/2) then gc.freeze()) run once per worker after model init/warmup, NOT on the per-iteration inference hot path. It matches the beneficial historical pattern (relocating gc.freeze() into the worker path so periodic oldest-gen GC collections stop repeatedly scanning static model weights/KV caches/CUDA graphs). This is a GC-pause REDUCTION, not a regression. No per-request cost is introduced.

`_rocm_aiter_hipb_mm_fp8_impl` runs on every FP8 linear-layer GEMM (via `hipb_mm_fp8` -> `apply_scaled_mm` -> layer forward), and unconditionally executes per-call setup-shaped work: `import aiter` (module lookup) and `_ensure_hipb_mm_extension_initialized()`. The latter calls `torch.accelerator.current_device_index()` and a set membership check on EVERY GEMM invocation, even after the extension has already been created for the device. `hipb_create_extension()` itself is properly guarded by `_HIPB_MM_INITIALIZED_DEVICES`, but the guard bookkeeping (device query + set lookup + the `from aiter import hipb_mm` import each call) is repeated on the hot path. This matches the redundant-hot-path-work pattern (init-shaped work placed on the per-forward path). It should be hoisted into process_weights_after_loading / one-time init, so the steady-state GEMM path only calls hipb_mm.
findings.suggested_benchmark: Microbench a decode step over many FP8 linear layers with VLLM_ROCM_USE_AITER_LINEAR_HIPBMM=1 and measure per-layer host overhead of _ensure_hipb_mm_extension_initialized vs. a one-time-init variant.

In the new `sample()` gating (sampler.py), FlashInfer is disabled for the ENTIRE batch if `any_greedy(idx_mapping_np)` or `any_explicit_seed(idx_mapping_np)` is True. Because these are batch-level `np.any(...)` reductions, a single greedy request (temperature==0) or a single request with an explicit seed forces every co-batched top-k/top-p request down the slow `apply_top_k_top_p` full-vocab sort path (`logits.sort(dim=-1)`), instead of the fused FlashInfer rejection-sampling kernel. In mixed serving batches (very common: some clients set seed, or one greedy/temp=0 request), the fast path is almost never taken, negating the intended win. This is the same fused-backend-swap latency mechanism the PR aims to add, but the coarse batch-level guard misfires and routes the whole batch to the sort path.
findings.suggested_benchmark: Sampling-only microbench on a mixed batch (e.g. 256 reqs, most with top_p<1.0, one greedy or one explicit-seed request) comparing per-step sampler latency with/without the fast path engaged; compare vs all-random batch.

It reworks how expert weights are pa

In the new NIXL zero-copy path, add_recv() now issues a *separate* NIXL transfer per expert: for every recv'd expert it builds fresh local/remote descriptor lists and calls _create_peer_xfer(), which internally does get_xfer_descs + prep_xfer_dlist (x2) + make_prepped_xfer, then transfer(). The parent implementation batched all experts from one peer into a single _create_peer_xfer/transfer call (one batched READ per peer). Moving descriptor construction and handle preparation from a per-peer batch (O(world_size) xfers) to a per-expert granularity (O(num_recv_experts) xfers) multiplies NIXL handle-prep overhead on the EPLB rebalance path. Each xfer also allocates/releases dlist + xfer handles. On layers with many migrating experts this is materially more NIXL API calls and small transfers than before. Consider re-batching descriptors per peer inside execute() rather than issuing one READ per expert in add_recv().
findings.suggested_benchmark: Microbenchmark EPLB rebalance transfer_layer wall-time with NIXL backend for a layer where many experts migrate (e.g. full reshuffle across 8 ranks), comparing per-expert vs per-peer batched READ issuance.

findings.suggested_benchmark: gpt-perf — CPU-bound launch overhead; small-batch step time

findings.claim: In SingleTypeKVCacheManager.cache_blocks (called for every request on every scheduling step via KVCacheCoordinator.cache_blocks), the parent code had a fast path that set `block_mask = None` without calling reachable_block_mask when `alignment_tokens is None or alignment_tokens <= self.block_size`. The diff removes this guard and ALWAYS calls `self.reachable_block_mask(...)` unconditionally, passing `alignment_tokens=self.scheduler_block_size`. For full-attention/MLA managers, the base `reachable_block_mask` still returns None immediately, so the extra cost is only a method dispatch; but for the SlidingWindowManager the override now allocates a `mask = [False] * (end_block - start_block)` list and iterates every block once (or twice, when the replay-boundary loop also runs) on the hot caching path for EVERY request — even when retention_interval is None (the default). Previously SWA sparse-mask computation only ran when alignment_tokens > block_size. This adds per-request O(num_full_blocks) Python-level list allocation + iteration on the hot path that was previously skipped in the common (dense) case.
findings.suggested_benchmark: Microbenchmark cache_blocks throughput for a sliding-window model with VLLM_PREFIX_CACHE_RETENTION_INTERVAL unset (default), comparing parent vs this commit for long-prompt requests (e.g. 8k+ tokens) to measure per-step scheduler overhead from the always-on reachable_block_mask list build.

findings.claim: SlidingWindowManager.reachable_block_mask builds a dense Python list `mask = [False] * (end_block - start_block)` and does explicit per-block Python for-loops for both the segment-boundary tails and the replay-boundary tail. When retention_interval is None (default dense behavior), the intended semantics is essentially 'cache all reachable tails', yet the code still materializes and iterates the full per-block mask rather than short-circuiting to `return None` where every block is reachable (it only returns None when `need >= per_segment`). For large prompts this is O(num_blocks) Python work per cache_blocks call on the scheduler hot path.
findings.suggested_benchmark: Profile allocate_slots/cache_blocks for SWA groups with long contexts to quantify the per-block Python loop cost vs the parent's boundary-only formula.

process_weights_after_loading calls super().process_weights_after_loading(layer) then does scale.data.t().contiguous() and replace_parameter on the weight_scale. This is a one-time load-time transpose+contiguous copy (not on the hot forward path), consistent with existing XPUFP8ScaledMMLinearKernel behavior. No per-call regression; only extra startup work proportional to scale size, which is acceptable.

In the new Triton fast path swap_blocks_batch(), the descriptor tensors are moved to GPU with src_addrs.to('cuda', non_blocking=True) (likewise dst_addrs/sizes). non_blocking=True only produces a truly async H2D copy when the *source* tensor resides in pinned (page-locked) memory. The descriptor buffers created by _new_descriptor_buffers() use pin_memory=is_pin_memory_available(); on any platform/config where pin memory is NOT available (pin=False), these .to('cuda') calls become synchronous host->device copies that block the calling thread on every transfer. Because transfer_async is meant to be a fire-and-forget async submission on the transfer stream, a synchronous H2D here serializes the CPU submission path against the copy and defeats the pipelining the C++ DMA path (cuMemcpyBatchAsync) would otherwise provide. Even worse, np/torch fallback path builds all_src via src.numpy() on these same buffers, so the fast path silently degrades to sync copies when pinning is unavailable.
findings.suggested_benchmark: Microbench swap_blocks_batch (Triton path) on a build with is_pin_memory_available()=False vs True; measure transfer_async wall time / CPU submission latency across many small CPU->GPU batches.

In DeepseekV4MegaMoEExperts._run_mega_moe (the per-forward MegaMoE execution path), a new EPLB block calls eplb_map_to_physical_and_record on topk_ids every forward when logical_to_physical_map is set. That triton kernel internally does topk_ids.contiguous().to(int32) plus .contiguous() on logical_replica_count / logical_to_physical_map on every call, and launches an extra kernel on the decode hot path. This is added work per token/step; the contiguity conversions in particular can re-copy on each forward. When EPLB is not enabled the guard (logical_to_physical_map is None) short-circuits, so the regression only affects the EPLB-enabled path, but there it is genuinely new per-forward overhead versus the parent (which had no EPLB in MegaMoE).
findings.suggested_benchmark: Decode-throughput benchmark for DeepSeek v4 MegaMoE with --enable-eplb comparing tokens/s and per-layer MoE latency vs. EPLB-disabled baseline.

It changes the number of concurrent batches used t

The new XPU Triton selective-scan kernel launches a 2D grid of only (batch_size, dim) and each program serially scans the ENTIRE sequence (the inner `for local_pos in range(chunk_tokens)` loop over every token), while placing the state-recurrence dimension (dstate) entirely inside a single program via BLOCK_DSTATE = next_power_of_2(dstate). For prefill with long sequences this leaves the sequence dimension completely unparallelized: total programs = batch*dim (e.g. a few thousand), each doing O(seqlen*dstate) serial work. On XPU this is a distinct parallelization scheme vs the reference CUDA selective_scan_fwd (which chunks the sequence and uses more threads per element). The mechanism matches kernel-occupancy-redesign: the parallelism grid is chosen per (batch,dim) row rather than distributing work across the sequence/state, risking low device occupancy and long per-program serial latency on long-context Mamba prefill.
findings.suggested_benchmark: benchmark_selective_state_update / mamba prefill throughput on XPU across seqlen in {512,2048,8192} and batch/dim sizes, comparing grid=(batch,dim) serial scan vs a sequence-chunked variant

findings.claim: 新增的 PowerPC VSX FP16<->FP32 转换构造函数 (FP16Vec16::FP16Vec16(const FP32Vec16&) 和 FP32Vec16::FP32Vec16(const FP16Vec16&)，csrc/cpu/cpu_types_vsx.hpp:~772/~789) 走了一条经内存往返的标量路径：先 vec_xst 存到栈上的 temp 数组，再用一个 16 次迭代的标量 for 循环调用 c10::Half(float)/float(c10::Half) 逐元素转换，最后 vec_xl 重新加载。这与 ARM 版本 (cpu_types_arm.hpp FP32Vec16(const FP16Vec16&)) 使用 vcvt_f32_f16 纯寄存器内 SIMD 转换形成对比。这两个转换正好位于 shm.cpp 的 all_reduce_sum_impl 热循环中：对 c10::Half 走 KernelVecType<c10::Half>=FP16Vec16，每个 vec 块都会 local_data_fp32(local_data) 和 reduced_data(local_data_fp32) 各做一次转换，因此 store->scalar-loop->load 的往返会在 SHM allreduce 的每次迭代中被执行，形成 FP16 allreduce 的吞吐瓶颈。这是新平台代码固有的次优 SIMD 路径（非把已有快路径 gate 掉），但值得后续用 VSX 原生 half<->float 指令替换标量循环。
findings.suggested_benchmark: PowerPC 上对 c10::Half 张量做 SHM all_reduce（tp>1）微基准，对比 float/bfloat16 dtype 的 allreduce 吞吐，量化 FP16 内存往返转换的开销

profile_cudagraph_memory() now instantiates a full temporary EncoderCudaGraphManager and calls encoder_cudagraph_manager.capture(graph_pool=encoder_profiling_pool), which captures ALL encoder budget graphs (get_num_graphs_to_capture() == len(token_budgets)) just to measure memory. Note the decoder path only profiles descs[:2] samples per mode, but the encoder path captures the full set of budgets, so it is proportionally more expensive.
