The dispatch gate for CPU top-k/top-p sampling was changed so that whenever Triton is available the CPU path now unconditionally routes to apply_top_k_top_p_triton (topk_topp_sampler.py: the `if current_platform.is_cpu(): if HAS_TRITON: return apply_top_k_top_p_triton(...)` block, and forward_cpu now calling apply_top_k_top_p). Previously the comment explicitly said 'Keep CPU logits on the PyTorch path to avoid invoking Triton kernels' and always used apply_top_k_top_p_pytorch. On CPU, the Triton (triton-cpu) top-k/top-p kernel is a JIT-compiled kernel; the first invocation triggers Triton compilation on the request-serving hot path, adding a large one-time latency spike, and per-launch kernel overhead for every sampling step thereafter. Unlike the GPU path (which is additionally gated by `logits.shape[0] >= 8`), the CPU path has no batch-size gate, so even single-row / small-batch decode steps go through Triton where the PyTorch sort/topk path was previously preferred for small batches. This risks a latency regression for typical CPU decode (batch size 1) unless triton-cpu meaningfully beats the native path at small sizes.
findings.suggested_benchmark: CPU decode latency (batch_size=1 and small batches, e.g. 1/4/8) with sampling params using top_k/top_p, comparing native PyTorch path vs triton-cpu path; also measure first-token latency to capture Triton JIT compile cost.

findings.claim: apply_top_k_top_p_triton on CPU now selects block_size=256/block_size_trunc=128 vs GPU's 8192/4096. Because these are Triton tl.constexpr kernel parameters, the CPU vs GPU choice creates a distinct kernel specialization that must be JIT-compiled on first use on the serving path. The device-type branch is fine, but the first CPU sampling call will pay a Triton-CPU compilation cost that was previously entirely absent (PyTorch native path). Consider warming up / precompiling this kernel during model warmup to avoid a first-request latency spike.
findings.suggested_benchmark: Measure first-request (cold) sampling latency on CPU with top_k/top_p enabled to quantify Triton-CPU JIT compile overhead.

findings.claim: In build() (per decode-step hot path), the new metadata-fingerprint logic reads common_attn_metadata.seq_lens_cpu[:num_reqs].numpy(). The CommonAttentionMetadata.seq_lens_cpu property is explicitly @deprecated with the warning 'Prefer using device seq_lens directly to avoid implicit H<>D sync which breaks full async scheduling.' When _seq_lens_cpu has not been materialized (the common decode case for this ROCm sparse MLA path, which previously never touched seq_lens_cpu — grep at parent finds no prior use in this file), the property lazily runs self.seq_lens.to('cpu'), i.e. a blocking device->host copy on every build() call. This is exactly the [blocking-d2h-sync] mechanism: a device value pulled to host forces a sync on the per-step hot path, and it breaks async scheduling. The stated goal (skip a get_mla_metadata_v1 launch when shapes are unchanged) may be net-negative because the D2H sync it introduces is itself a per-step synchronization that can be more expensive than the launch it saves.
findings.suggested_benchmark: DSv3.2 MI355X TP4 decode-step latency: compare per-step build() latency and end-to-end decode throughput with/without the metadata_key fingerprint, confirming whether seq_lens_cpu materialization forces a D2H sync (torch profiler / check for cudaStreamSynchronize in build())

findings.claim: Adjacent to the D2H read, np.minimum(seq_lens_cpu[:num_reqs].numpy(), self.topk_tokens) plus clamped_seq_lens.tobytes() runs per-step host-side arithmetic and a byte-serialization to form the cache key. Combined with the .numpy() readback this adds host work to the decode hot path that scales with num_reqs each step; if the D2H sync above is confirmed, this host post-processing compounds the latency.
findings.suggested_benchmark: Micro-bench the per-step cost of np.minimum + tobytes over num_reqs for typical TP4 decode batch sizes

findings.claim: This diff REMOVES a device-to-host synchronization, not introduces one. In build_ragged_indices_from_dense, the parent computed the flat buffer size via `int(indptr[-1].item())`, which reads a device scalar back to host and forces a full device pipeline stall (the CPU must wait for the cumsum kernel to complete before it can call `.item()` and then allocate `flat`). The new code sizes the buffer as `indices.shape[0] * max_width` (pure host-side Python int arithmetic from static tensor shapes), eliminating the blocking d2h sync. This is the exact 'blocking-d2h-sync' mechanism from the historical priors, but here the change fixes it rather than regressing it. No new sync is introduced; `indptr` init also changes from empty()+scalar-assign to zeros(), which removes the separate `indptr[0]=0` device write. Net: strictly beneficial for latency on the sparse-attention hot path.
findings.suggested_benchmark: Microbench build_ragged_indices_from_dense on GPU tensors: measure per-call latency / stream stall before vs after (torch.cuda.synchronize gating), and end-to-end DSv4 sparse-attention decode step latency on ROCm.

findings.claim: The new flat-buffer sizing uses the upper bound `indices.shape[0] * max_width` instead of the exact `indptr[-1]` sum of clamped lengths. When lengths are shorter than max_width for many rows, `flat` is over-allocated relative to actual valid entries. This is a benign, intentional trade-off (avoid the d2h sync at the cost of a slightly larger temporary buffer) and is consistent with the commit intent; not a regression, but worth noting as a minor memory-vs-latency trade. Verify downstream consumers use indptr to bound reads and do not assume flat.numel()==indptr[-1].
findings.suggested_benchmark: Compare peak temp allocation of `flat` vs sum(clamped lengths) across representative sparse index distributions; confirm no correctness/perf regression in the Triton fill kernel that follows.

findings.claim: core.py calls configure_subprocess(..., process_kind="engine_core") (lowercase, see grep at vllm/v1/engine/core.py:2050), but the refactored configure_subprocess now only accepts exactly 'worker' or 'EngineCore' and raises ValueError('Unknown process_kind ...') for anything else. If this call path with process_kind='engine_core' is reached while numa_bind=True, it will crash the engine-core subprocess spawn. Need to verify whether core.py:2050 was updated in the same PR; if not, this is a hard break of the NUMA-bound startup path.
findings.suggested_benchmark: Start engine with --numa-bind on a multi-NUMA host and confirm EngineCore/worker subprocesses spawn without ValueError.

findings.claim: For the EngineCore process, _get_numactl_enginecore_args now unions PCT priority cores across all shard NUMA nodes and emits --physcpubind=<union>. If the union of priority cores computed from /sys node cpulists happens to be empty or narrower than the worker --physcpubind sets (e.g. an SNC/fakenuma or HBM-flat topology where node cpulists are duplicated/empty), EngineCore's cpus_allowed can become a non-superset of the workers' physcpubind, breaking worker spawn or leaving memory unbound. This mirrors the historical get_numactl_cmd() failure where memory binding was silently dropped on non-'normal' topologies (HBM-flat/SNC), because the priority-core detection depends on node cpulist parsing that fails-open to no binding.
findings.suggested_benchmark: Run --numa-bind on an SNC/fakenuma host (duplicated core lists) and HBM-flat host; verify EngineCore physcpubind is a superset of every worker physcpubind and that --membind is applied.

findings.claim: static rule 'redundant-copy-of-kernel-output-buffer' matched antipattern: Copying the return value of a function into a pre-allocated slice buffer instead of passing that buffer as an out= argument, causing an extra full device-to-device copy. (matched: 'dst[:n].copy_(src)')

This commit is a performance FIX, not a regression: it replaces the over-padded `max_window_seqs_per_batch = min(max_num_batched_tokens, max_model_len)` sizing of cu_window_seqlens with a geometry-driven `get_encoder_cudagraph_max_window_seqs()` bound, and adds pluggable `padding_logics` so replay fills padded cu*_seqlens entries with the last cumulative offset (empty sequences) instead of zeros. The prior code over-padded window-attention metadata, causing FlashAttention to launch thousands of empty CTAs during CUDA-graph replay (symptom akin to kernel-noop-skip: needless work driven by padding). The diff removes that needless work; no new asymptotic or hot-path regression is introduced.

findings.claim: The producer scheduler now keeps two long-lived per-request dicts (transfer_id_to_request_id / request_id_to_transfer_id) that are no longer unmapped in request_finished on the producer path (`if not self.is_producer: self.unmap_request_id(...)`). Cleanup was moved to update_connector_output, which only unmaps req_ids that appear in connector_output.finished_sending OR that hit the defer_timeout. If a finished_sending notification is genuinely lost AND the deferred deadline was never registered (e.g. request_finished's `do_remote_prefill=False` deferred branch not taken, or a race where the entry was popped by finished_sending before deadline was set), the transfer_id<->request_id mapping stays forever. Combined with the new self._deferred_send_deadlines dict, this is precisely the 'per-request state pruned only via a notification/timeout path' shape the NIXL _reqs_to_process leak (matched leaf) warns about. The reap timeout (60s default) bounds it in the happy failure case, but any code path that maps a request_id without ever registering a deferred deadline nor emitting finished_sending will leak the mapping entries monotonically under sustained load.
findings.suggested_benchmark: Run producer (kv_producer, WRITE mode) under sustained high concurrency with a fraction of transfers that never receive finished_sending and never take the deferred-send branch; assert len(transfer_id_to_request_id)+len(request_id_to_transfer_id) returns to baseline within defer_timeout and does not grow monotonically over many thousands of requests.

findings.claim: The new consumer-side set self._unmatched_write_completions accumulates completion transfer_ids that arrived before their transfer_id was registered in transfer_id_to_request_id (`self._unmatched_write_completions |= fresh`), and is only pruned for ids that eventually match (matched_xfer_ids subtracted). If a write completion arrives for a transfer_id that never gets registered (e.g. aborted/cancelled request on the consumer, or a stale/duplicate notification), that id is never removed and the set grows unboundedly across the process lifetime. Unlike the deferred-send path this set has NO timeout/reaper, so it is a strict monotonic-growth risk under the same lost/stale-notification conditions that motivated this bugfix.
findings.suggested_benchmark: Drive the consumer (WRITE mode) with write completions whose transfer_ids never get registered (simulate aborted requests / stale notifies) and assert len(_unmatched_write_completions) stays bounded rather than growing per orphaned completion.

findings.claim: This PR fully removes the inplace fused-experts mechanism. In fused_experts_impl the output buffer changes from `out_hidden_states = hidden_states if inplace else torch.empty_like(hidden_states)` to unconditional `out_hidden_states = torch.empty_like(hidden_states)`, and analogous inplace paths in modular_kernel.py (FusedMoEKernelModularImpl) and marlin_moe.py are removed. At parent, `moe_config.disable_inplace` defaulted to `disable_inplace()` == `is_torch_equal_or_newer('2.9')`, so on torch < 2.9 without shared-experts overlap the standard/quantized MoE kernels wrote results in-place into `hidden_states` (no extra buffer). After this change, every MoE forward on torch < 2.9 now allocates a full [num_tokens, hidden] activation buffer per layer on the hot decode/prefill path, increasing peak activation memory and allocator traffic that inplace previously avoided. On torch >= 2.9 there is no behavior change (inplace was already disabled).
findings.suggested_benchmark: benchmarks/kernels/benchmark_moe.py comparing peak memory + throughput of an MoE model (e.g. Mixtral / Qwen3-MoE) on torch<2.9 before/after this commit, single-stream decode with large batch to surface the extra empty_like allocations.

findings.claim: In marlin_moe.py fused_marlin_moe, the output selection changes from `output = hidden_states if inplace else torch.empty_like(hidden_states)` to always `torch.empty_like(hidden_states)`, removing the in-place reuse for Marlin WNA16 MoE paths (compressed_tensors_moe_wna16_marlin, etc.). For torch<2.9 deployments this adds an activation-sized allocation per Marlin MoE call.
findings.suggested_benchmark: Marlin WNA16 MoE microbenchmark on torch<2.9, measure peak memory delta from removed inplace.

findings.claim: The parent code performed q head padding with a separate `F.pad(q, (0,0,0,pad_size), value=0.0)` (an extra allocation + memcpy kernel) after `_fused_qnorm_rope_kv_insert` wrote into an in-place `q` buffer. This PR fuses the pad into the fused DeepseekV4 kernel: the kernel now allocates a padded `q_out` ([N, num_heads_q_padded, 512]) and zero-fills the pad-Q slots inline. This is a net optimization (removes the standalone F.pad launch and folds strides to compile-time constants via the `kNumHeadsQPadded` template). It is the beneficial variant of the kernel-fusion pattern, not a regression.
findings.suggested_benchmark: Benchmark fused_deepseek_v4_qnorm_rope_kv_rope_quant_insert vs parent (kernel + prior F.pad) across num_heads_q in {padded 64,128}; verify no per-call regression.

findings.claim: The new CuTe DSL sparse-attn compressor uses the same `for coarsen ... : Kernel.compile(...)` warm-compile pattern as the existing indexer-Q cutedsl path (fused_indexer_q_cutedsl.py). `SparseAttnCompressNormRopeStoreC4Kernel.compile` / `SparseAttnCompressKernel.compile` are `@cache @staticmethod` decorated (so amortized after first call), but the public wrappers `_compress_kv_sparse_attn_cutedsl` / `_norm_rope_insert_sparse_attn_cutedsl` / `_fused_kv_compress_norm_rope_insert_sparse_attn_cutedsl` are invoked per-forward-token-batch. If the dispatch layer (not fully visible in the truncated diff) calls `.compile()` for multiple parameter variants on every forward instead of relying purely on the `@cache`d compiled artifact, the JIT compile (`cute.compile`) — an init-shaped operation — would land on the hot decode path. Verify the wrapper's per-call path only does a dict/cache lookup and never re-enters `cute.compile` under changing sym_int/shape args.
findings.suggested_benchmark: Micro-benchmark: time DeepseekCompressor.forward over N successive decode steps with cutedsl enabled; assert no per-step cute.compile invocation (patch/count cute.compile calls) after warmup.

findings.claim: In the batch-invariant path (VLLM_BATCH_INVARIANT=1), both SM100 and SM120 NVFP4 CUTLASS GEMMs now force the large-M `*_config_default` tile config for ALL M sizes (SM100: TileShape 256x256x256, cluster 2x1; SM120: MmaTileShape 256x128x128). This deliberately bypasses the M-based dispatch (mp2<=16 -> config_M16, mp2<=256 -> config_M256, else default). For small M (decode-time / batch-invariant token-by-token generation where M is small, e.g. M=1..16), running the large fixed tile is significantly less efficient: it wastes SM occupancy and pads M up to the tile size, so batch-invariant mode trades throughput for determinism. This is the same class of regression as the historical NVFP4 tile/config tuning case — a single fixed large-M config used regardless of M. Note this is an intentional correctness (numerical-invariance) tradeoff, so it borders on correctness-forces-slow-path, but the throughput impact on small-M shapes in batch-invariant serving should be flagged.
findings.suggested_benchmark: cutlass_scaled_fp4_mm latency across small M (1,8,16,64) vs large M (512+) with VLLM_BATCH_INVARIANT=1 vs =0, SM100/SM120

findings.claim: static rule 'tensor-to-cpu-forces-d2h-sync' matched antipattern: Moving a GPU tensor to CPU via .to('cpu')/.cpu() on a hot path forces a blocking device→host copy and CUDA synchronization. (matched: '.cpu()')

This commit adds a Triton kernel tuning workflow plus per-device tuned config JSONs (headdim=64,dstate=128 for B200/GB200/H100, fp16/fp32) for the Mamba selective_state_update (SSU) kernel, replacing/augmenting the hard-coded BLOCK_SIZE_M/num_warps heuristic in selective_state_update. This matches the historical 'kernel-config-tuning' pattern (same algorithm, tuned launch knobs). This is the intended beneficial change (better BLOCK_SIZE_M/num_warps selection); the risk to flag is only if a shipped JSON maps a shape to a WORSE config than the previous heuristic. Configs cover only headdim=64,dstate=128 — shapes without a matching JSON fall back to the heuristic, so no regression there, but the tuned configs should be spot-checked against the old defaults (dstate>128 heuristic path, non-64 headdim) to ensure no per-shape slowdown was locked in.

此改动触及 encoder-execution-mode（inference-serving），该类问题历史上主要在 [supports PP (pipelining forward step paths retained), disaggregated encoder (EPD) instances, ViT with TP (num_heads_per_partition)] 配置下显现；The diff reworks how the multimodal vision encoder batches/chunks work: it replaces the lazy per-item memory-budget heuristic (_encoder_max_batch) with a per-bu

The diff changes the default EPLB communicator for the *synchronous* (non-async) EPLB path. Previously the `else` branch (i.e. `not use_async`) selected `torch_nccl`; after this change BOTH the async and sync cases fall into the new `else` branch, which now prefers `nixl` when the nixl/rixl package is merely installed (`is_nixl_available()` only checks `importlib.util.find_spec(pkg) is not None`), falling back to `torch_gloo`. Consequences: (1) For synchronous EPLB, `torch_nccl` (GPU-native NCCL send/recv on the device process group) is no longer used at all; expert-weight exchange is routed either through NIXL staged send/recv buffers or CPU-staged Gloo, both of which are materially slower than device-side NCCL for the sync path where the multi-stream/hang rationale (cited only for async) does not apply. (2) Merely having nixl/rixl present in the environment silently reroutes the transport to NIXL even when no NIXL/RDMA fabric is beneficial, and `find_spec` presence does not guarantee a working/fast NIXL agent. This is a static default-value change that selects a slower transport path rather than a runtime predicate misfire.
findings.suggested_benchmark: Enable EPLB (enable_eplb=True) with use_async=False on a multi-GPU node, and measure expert-rearrange step latency across communicator=torch_nccl vs nixl vs torch_gloo, with and without the nixl/rixl package installed, to quantify the regression from losing the torch_nccl fast path.

findings.claim: The diff introduces a second kernel variant `fusedDeepseekV4QNormRopeKVRopeQuantInsertKernelReducedGrid` and a size-based dispatch at NUM_TOKEN_CUTOFF=1024. This changes the parallelization scheme itself for the high-token regime (>=1024 tokens): the original kernel maps one warp per (token, head_slot) pair — grid = ceil(num_tokens_full*(num_heads_q+1)/warps_per_block) — giving massively-parallel, fully-independent warps. The new reduced-grid kernel launches only gridDim.x = num_tokens_full blocks (one CTA per token, 8 warps) and each warp SERIALLY loops over its head slots with stride warpsPerBlock (`while (curr_slot < slot_end) { ... curr_slot = next_slot; }`). For e.g. n_heads=64 there are 65 slots / 8 warps ≈ 8 slots processed serially per warp, versus one warp per slot before. Whether this improves throughput depends entirely on the SM count and whether the reduced block count still saturates the GPU; on large-token workloads with high head counts this serialization of per-head work inside a warp risks reduced SM-level parallelism / lower effective occupancy relative to the flat one-warp-per-slot mapping it replaces. This is exactly a parallelization-scheme redesign (not just a launch-param tweak), so it warrants a throughput benchmark across the cutoff boundary (num_tokens 512/1024/2048/4096, n_heads 8 and 64) rather than only correctness parity.
findings.suggested_benchmark: Microbench the op at num_tokens ∈ {512,768,1024,2048,4096} × n_heads ∈ {8,64} on H100/MI300, measuring kernel latency/throughput on both sides of NUM_TOKEN_CUTOFF=1024 to confirm the reduced-grid variant is faster at >=1024 and there is no cliff at the boundary.

findings.claim: NUM_TOKEN_CUTOFF is a single hard-coded literal (1024) declared as `constexpr float` and applied uniformly regardless of num_heads_q. The crossover point between the flat-grid and reduced-grid schemes almost certainly depends on num_heads_q (which sets slots-per-token = num_heads_q+1 and thus how much work each per-token block serializes) and on GPU SM count. A fixed token-count threshold that ignores head count and device may pick the slower kernel in part of the parameter space (e.g. small n_heads at high token counts where the flat grid already saturated fine, or large n_heads just above the cutoff where per-block serial work is heavy). Consider deriving the cutoff from num_tokens_full*(num_heads_q+1) occupancy vs. SM count instead of a raw token count. Note also the float type for an integer token threshold is odd (implicit int->float compares).
findings.suggested_benchmark: Sweep n_heads and device SM counts to map the true crossover surface between the two variants and validate the constant 1024 threshold is not leaving throughput on the table for high-head-count / low-SM configurations.

The refactor batches the encoder call (good), but re-introduces two O(total_images)/O(total_frames) Python per-item loops for the pooler. In both _process_image_input and _process_video_input, after the batched encoder runs, the code loops over every image/frame calling vt.pooler(...) on a single-item tensor (single_hidden = last_hidden_states[i].unsqueeze(0)). Since the encoder outputs are already a concatenated/uniform batch (per resolution bucket for images, and all frames share pixel_values.shape[1] for video → output_length constant), the pooler could be invoked once on the full batch instead of N serial calls. As written, batching helps the encoder attention but the pooling stage still launches N separate kernel sequences plus per-item .unsqueeze/mask recomputation, capping the achievable speedup on many-image / many-frame requests.
findings.suggested_benchmark: Encode a request with many same-resolution images (e.g. 32) and a multi-frame video; compare _process_image_input/_process_video_input latency vs a version that pools the whole batch in one vt.pooler call.

findings.claim: New redundant large-tensor concatenations/copies on the hot vision path. Image path builds pv_tensor/pp_tensor via torch.cat([item[1].unsqueeze(0) ...]) per bucket-chunk, then later torch.cat(all_valid_states) and per-frame torch.cat(last_hidden_states_list) / torch.cat(all_frame_valid_states). Each torch.cat materializes a fresh full-size buffer. When inputs already arrive as a stacked tensor (same-resolution batch), the per-item unsqueeze+cat reconstructs a tensor that was already contiguous, and the intermediate last_hidden_states_map + per-index reassembly adds copies that the previous per-image path did not amortize. On large image batches this is extra allocation/copy proportional to (num_patches * hidden_size).
findings.suggested_benchmark: Profile _process_image_input with a pre-stacked same-resolution pixel_values tensor; measure allocator activity / peak memory of the cat-based bucket reconstruction vs slicing the already-stacked tensor directly.

(matched: 'torch.tensor([0, -1, 1, -1], device=')

findings.claim: MambaSpecDecodeGPUContext.create() allocates several new persistent GPU tensors and CpuGpuBuffers (state_base_addrs/state_block_strides/state_elem_sizes/state_inner_sizes/state_conv_widths/state_group_indices sized [num_layers*num_state_types], num_accepted_tokens_out sized [max_num_reqs], plus 4 CpuGpuBuffers of size max_num_reqs). These are correctly gated: MambaBuffers.create only constructs the postprocess_align sub-object when with_postprocess_align=(speculative_config is not None and model_config.is_hybrid) is true, and _prepare_inputs only stages inputs when mamba_bufs.postprocess_align is not None. So the extra allocations do NOT occur on the common non-spec / non-hybrid path. This is the intended gating and matches the buffer-sizing prior's opposite: the buffer scope is correctly bounded here. No regression, but reviewers should confirm the gate condition stays aligned with the actual kernel launch path.

In vllm/model_executor/models/utils.py, cast_overflow_tensors was changed from a guarded clamp (only clamp when isinf/isnan detected) to an unconditional torch.clamp. The old code's `tensors.isinf().any() or tensors.isnan().any()` is itself a GPU->CPU sync (the `.any()` reduction feeding a Python bool branch), so removing it eliminates a blocking-d2h-sync in the vision encoder hot path (called per-layer/per-forward in qwen2_5_vl, whisper, minicpmo). This is a legitimate sync removal, not a regression — but note it now always runs the clamp kernel, adding a small unconditional GPU op. Net: sync removed, tiny extra kernel. No regression.

The new early-return path (when _FLASHINFER_USE_PERSISTENT_CACHE=False) makes EVERY rank run FlashInfer autotuning (`fi_utils.autotune()` wrapping `runner._dummy_run` with max_num_batched_tokens) instead of only the leader rank. In the parent code, only rank 0 ran `_dummy_run` inside `autotune(tune_mode=True, cache=...)` and the resulting tactics were broadcast to all other ranks (which ran a plain, cheaper `_dummy_run`). Now all N ranks perform the full benchmark-based tuning sweep in parallel, multiplying autotune benchmarking work by the world size and lengthening startup/warmup time proportionally, especially for large TP/PP deployments.
findings.suggested_benchmark: Measure model warmup/startup wall time for flashinfer_autotune with world_size>1 (e.g. TP=8) comparing before/after this change on a Hopper/Blackwell node.

findings.claim: Disabling the persistent on-disk cache means every process restart re-runs the full autotune benchmark sweep instead of loading previously cached tactics (`AutoTuner.get().load_configs`). This trades a correctness fix (avoiding cache-collision on use_8x4_sf_layout) for repeated startup autotune cost on each launch, which will show up as recurring cold-start latency until FlashInfer upstream fixes the cache.
findings.suggested_benchmark: Compare cold-start (no cache) vs warm-start autotune time to quantify the recurring per-launch autotune cost now incurred on every startup.

findings.claim: XPUPlatform.use_custom_op_collectives() now returns True. In GroupCoordinator.__init__ (vllm/distributed/parallel_state.py:392-394) this sets self.use_custom_op_call=True, so per-collective calls (all_reduce/all_gather/reduce_scatter) on XPU are now routed through torch.ops.vllm.all_reduce/all_gather/reduce_scatter custom ops rather than directly invoking the device communicator via _all_reduce_out_place/_all_gather_out_place. This adds a group-name string lookup (_groups[group_name]() weakref deref) plus custom-op dispatch overhead on every collective in the hot forward path. This is only a net win if the ops are meant to be captured/fused under torch.compile (i.e. XPU graph is actually enabled). Note that check_and_update_config disables cudagraph_mode (falls back to PIECEWISE) for FLASH_ATTN and disables it entirely when world_size_across_dp>1 or when VLLM_XPU_ENABLE_XPU_GRAPH is off; in those non-compiled paths the custom-op indirection is pure added Python/dispatch overhead per collective with no capture/fusion benefit. Confirm XPU graph capture is actually enabled for the multi-GPU distributed configs where these collectives fire, otherwise this trades a direct communicator call for extra dispatch on the critical path.
findings.suggested_benchmark: Multi-XPU tensor-parallel decode throughput/latency (world_size>1) with and without use_custom_op_collectives, both with XPU graph enabled and disabled (VLLM_XPU_ENABLE_XPU_GRAPH=0/1), measuring per-step collective overhead.

config/vllm.py OPTIMIZATION_LEVEL_01 and _02 flip enable_flashinfer_autotune from False -> True by default. Previously it was intentionally disabled with a comment citing correctness issues (flashinfer #3197). Re-enabling autotune by default means, on Hopper/Blackwell, kernel_warmup.flashinfer_autotune() now runs an extra full _dummy_run(max_num_batched_tokens) benchmarking pass at startup. This is one-time init/warmup cost, not a steady-state hot-path regression; it lengthens startup/warmup time (potentially significantly, since autotuning benchmarks many tactics). The intent is to improve steady-state throughput. Flag as a default-value toggle change to be aware of for startup-latency benchmarks; not a per-iteration hot-path regression.
findings.suggested_benchmark: Measure engine startup/warmup wall-clock time (kernel_warmup) at optimization level 0/1/2 on SM90/SM100 with a FlashInfer MoE model, before vs after.

findings.claim: The old code wrapped every MoE forward call (TrtLlmMxfp4ExpertsMonolithic.apply, TrtLlmMxfp4ExpertsModular.apply, FlashInferCuteDSLExperts.apply) in `with autotune(_is_fi_autotuning):`. Because _is_fi_autotuning defaults to False, that context manager put the FlashInfer AutoTuner in dispatch (lookup) mode on every hot-path invocation, adding a per-call context-manager entry/exit and tactic-lookup overhead in the decode/prefill critical path. The diff REMOVES these wrappers from the forward paths, so the hot path no longer pays that per-call cost. This is a net improvement, not a regression. Confirming no regression here.

findings.claim: This commit reverts #42686, removing `_patch_should_realize_on_reuse()` and its override of Inductor's `StorageBox.should_realize_on_reuse` heuristic (gated to torch 2.11.0). Per the removed patch's own docstring, without this patch Inductor inlines a chain of computations when compiling the whole model graph at once: e.g. the residual in `fused_add_rms_norm` gets recomputed every time, and this cascades through the model. The removed heuristic used a size-aware memory-traffic cost model to force materialization of tensors reused multiple times, preventing that per-forward recomputation. Reverting it re-introduces redundant recomputation on the compiled-model runtime hot path (every forward pass) specifically for torch 2.11.0, degrading latency/throughput of compiled models. This is not init/warmup work — the recompute happens at inference time on each iteration.
findings.suggested_benchmark: On torch 2.11.0, run torch.compile fullgraph model latency/throughput benchmark (e.g. a Llama-class model with fused_add_rms_norm) with and without _patch_should_realize_on_reuse to measure per-forward recompute overhead of the residual chain.

Precision of the sampling

此改动触及 suboptimal-kernel-gate（kernel-efficiency），该类问题历史上主要在 [ZeRO-Offload / CPU-Adam, Megatron-FSDP with gradient_accumulation_fusion enabled, TP1/PP1 in test config] 配置下显现；The diff widens the eligibility gate for the AITER fused all-reduce + RMSNorm 1-stage kernel path. It replaces a fixed hidden-dim whitelist with a dtype/pack-si

The change WIDENS the 1-stage fused AllReduce+RMSNorm kernel gate rather than narrowing it. Parent code accepted only a small hardcoded set of hidden dims `hidden_dim in (512, 1024, 2048, 4096, 7168)`; the new code accepts any bf16/fp16 hidden dim that is a multiple of pack_size (8 for bf16/fp16) with `hidden_dim // pack_size <= 1024`. This makes the fast fused 1-stage kernel eligible for MORE shapes (e.g. 3072, 5120, 6144, 8192), which is the opposite of the historical `suboptimal-kernel-gate` regression (a gate that wrongly REJECTS a fast kernel). No fast path is being newly excluded here.
findings.suggested_benchmark: Fused AR+RMSNorm micro-benchmark across hidden dims {3072,5120,6144,8192} at token_num<=80 on gfx9, comparing 1-stage fused kernel vs fallback path latency to confirm the widened gate is a net win and doesn't route slower shapes into the 1-stage kernel.

findings.claim: In mhc.py, MHCPreOp.forward_hip and MHCPostOp.forward_hip now unconditionally fall back to the PyTorch-native mhc_kernels.mhc_pre_torch/mhc_post_torch, with the fused AITER kernel path (torch.ops.vllm.mhc_pre_aiter / mhc_post_aiter, taken when hidden_size % 256 == 0) commented out. For the common DeepSeek V4 config where hidden_size is divisible by 256, the previously used prebuilt fused AITER kernel is disabled, so every decoder layer on ROCm now executes the slower unfused torch path on a hot per-layer forward path (hc_pre/hc_post are called once or twice per DeepseekV4DecoderLayer, i.e. per layer per step). This is the reverse of a fused-backend-swap: removing a specialized AITER kernel forces the native fallback, a throughput regression on ROCm/AITER. Note it is an intentional accuracy fix (aiter kernel has accuracy problems at large token counts per the TODO), but it does trade throughput for correctness.
findings.suggested_benchmark: DeepSeek V4 decode/prefill throughput on ROCm/AITER (VLLM_ROCM_USE_AITER=1) with hidden_size % 256 == 0, comparing mhc_pre/mhc_post fused AITER vs torch fallback

findings.claim: In sparse_attn_indexer.py forward_hip, the non-AITER / skip_k_cache_insert branch that dispatched to rocm_aiter_sparse_attn_indexer_native was removed and the code now raises RuntimeError unless AITER is enabled. This is not a perf regression per se (it restricts a code path rather than slowing an existing one), but callers that previously ran the eager native ROCm path with AITER disabled will now error rather than run; verify no supported configuration silently loses a working path. The topk changes (removal of _topk_indices_prefill/_topk_indices_decode fallback and _TOPK_FAST_PATH_VALUES={2048} gating in favor of always calling the fused torch.ops._C.top_k_per_row_prefill/decode) are a perf improvement (removes the slow logits.topk + masked_fill fallback), matching the historical kernel-fusion sparse indexer case in the improving direction, not a regression.
findings.suggested_benchmark: DeepSeek V3.2/V4 sparse attention indexer topk latency on ROCm with topk_tokens != 2048 (previously slow torch fallback, now fused kernel)

findings.claim: 此改动触及 unfused-transpose-copy（kernel-efficiency），该类问题历史上主要在 [context parallel (thd/CP path affected); applies when apply_rope_fusion=True, context-parallel/PP2 TP1 in affected tests, context parallelism (CP) and/or packed sequence (THD qkv_format); apply_rope_fusion enabled] 配置下显现；The diff optimizes the MLA _v_up_proj bmm by removing an explicit intermediate transpose + reshape + resize + copy sequence, instead writing bmm output directly

This diff is a performance improvement, not a regression. In the non-aiter branch of `_v_up_proj`, the parent code performed `torch.bmm(x, self.W_UV, out=out)` on a transposed `out` view, then created `out_new` via `out.transpose(0,1).reshape(...)`, `out.resize_(...)`, and finally an explicit `out.copy_(out_new)` device-to-device copy plus a temporary allocation. The new code writes the bmm result directly into the correctly-shaped transposed output buffer with `torch.bmm(x, self.W_UV, out=out.transpose(0, 1))`, eliminating the redundant copy and temp allocation. This directly matches (and fixes) the historical `redundant-buffer-copy` mechanism seen in the MLA `_v_up_proj` path — no new regression is introduced.
findings.suggested_benchmark: MLA decode/prefill microbenchmark on DeepSeek-V3 measuring _v_up_proj latency (bmm + output layout) to confirm removal of extra copy improves latency without accuracy change

findings.claim: In `eager_break_during_capture.wrapper`, the eager-break dispatch only checks `is_forward_context_available()` -> `cudagraph_runtime_mode == FULL` to bail out to `fn(...)` directly. But the segment-splitting path (`capture.add_eager`) still fires for any non-FULL mode whenever a `BreakableCUDAGraphCapture` is active and `_capturing`. If an attention/kv-cache custom op decorated with `@eager_break_during_capture` is invoked during a context where the tensors are NOT the static capture buffers (e.g. a nested/eager forward that happens to run inside a capture thread), the recorded `lambda: fn(*weak_args, **weak_kwargs)` will deref weak refs whose backing memory was reclaimed, silently producing wrong results or a crash on replay. The correctness of the whole scheme hinges on decorated ops always writing in-place into static buffers; a fresh-tensor-returning op (or one added later without the in-place contract) would change addresses each replay and break downstream graph segments — exactly the cudagraph-incompatibility class where capture succeeds but replay reads stale/moved memory.
findings.suggested_benchmark: Capture+replay a model with a decorated attention op that returns a fresh tensor and assert output address stability across replays under VLLM_LOGGING_LEVEL=DEBUG.

findings.claim: 此改动触及 one-time-setup-on-hot-path（host-overhead），该类问题历史上主要在 [tensor/sequence parallel (NVLS symmetric-memory collectives); regression affected all configs incl. training, any (per-iteration DDP grad-buffer path); MoE/expert-parallel params also covered, distributed checkpointing (use_dist_ckpt), data-parallel + context-parallel save group] 配置下显现；The change moves IR op priority setup (and torch-wrap flag) out of the per-forward-pass set_forward_context context managers and into a one-time worker-init cal
findings.suggested_benchmark: gpt-perf — CPU-bound launch overhead; small-batch step time

findings.claim: This PR is a performance optimization, not a regression. At parent, set_forward_context() (called on EVERY forward pass, e.g. gpu_model_runner.py:4150 and :5731) entered vllm_config.kernel_config.ir_op_priority.set_priority() and vllm.ir.enable_torch_wrap() as context managers on each invocation. That per-forward path called current_platform.import_ir_kernels() (imports vllm.kernels) and rebuilt filtered priority impl lists via an ExitStack over all op fields plus set/restore of the global _ENABLE_TORCH_WRAP every pass. The diff hoists this constant-for-worker-lifetime state into WorkerBase.__init__ via set_default()/set_default_torch_wrap(), removing repeated ExitStack construction, per-forward assert/filter loops, and repeated import_ir_kernels() checks from the hot forward path. The dispatch hot path (IrOp.dispatch reading self._priority_impls) is unchanged. No new per-forward allocation or gating that rejects a fast kernel was introduced. Verified the new set_default keeps identical filtering semantics (_filter_priority_impls extracted from the old inner closure). No regression found.
findings.suggested_benchmark: Measure per-step overhead of set_forward_context before/after (e.g. many small decode steps) to confirm reduced Python overhead; also verify eager-mode latency for a model with IR ops enabled.

findings.claim: In AiterMLAMetadataBuilder._build_fp8_prefill_ps_metadata (invoked from build() every step whenever prefill tokens are present and FP8 MLA prefill is enabled) two host-blocking device->host reads happen unconditionally on the per-step metadata path: `total_prefill_tokens = int(qo_indptr_cpu[-1].item())` and, worse, `num_partial_tiles = int(self.fp8_ps_reduce_indptr[-1].item())`. The latter reads a DEVICE tensor filled by get_ps_metadata_v1, forcing a full GPU sync every build. The code comment even acknowledges 'build is allowed to incur an occasional sync' — but this sync now occurs on EVERY prefill/chunked-prefill build step, matching the blocking-d2h-sync historical mechanism (device value pulled to host, then host arithmetic/allocation done from it).
findings.suggested_benchmark: Prefill/chunked-prefill throughput on gfx950 with FP8 MLA enabled: measure per-step attention metadata build() latency and end-to-end prefill tokens/s vs. the flash_attn_varlen_func fallback; isolate the .item() sync via a torch profiler timeline (look for cudaStreamSynchronize in build()).

findings.claim: static rule 'tensor-item-in-forward-arg' matched antipattern: Calling .item() on a device tensor to pass a Python scalar into a kernel/op on the per-forward hot path forces a device-to-host sync and stalls the GPU pipeline. (matched: 'qo_indptr_cpu[-1].item())')

此改动触及 warmup-shape-gap（compilation），该类问题历史上主要在 [context-parallel (cp>1) and tensor-parallel; sequence_parallel affects shapes, sequence parallelism enabled with tensor_model_parallel_size > 1, sequence-parallel with tensor_model_parallel_size>1] 配置下显现；The diff gates model warmup/compilation on the CPU worker based on whether kv_caches are empty (embedding/no-kv-cache models). Previously these models were neve

The added block conditionally calls `self.model_runner.warming_up_model()` inside `compile_or_warm_up_model()` when `len(self.model_runner.kv_caches) == 0` (embedding/pooling models without KV cache). This is intentionally one-time init-shaped work (model warmup/compilation) placed on the startup path, not the per-step hot path. For KV-cache-less models, `determine_available_memory()` (which normally triggers `warming_up_model()`) is skipped in `EngineCore._initialize_kv_caches` because `has_kv_cache` is False, so this branch ensures embedding models still get compiled exactly once at startup. This is a correctness fix, not a regression; no redundant/duplicate compilation because it only runs when kv_caches is empty (i.e., the path where determine_available_memory did NOT warm up). No performance regression introduced.
findings.suggested_benchmark: CPU embedding model startup time (compile-or-startup) with and without KV cache to confirm warmup runs exactly once

findings.claim: The new TOKENSPEED_MLA decode backend is inserted at auto-selection priority 2 in `_get_backend_priorities` (vllm/platforms/cuda.py), directly behind FLASHINFER_MLA and ahead of CUTLASS_MLA/FLASH_ATTN_MLA/FLASHMLA. The PR's own comment states it 'wins past bs≈8, regresses at bs≤2', yet `supports_combination` in vllm/v1/attention/backends/mla/tokenspeed_mla.py only gates on R1 dims + FP8 KV + compute capability — it has NO batch-size/query-len gate. Backend selection happens once at init (cold path, model_config-driven), so for any DSR1/Kimi + FP8 KV workload on Blackwell running at small batch sizes (bs≤2, the common low-latency / interactive-serving regime), auto-selection will pick TOKENSPEED_MLA over FLASHINFER_MLA and lock in the slower kernel for the whole run. Since FLASHINFER_MLA supports the exact same (fp8, R1) combination and would validate first at priority 1, but TOKENSPEED at priority 2 is placed ahead of the previously-default backends, this is a gating decision that can systematically route small-batch decode onto a kernel the authors know regresses there.
findings.suggested_benchmark: MLA decode latency (DeepSeek-R1, --kv-cache-dtype fp8) on SM100/Blackwell at batch sizes 1, 2, 4, 8, 16 — compare FLASHINFER_MLA vs auto-selected TOKENSPEED_MLA to confirm the crossover point and whether auto-selection regresses at bs<=2.

findings.suggested_benchmark: gpt-perf — CPU-bound launch overhead; small-batch step time

findings.claim: 此改动触及 cudagraph-enablement（compilation），该类问题历史上主要在 [inference; expert-parallel path has special handling (decode_only_cuda_graphs / EP all-reduce guard against NCCL hang), expert parallel (EP>1) combined with tensor + data parallel; MoE dynamic inference, distributed optimizer with fp8-param-gather; tp=2 in tests] 配置下显现；This commit enables ViT full CUDA graph support for Qwen3.5 vision encoder — adding the model to MODELS_SUPPORT_VIT_CUDA_GRAPH, wiring model_config, and documen

In FlexAttentionMetadataBuilder.__init__, the new logic `if self.direct_build and self.kv_block_size != self.block_size: self.direct_build = False` disables the direct_build fast path whenever a custom flex_attn_kv_block_size is specified that differs from cache block_size. The direct_build path (supported on torch>=2.9) is the optimized block-mask construction route; falling back to the non-direct-build path routes through get_kernel_options's ensure_divisible / halving branch, which (per the historical kernel-config-tuning case) can compute BLOCK_M/BLOCK_N tiles well below 16 and hurts GPU utilization / adds pipeline bubbles. Exposing these knobs to users makes it easy to silently drop out of the direct_build fast path.
findings.suggested_benchmark: Benchmark FlexAttention throughput/latency with default kv_block_size vs a custom flex_attn_kv_block_size != cache block_size to quantify the cost of dropping direct_build.

findings.claim: 此改动触及 kernel-fusion（kernel-efficiency），该类问题历史上主要在 [MoE (expert parallelism / token dispatch), inference dynamic batching (non-static), Megatron-FSDP (requires __fsdp_param__), used with gradient_accumulation_fusion=True; TE activation offloading context mentioned] 配置下显现；This diff reorders compilation passes in the PostGradPassManager so the more-specific RMSNorm+router-pad fusion (RocmAiterTritonAddRMSNormPadFusionPass) runs be

In `prepare_encoder_metadata` (qwen2_vl.py), `compute_attn_mask_seqlen(cu_seqlens)` is called on the CPU tensor `cu_seqlens` (built via torch.from_numpy) to compute `max_seqlen`. Historically for Qwen2-VL this method's `.max()` on cu_seqlens is the site of a device->host CUDA sync when cu_seqlens lives on GPU. In the new refactor cu_seqlens is CPU-side before the `.to(device=...)` copy, so on the eager path max_seqlen is computed on CPU — this is acceptable — but note the returned `max_seqlen` is a CPU tensor (torch.tensor(...) with no device), which the attention backends read; reading a host-scalar into the FA kernel per forward keeps behavior similar to prior code. Verify max_seqlen is not later `.item()`'d on a GPU tensor in the hot (eager, non-CG) path introduced by encoder_eager_forward.
findings.suggested_benchmark: Profile Qwen2-VL image/video eager (non-CUDA-graph) encoder forward and check for a cudaStreamSynchronize / D2H copy in compute_attn_mask_seqlen per multimodal item.

findings.claim: static rule 'tensor-to-cpu-forces-d2h-sync' matched antipattern: Moving a GPU tensor to CPU via .to('cpu')/.cpu() on a hot path forces a blocking device→host copy and CUDA synchronization. (matched: '.cpu()')

The new `_should_share` helper adds a `torch.equal(draft.weight, target.weight)` comparison over the full weight tensor (e.g. vocab embedding / lm_head, which can be hundreds of MB). Under low-memory conditions it even copies both tensors to CPU (`w.cpu()`, `target.weight.cpu()`) before comparing. However, this runs inside `load_eagle_model`, which is called once from `EagleSpeculator.load_model` at model-initialization time — not on any per-step/hot path. So it is one-time setup cost, not a recurring hot-path regression. It only fires when the draft actually has its own copy (`has_own_*` True and draft weight present), so the common weight-sharing case still short-circuits early. Impact is a bounded, one-time load-time cost; acceptable, but worth noting the added D2H copies + equality scan can add seconds at startup for large vocab tables.
findings.suggested_benchmark: Measure eagle/MTP model load_model() wall time with a large-vocab draft that has_own_embed_tokens/has_own_lm_head, comparing GPU torch.equal path vs the low-mem CPU-copy path.

findings.claim: This is a revert of PR #39917 which replaced the old SharedMemory/fcntl routing-replay capture with a device cache + async D2H pipeline. The revert reintroduces the old routed_experts_capturer.py that uses multiprocessing.shared_memory + fcntl file-locking and synchronous .cpu().numpy() D2H transfers to move routing data from the GPU worker to the scheduler. Per the deleted design doc's own analysis, the old path performs synchronous D2H copies that block the GPU pipeline (drain the command queue) plus file-based locking overhead on every capture — this is the exact mechanism the PR being reverted was written to eliminate (~2% -> higher overhead). Restoring it re-adds a blocking D2H + fcntl lock on the routing-capture path when --enable-return-routed-experts is set.
findings.suggested_benchmark: Throughput with --enable-return-routed-experts, ISL=1024/OSL=1024, MoE model: compare async-pipeline (parent) vs reverted synchronous-D2H+fcntl capture.

In both acquire_write and acquire_read the `def check(): ...` closure is defined *inside* the `while True:` polling loop body (recreated on every loop iteration, and every acquire call). These are the hottest paths of the SHM message queue (per-broadcast, per-poll). Defining a new Python function object (capturing metadata_buffer, self, local_reader_rank) on every iteration adds allocation/bind overhead on a lock-free spin path that previously was pure inline byte reads. Even when VLLM_USE_SPINLOOP_EXT is disabled, `check()` is now called (replacing the previously inlined `sum(metadata_buffer[1:])`/flag reads) via Python function-call indirection plus closure creation each iteration, adding per-iteration overhead to a path designed to be as tight as possible.
findings.suggested_benchmark: Microbench MessageQueue acquire_read/acquire_write busy-poll throughput with VLLM_USE_SPINLOOP_EXT=0 before vs after; compare per-iteration cost of inline flag check vs closure+call.

findings.claim: `VLLM_USE_SPINLOOP_EXT` env var is read via the module-level snapshot `if envs.VLLM_USE_SPINLOOP_EXT: from vllm.spinloop import spinloop` at shm_broadcast.py import time. This snapshots the toggle at first-import; the runtime branches in acquire_write/acquire_read re-read `envs.VLLM_USE_SPINLOOP_EXT` (dynamic), so if the env is enabled after this module is imported, the runtime branch evaluates True but `spinloop` was never imported into the module namespace -> NameError at the hot poll path. Conversely if the module is imported before the env is set (common: envs read lazily), the fast busy-poll path is never wired. This is the classic 'static import-time snapshot of a perf toggle chooses the wrong (slow/broken) path' mechanism.
findings.suggested_benchmark: Set VLLM_USE_SPINLOOP_EXT=1 and verify acquire_read/acquire_write hot loop uses spinloop; also test enabling env after module import to catch NameError/stale-toggle.

findings.claim: The new cutlass_scaled_mm_*_fp8_batch_invariant_dispatch paths deliberately pin a fixed small-M CUTLASS config (SM90: M64_N1280/M64_N8192 with swap_ab; SM100: M64/M64_swap_ab; SM120/SM89: M64) regardless of the actual M, whereas the normal dispatch selects M-tuned tiles (SM90 default 128x128x128 / M8192 256x128x128; SM100 M256 128x128x128 / default 256x128x128). For large-M (prefill / high batch) this fixed M=64 config yields lower GEMM occupancy/throughput than the M-tuned config. However this is the intended and required mechanism for batch invariance (tile/reduction schedule must be M-independent), and it only runs when vllm_is_batch_invariant()/VLLM_BATCH_INVARIANT is true, so it is not an unintended regression. This matches the 'correctness/determinism forces a slower path' shape rather than an unnecessarily restrictive gate.

findings.claim: In the new CuteDSL dispatch path, fused_indexer_q_rope_quant_mxfp4_cutedsl() unconditionally loops `for coarsen in (1, 4): IndexerQMxFp4Kernel.compile(...)` on EVERY call, and then calls compile() a third time for the chosen coarsen. This function is invoked from the SparseAttnIndexer on the indexer hot path (per forward/decode step, see deepseek_v4_attention.attention_impl -> indexer(...)). The intent is 'compile all variants at first invocation', but there is no first-call guard; correctness relies entirely on the @cache memoization on compile(). Any cache miss (e.g. dtype/head_dim/num_heads variation between prefill and decode, or the ordering issue below) turns this into a full CuteDSL cute.compile() on the critical path.
findings.suggested_benchmark: Microbench fused_indexer_q_rope_quant(use_fp4=True) decode-shape loop over many iterations; assert no cute.compile invocations after warmup and steady-state latency unchanged vs Triton path.

findings.claim: IndexerQMxFp4Kernel.compile is decorated as `@cache` ON TOP OF `@staticmethod` (cache is the outer decorator). On Python <3.10 this raises because staticmethod objects were not callable; even where it runs, the intended memoization is fragile. If the cache ever fails to key/hit (e.g. because compile is looked up through the class each call and re-wrapped, or arguments like the Numeric type object don't hash stably), the 'compile all variants at first invocation' comment is violated and cute.compile() (an expensive JIT compile) runs on every indexer call — a severe per-step latency regression rather than a one-time warmup cost.
findings.suggested_benchmark: Instrument cute.compile with a call counter; run repeated fused_indexer_q_rope_quant_mxfp4_cutedsl calls with fixed shapes and verify counter stays at 2 (the two coarsen variants) after warmup.

findings.claim: In build_connector_meta (called every scheduler step, a hot path), when self._heartbeat_by_engine is non-empty and the throttle interval has elapsed, the entire self._heartbeat_by_engine dict is assigned by reference to meta.heartbeat_by_engine (meta.heartbeat_by_engine = self._heartbeat_by_engine). This dict is then serialized/passed to the worker every heartbeat_interval (kv_lease_duration // 6, e.g. 5s by default). While throttled, the cost per step is small, but the D-worker side _send_heartbeats iterates over every engine and every remote_agent and calls nixl_wrapper.send_notif() for the full accumulated set of req_ids on each heartbeat tick. As the number of in-flight D-side requests grows, this scales O(engines * agents) with a comma-joined string of ALL tracked req_ids per engine, sent repeatedly. The per-step guard mitigates frequency but the message payload grows unbounded with queue depth.
findings.suggested_benchmark: PD disaggregation with a large D-side WAITING queue (hundreds of do_remote_prefill requests); measure per-step scheduler latency and heartbeat notif send cost as queue depth grows.

In the newly added padding path of CutlassFP8ScaledMMLinearKernel.apply_scaled_mm, the weight matrix B is re-padded on EVERY forward call via `torch.nn.functional.pad(B.t().contiguous(), (0, pad_k, 0, pad_n)).t()`. apply_scaled_mm is invoked from FP8ScaledMMLinearKernel.apply_weights (ScaledMMLinearKernel.py:145) for every forward pass, and B here is the layer weight `w` — a STATIC tensor that never changes. The `.t().contiguous()` forces a full O(N*K) materializing copy of the weight matrix, followed by a pad, followed by another transpose, redoing constant work on every token/batch. This is exactly the unfused-transpose-copy pattern from the historical SM100 blockwise-FP8 case (pad(...).contiguous() copy + post-slice). Weight padding should be hoisted into process_weights_after_loading (currently a no-op `pass` in FP8ScaledMMLinearKernel), so the padded weight is computed once at load time. For models like Qwen2.5-VL vision MLP (the stated motivation) this padded copy runs on the critical GEMM path repeatedly.
findings.suggested_benchmark: Micro-benchmark apply_scaled_mm on Qwen2.5-VL vision MLP dims (e.g. N=3420,K=1280) across batch sizes m=1..256; compare per-call latency of padding-in-forward vs padding weight once at load; also profile end-to-end vision encoder throughput.

findings.claim: The output slice-and-copy `output = output[..., :N].contiguous()` in the pad_n>0 branch adds a full-output materializing copy on every forward. The subsequent `.view(*output_shape)` only needs a contiguous slice; the extra `.contiguous()` copies the entire [M, N] output each call. This compounds the per-forward overhead of the padding path (same unfused-transpose/copy overhead class).
findings.suggested_benchmark: Compare latency of returning output[..., :N].contiguous() vs a non-copying reshape path for the padded-N case across m in {1,32,64,256}.

In _forward_core_rocm (new ROCm path), for every non-decode-fast invocation the code eagerly zeroes both `core_attn_out.zero_()` and `z_out.zero_()` before writing them, then immediately overwrites them: `z_out[:] = z` fully populates z_out, and `_forward_core` writes core_attn_out via the fused kernel. This matches the redundant-buffer-copy prior (wasted zero on a correctly-sized buffer).

The diff removes the dedicated `WorkerAsyncOutput` ThreadPoolExecutor from UniProcExecutor and replaces the previously async-submitted output with a lazy `AsyncOutputFuture` whose `result()` calls `self.async_output.get_output()` inline on the caller's thread. `AsyncOutput.get_output()` (vllm/v1/worker/gpu/async_utils.py) performs a blocking `self.copy_event.synchronize()` plus D2H `.tolist()` materialization of sampled tokens/logprobs. In the async-scheduling path (`max_concurrent_batches > 1`), the old code ran this blocking sync+materialization on a background worker thread, allowing it to overlap with the main thread submitting/executing the next batch. Now, when the engine calls `future.result()`, the CUDA sync and Python-list materialization run on the main/engine thread and block it, removing the compute/output-copy overlap that async scheduling was designed to provide. This can reduce throughput for the async-scheduling uniproc case.
findings.suggested_benchmark: Async-scheduling (async_scheduling=True) single-GPU decode throughput benchmark comparing tokens/s and per-step latency before vs after; also check p50/p99 ITL under sustained decode.

This diff enables SimpleCPUOffloadConnector on ROCm by routing the batch-DMA path to hipMemcpyBatchAsync (ROCm 7.1+) with numAttrs=0 in copy_blocks() (vllm/v1/simple_kv_offload/cuda_mem_ops.py). On ROCm 7.2.1/7.2.2 the runtime rejects any call with numAttrs>0, so the srcAccessOrder=ANY attribute the CUDA path relies on is dropped. Passing numAttrs=0 means srcAccessOrder defaults to the runtime default (STREAM/ordered) rather than ANY. srcAccessOrder=ANY is a performance hint that lets the driver reorder/overlap the individual copies inside the batch; forcing ordered access order on ROCm can serialize the per-block DMAs and reduce copy overlap versus the CUDA path. This matches the fused-backend-swap prior (routing ROCm to a platform-specific batch API) but with a degraded attribute set. Verify hip default srcAccessOrder is not more conservative than ANY.
findings.suggested_benchmark: Benchmark KV offload store/load throughput (GB/s) on ROCm 7.2.x comparing hipMemcpyBatchAsync numAttrs=0 vs the CUDA cuMemcpyBatchAsync path with srcAccessOrder=ANY, for a workload with many layers x blocks.

The diff flips `enable_flashinfer_autotune` from True to False in OPTIMIZATION_LEVEL_01 and OPTIMIZATION_LEVEL_02 (the latter being the default optimization_level = O2). Per flashinfer_autotune() docstring in vllm/model_executor/warmup/kernel_warmup.py, 'Without autotuning, FlashInfer will rely on heuristics, which may be significantly slower.' Disabling it removes the startup autotuning pass, so FlashInfer kernels fall back to heuristic selection at runtime, degrading steady-state attention/GEMM/MoE kernel performance (latency & throughput) on Hopper/Blackwell GPUs. This is a static config default choosing the slower path — matching the config-toggle-perf-feature mechanism. Note this affects the *default* profile (O2), not an opt-in path.
findings.suggested_benchmark: End-to-end decode/prefill latency and throughput benchmark on a Hopper/Blackwell GPU at default -O2 with a FlashInfer-backed model, comparing enable_flashinfer_autotune True vs False.

findings.claim: The change is explicitly a temporary correctness workaround (comment references flashinfer-ai/flashinfer#3197). This is a deliberate slow-path-for-correctness tradeoff rather than an unnecessary gate; it saves startup autotune time but sacrifices runtime kernel performance. It should be reverted once the upstream FlashInfer correctness issue is fixed, otherwise the perf regression becomes permanent by inertia.

static rule 'tensor-from-pylist-on-device' matched antipattern: Constructing a tensor from a Python list literal with a non-CPU device forces a CPU materialization plus a host-to-device copy on every call; use torch.zeros/ones/full/empty directly on the device instead. (matched: 'torch.tensor(\n            [seed, offset], dtype=torch.int64, device=')

In the new hot-path forward_xpu (called every decode step for sampling), the code pulls the XPU RNG state to host and builds a CPU scalar tensor: `state = generator.get_state(); seed, offset = state.view(torch.int64); seeds = torch.tensor([seed, offset], dtype=torch.int64, device=torch.device('cpu'))`. `generator.get_state()` reads device RNG state to host, and unpacking `seed, offset = state.view(...)` materializes device scalars on host each call. Building the CPU tensor from a Python list adds further per-step host work. This is the classic blocking-d2h-sync pattern (reading a device value to host in a per-step hot loop) previously seen with eagle spec-decode next_token_ids and FP8 overflow-buffer construction.
findings.suggested_benchmark: Micro-benchmark forward_xpu vs forward_native on XPU across decode steps measuring per-step host/device sync stalls introduced by generator.get_state() + seeds tensor construction.

findings.claim: This diff flips the default of VLLM_USE_RAY_V2_EXECUTOR_BACKEND from "0" to "1" in vllm/envs.py, changing the default Ray executor (when distributed_executor_backend="ray") from RayDistributedExecutor (compiled-graph / accelerated-DAG backend) to RayExecutorV2 (MQ / message-queue based). This is the same mechanism as the retrieved VLLM_ALLREDUCE_USE_SYMM_MEM cases: a static config default now routes all Ray-based multi-GPU deployments to a different per-step dispatch path (see vllm/v1/executor/abstract.py:60-66 `if envs.VLLM_USE_RAY_V2_EXECUTOR_BACKEND: RayExecutorV2 else RayDistributedExecutor`). The compiled-graph (aDAG) path exists specifically to cut per-step RPC/orchestration overhead in the decode hot loop; defaulting away from it risks a per-token dispatch latency/throughput regression for TP/PP Ray users if RayExecutorV2's MQ path has higher steady-state overhead. Note the (TYPE_CHECKING) annotated default at envs.py:63 was left as `False`, so the declared type stub and the runtime default now disagree — a secondary inconsistency worth fixing.
findings.suggested_benchmark: Multi-GPU (TP>=2, and TP+PP) online-serving throughput/latency (ITL, TTFT) with distributed_executor_backend=ray, comparing VLLM_USE_RAY_V2_EXECUTOR_BACKEND=0 (RayDistributedExecutor/compiled-graph) vs =1 (RayExecutorV2/MQ) at steady-state decode.

findings.claim: The new `TokenizerPool` class in `maybe_make_thread_pool` wraps every hot-path tokenizer method (`encode`, `decode`, `batch_decode`, `batch_encode`, `convert_tokens_to_ids`, `convert_ids_to_tokens`, `__call__`, etc.) so that each individual call now pays the overhead of entering the `_borrow_from_pool()` contextmanager: a generator-based `@contextlib.contextmanager` setup/teardown plus a `queue.Queue.get_nowait()` + `queue.Queue.put()` (each takes the Queue's internal lock). Previously these calls dispatched straight to the underlying Rust fast tokenizer with zero Python indirection. Per-request tokenization (`_tokenize_prompt`/`_detokenize_prompt` via `tokenizer.encode`/`tokenizer.decode`) is a hot path, so this adds constant per-call Python/lock overhead to every tokenize/detokenize on HfRenderer models.
findings.suggested_benchmark: Microbenchmark tokenizer.encode/decode throughput before vs after wrapping with maybe_make_thread_pool; e.g. tight loop of 100k encode() calls on a short prompt, and an end-to-end serving benchmark measuring TTFT / detokenize latency under concurrency.

findings.claim: `_borrow_from_pool()` can silently exceed the pool size: when the queue is empty it falls into the `queue.Empty` branch and does a full `copy.deepcopy(og_tokenizer)` (a fresh Rust tokenizer clone) then `put`s it back into the pool, permanently growing the pool. Under high concurrency (more simultaneous callers than `renderer_num_workers + 1`) this triggers repeated expensive deepcopies of the tokenizer on the request path, and the pool never shrinks — a latency spike on bursty load plus unbounded memory growth.
findings.suggested_benchmark: Drive N concurrent tokenize requests with N > renderer_num_workers+1 and measure the count/cost of deepcopy calls and steady-state pool size / RSS.

This PR refactors Qwen2.5-VL's ViT forward to extract `prepare_encoder_metadata()`, which is now called from the eager `forward` path when `encoder_metadata is None`. The metadata computation is functionally identical to the pre-change inline path (same `compute_attn_mask_seqlen`, same non_blocking .to(device) copies), so the eager (non-CUDA-graph) path retains the same host/device sync characteristics. No new blocking device→host sync (e.g. GPU-tensor .item()/slice-bound reads) is introduced on the eager hot path by the refactor itself. This diff *adds* warmup coverage rather than removing it. The new second self.sampler(...) call in _dummy_sampler_run intentionally exercises the forward_native code path of TopKTopPSampler (taken when generators dict is non-empty) so its kernel is warmed during profile_run, closing a warmup-shape/path gap. This is the correct direction (matching the historical warmup-shape-gap fix that added a dummy sampler run) and does not introduce a regression. The author correctly gates out 'processed_logits'/'processed_logprobs' modes where forward is already bound to forward_native at init, avoiding a redundant extra call that would only inflate peak memory during profile_run.

This PR introduces the fused_add_rms_norm IR op with a maybe_inplace overload plus a functionalization/clone-elimination pipeline whose entire purpose is to preserve the historical zero-copy behavior of the in-place fused_add_rms_norm kernel. This is exactly the code region that the historical 'redundant-buffer-copy under auto_functionalize' bug touched (in-place fused_add_rms_norm wrapped by functionalization inserting extra copy/slice_scatter nodes). The correctness of the memory savings depends on (a) the pre-grad inplace-functionalization pass correctly recording donated inputs and (b) the UnsafeCloneEliminationPass actually removing the clones the lowering pass inserts around torch.ops._C.fused_add_rms_norm. If either pass fails to fire (e.g. a donated graph input is not marked, or a clone is not recognized as removable), the compiled graph will retain aten.clone(x)/aten.clone(residual) per layer — reintroducing exactly the per-layer redundant buffer copies of the historical regression. Recommend validating via a compiled-graph node count assertion (no residual aten.clone on the fused_add_rms_norm inputs) in tests/compile/passes/ir/test_clone_cleanup.py.
findings.suggested_benchmark: Compile a transformer decoder layer with fused_add_rms_norm.maybe_inplace and assert the post-lowering graph contains no residual aten.clone on the norm inputs; compare peak memory / throughput vs parent.

findings.claim: In rocm_unquantized_gemm_impl (a hot per-linear-layer path), a new AITER tuned-GEMM branch is added: `if rocm_aiter_ops.is_tgemm_enabled(): from aiter.tuned_gemm import tgemm; return tgemm.mm(x, weight, bias)`. The gate `is_tgemm_enabled()` only checks `is_linear_enabled() and on_gfx950()` — it does NOT verify that the specific (M,N,K,dtype) shape is actually tuned/present in aiter's tuned config (unlike the existing use_aiter_triton_gemm which whitelists exact shapes, and unlike _check_kernel_tuned which validates tuned configs). This mirrors the historical skinny-gemm gating bug (#15830) where a fast kernel was applied on all shapes/arches based on a coarse gate. If tgemm.mm falls back to an untuned/slow path or a JIT-tuning path for arbitrary shapes on gfx950, this can regress throughput vs torch.nn.functional.linear for the unquantized attention projection GEMMs it now intercepts.
findings.suggested_benchmark: On gfx950 with VLLM_ROCM_USE_AITER_LINEAR=1, benchmark unquantized linear (qkv/o projections) latency across representative (M,N,K) shapes comparing tgemm.mm vs torch.nn.functional.linear, including shapes NOT present in aiter's tuned config to catch untuned fallback regressions.

The bidirectional KV transfer changes broaden the set of requests that go into the deferred-free path (_reqs_need_send -> worker _reqs_to_send) in NixlConnectorScheduler.request_finished. Previously only P-side do_remote_decode requests with status FINISHED_LENGTH_CAPPED had their blocks retained until the remote decoder issued a READ notification. Now (1) D-node requests (is_d_node = not do_remote_decode) also enter this path when is_bidirectional_kv_xfer_enabled, and (2) the accepted terminal status set is widened to include FINISHED_STOPPED in addition to FINISHED_LENGTH_CAPPED. This is exactly the historical NixlConnector delay_free_blocks pattern: blocks are held pending a remote READ, and if the remote consumer never reads (proxy down, request aborted, or the proxy's single-use ConversationKVCache TTL of 450s expires before the peer pulls), the blocks are stranded until VLLM_NIXL_ABORT_REQUEST_TIMEOUT. Widening both the node role and status conditions increases the volume and lifetime of retained blocks, so the deferred-free window grows and reclamation depends on the peer/proxy behaving. Confirm the worker-side _reqs_to_send timeout sweep still reclaims all newly-added D-node entries.
findings.suggested_benchmark: Multi-turn disagg P/D run under bidirectional_kv_xfer where a fraction of turns are aborted / proxy drops the next turn; monitor prefiller+decoder KV block free-pool occupancy over time vs baseline to verify blocks are reclaimed within VLLM_NIXL_ABORT_REQUEST_TIMEOUT and do not grow monotonically.

The AMX FP8 KV-cache path adds a per-tile software dequant (deq_tile_amx/prepare_b_tile) that runs inside the inner GEMM k-loop of TileGemm224/TileGemm122. For every _tile_stream_loadd of a B (K/V cache) tile, the FP8 bytes are first widened to BF16 into an on-stack scratch buffer via a bit-manipulation BF16Vec32 path (fp8_bf16_e4m3_tag/e5m2_tag), then re-loaded into the tile register. This inserts a dequant + store + reload round-trip through L1 on the hottest inner loop (QK and PV phases), adding latency and defeating the benefit of _tile_stream_loadd (which was designed to stream cache tiles directly). This mirrors the historical 'FP8 conversion via software emulation on the hot path' cases.
findings.suggested_benchmark: CPU attention decode/prefill throughput with kv_cache_dtype=fp8_e4m3 vs auto (bf16) on AMX, same head_dim/block_size

The diff appends max_num_batched_tokens to cudagraph_capture_sizes when it is <= max_cudagraph_capture_size and off-stride. This is a bug FIX that closes a warmup-shape-gap: previously the max batch size (max_num_batched_tokens) that lands off the step-8/step-16 stride (e.g. 257) was not captured, so batches at that size fell back to eager. This diff narrows the Triton persistent-matmul override gate from `is_device_capability_family(100) OR is_device_capability_family(80)` to only `is_device_capability_family(80)` (Ampere/SM80). At parent, B200/Blackwell (capability family 100) was included in the branch that installs the slow Triton persistent kernels for aten::mm/addmm/matmul/linear (`mm_batch_invariant` etc.). After the change, SM100 falls into the `else` branch which only sets the cuBLAS workspace config and relies on cuBLASLt determinism — i.e. it no longer forces the slow Triton overrides. This is the INVERSE of the historical `suboptimal-kernel-gate` regression: it REMOVES an unnecessarily-installed slow path on B200, restoring the fast cuBLAS matmul path. This is a performance improvement / neutral for SM100, not a regression. The commit message ('Remove workaround code for fixed cublas issue') is consistent with this. No throughput regression introduced on B200.
findings.suggested_benchmark: batch-invariant / deterministic-mode GEMM throughput on B200 (SM100) vs Hopper (SM90) vs Ampere (SM80): compare aten::mm latency with enable_batch_invariant_mode() active

findings.claim: The `_fp16_block_size_n` computation (256 vs 128 based on shared memory size) was previously only executed inside the SM80/SM100 branch; it is now moved to run unconditionally for all CUDA platforms (`if current_platform.is_cuda()`). Since bmm/persistent-matmul kernels (bmm is overridden on all CUDA platforms via bmm_batch_invariant) read `_fp16_block_size_n` for the FP16 N-tile size, running this on Hopper/Blackwell too ensures the fp16 tile size is correctly sized rather than left at the default 256, which could otherwise cause a Triton OutOfResources fallback or a suboptimal tile. This change is corrective, not a regression.
findings.suggested_benchmark: bmm_batch_invariant fp16 kernel launch on SM90/SM100 with block_size_n=256 vs 128 to confirm no OutOfResources / tile-size regression

static rule 'cuda-stream-in-init' matched antipattern: Allocating a fresh torch.cuda.Stream() inside __init__ (or per-object construction), so N objects create N CUDA streams instead of sharing one. (matched: 'torch.cuda.Stream()')

execute_in_parallel allocates aux GEMM output tensors (kv_score, indexer_weights, indexer_kv_score) on aux streams (aux_streams[i]) inside `with torch.cuda.stream(aux_streams[i])`, then returns them to the default stream where they are consumed downstream (attention_impl passes kv_score/indexer_kv_score/indexer_weights into compressor(...) and indexer(...) which run on the default and other aux streams). The default stream joins via `done_events[i].wait()`, so ordering of the *compute* is correct, but there is NO `torch.Tensor.record_stream` on any of these aux-allocated tensors. Under the caching allocator, a tensor allocated on aux stream A whose last recorded use is A can be freed and reused as soon as A's work completes, even though the consuming default-stream (or another aux stream) work is still pending — the same class of cross-stream aliasing race that #26440 fixed by calling `record_stream` on the cross-stream input (see shared_experts.py:129). Event.wait() enforces kernel ordering but does not inform the allocator of cross-stream tensor lifetime.
findings.suggested_benchmark: DSV4 correctness + throughput under memory pressure (high concurrency, many decode steps) to surface allocator reuse races; compare against parent for output determinism.

此改动触及 suboptimal-kernel-gate（kernel-efficiency），该类问题历史上主要在 [ZeRO-Offload / CPU-Adam, Megatron-FSDP with gradient_accumulation_fusion enabled, TP1/PP1 in test config] 配置下显现；This diff modifies the gating logic that decides which MoE routing methods are eligible for the fused TRTLLM FlashInfer kernels (is_routing_method_supported / s

The dequant buffers k_buf/v_buf are moved from per-layer persistent buffers to `current_workspace_manager().get_simultaneous(...)`. get_simultaneous returns *views into one shared workspace tensor* keyed only by ubatch. These views (k_cached/v_cached) are read after the `_tq_full_dequant_kv` kernel writes them, but before that read they are used to build k_full/v_full. If any *other* workspace acquisition happens (e.g. the decode path also calls get_simultaneous for mid_o/output/lse in the mixed/continuation-decode branch that runs within the same forward for the same ubatch), the earlier k_buf/v_buf views become aliased/overwritten by the later request's allocation. Verify the continuation-prefill path does not interleave with a decode-path get_simultaneous call while k_cached/v_cached views are still live.
findings.suggested_benchmark: Long-context continuation prefill + mixed decode batch correctness/latency with TurboQuant KV cache

findings.claim: Removed `_init_turboquant_buffers` (in attention.py __init__) pre-allocated the decode intermediate buffers (_tq_mid_o_buf/_tq_output_buf/_tq_lse_buf) at construction so model.to(device) moved them to GPU *before* the memory profiler runs. The comment there explicitly warned: without pre-allocation the profiler gives all free memory to KV cache blocks and the first decode OOMs when these buffers are lazily allocated. The replacement lazily grows the WorkspaceManager buffer on first decode via get_simultaneous. If the workspace is not warmed/reserved before the memory profiler runs (or before lock_workspace()), this can either OOM at first decode or trigger a locked-workspace AssertionError. Confirm the workspace is reserved/grown during warmup so the profiler accounts for it.
findings.suggested_benchmark: Startup memory profiling + first-decode step at max_num_seqs with TurboQuant to confirm no OOM/locked-workspace assert
