findings.claim: The new FP8 ViT dynamic-scaling path (docs: '16-entry circular buffer of observed Q/K/V amax values drives per-forward scale updates') computes per-forward amax and folds it into a per-tensor scale. If the amax is read to host (e.g. .item()/.tolist() or a Python-side circular-buffer update) to compute the scale each forward, this forces a device->host sync on the hot ViT encoder attention path (called per attention block, e.g. Qwen3-VL has many blocks), serializing the pipeline. Verify the amax history buffer is updated fully on-device and the scale tensor is passed to the quant kernel without a host readback. This matches the historical FP8 delayed-scaling D2H-sync case.
findings.suggested_benchmark: benchmarks/kernels/benchmark_vit_fp8_attn.py --seq-lens 2304 4096 --profile ; compare CPU-side stalls between dynamic and static FP8 paths

findings.claim: The PR enables on-the-fly FP8 quantization of Q/K/V before the FlashInfer cuDNN attention call (mm_encoder_attn_dtype='fp8'). Per the PR's own perf table, this is a net regression for small/medium images (HD 0.87x, FullHD ~same) because the 3 extra quantization kernel launches + head_dim padding + un-padding overhead exceed the FP8 tensor-core savings; gains only appear at QHD/4K. Since this is gated behind an opt-in flag (default off) it is not a regression on the default path, but users enabling it on non-large-image workloads will see slowdowns.

此改动触及 allocator-config（memory-management），该类问题历史上主要在 [pipeline (guarded by mpu.is_pipeline_last_stage), training and evaluation loops (train_step / evaluate), tensor/pipeline parallel NCCL collectives (allreduce/allgather/reducescatter)] 配置下显现；The diff dynamically toggles PYTORCH_CUDA_ALLOC_CONF's expandable_segments setting on/off around the cumem memory pool context (via torch.cuda.memory._set_alloc

This change disables PyTorch's `expandable_segments` for the entire duration of the `use_memory_pool` context (calling `_set_allocator_settings("expandable_segments:False")` on enter and restoring on exit). The primary callers of `use_memory_pool` are cold/init paths: `load_model` (tag="weights") and KV-cache init (tag="kv_cache") in gpu_worker.py, so this runs during model load/init rather than per-request. Because expandable segments is a global allocator setting, disabling it during the memory-pool context can cause the default (non-pool) allocator to allocate non-expandable segments during this window; any host allocations made concurrently while the context is active would not benefit from expandable-segment coalescing and could fragment the CUDA caching allocator. Restore is correctly guarded with try/finally, so it is not leaked. This is a config trade-off, not a per-request hot-path regression.
findings.suggested_benchmark: Measure GPU memory fragmentation / peak reserved bytes during model load with PYTORCH_CUDA_ALLOC_CONF=expandable_segments:True and sleep mode enabled, comparing before/after this change.

findings.claim: 此改动触及 eplb-issues（parallelism-scheduling），该类问题历史上主要在 [expert-parallel / data-parallel MoE with EPLB (VLLM_USE_FLASHINFER_MOE_FP4=1, backend latency/throughput), expert parallelism (EP) with EPLB enabled; test uses tp_size=4, world_size=2/4, expert parallelism (EP) — EPLB only active with multiple EP ranks] 配置下显现；The diff modifies the EPLB (expert-parallel load balancing) replica-selection logic inside the fused_moe router's Triton kernel, changing how logical experts ma

The default rejection_sample_method changes from 'strict' to 'standard' (rejection_sampler.py / speculative.py). Previously the default 'strict' path ran the cheap _strict_rejection_sample_kernel that merely compares draft vs target sampled token ids per position, doing no full-vocab work. The new default 'standard' always routes to probabilistic_rejection_sample(), which on every spec-decode verification step launches _compute_block_stats_kernel over (num_logits, vocab_num_blocks) computing a full-vocab argmax/max/sumexp on [num_logits, V] target logits, plus _probabilistic_rejection_kernel (global LSE) and a full-vocab _resample_kernel over V. Even in the new default draft_sample_method='greedy' (HAS_DRAFT_LOGITS=False), the diff only skips the *draft*-side full-vocab loads; the target-side full-vocab scan + residual resample still run each step. So existing MRV2 spec-decode users implicitly get a heavier per-decode-step logits workload than before, despite greedy matching being equivalent to the old cheap strict comparison.
findings.suggested_benchmark: Spec-decode (EAGLE/MTP) greedy decode throughput/latency on MRV2 before vs after this commit at large vocab (e.g. 128k+): measure per-step rejection-sampling time for default settings; compare 'standard'+'greedy' path vs the removed 'strict' path.

findings.claim: In the greedy default (draft_logits is None -> draft_sample_method='greedy'), probabilistic_rejection_sample still allocates draft_local_max and draft_local_sumexp buffers of shape [num_logits, vocab_num_blocks] each decode step even though the kernels never read them when HAS_DRAFT_LOGITS=False. A 1x1x1 dummy is created for draft_logits, but the two per-block draft stat tensors are still allocated unconditionally, adding small per-step allocations on the hot path.
findings.suggested_benchmark: Micro-benchmark probabilistic_rejection_sample with HAS_DRAFT_LOGITS=False over many iterations; profile the draft_local_max/draft_local_sumexp new_empty allocations.

此改动触及 warmup-shape-gap（compilation），该类问题历史上主要在 [context-parallel (cp>1) and tensor-parallel; sequence_parallel affects shapes, sequence parallelism enabled with tensor_model_parallel_size > 1, sequence-parallel with tensor_model_parallel_size>1] 配置下显现；The change extends startup warmup to also warm the readonly multimodal processor using dummy inputs sized at max_model_len, and adjusts skip_mm_cache plumbing.

The new readonly MM-processor warmup (`_warmup_mm_processor(self._readonly_mm_processor, ...)`) is added inside `BaseRenderer.warmup`, which is invoked once during renderer startup (mirroring the existing `self.mm_processor` warmup). This is correctly init-shaped work placed on the startup path, NOT on the per-request hot path, so it does not introduce a hot-path regression. The only cost is a modest, one-time increase to server startup latency (an extra `processor.apply` on a dummy input plus a cache clear). No per-request penalty is introduced.

此改动触及 warmup-shape-gap（compilation），该类问题历史上主要在 [context-parallel (cp>1) and tensor-parallel; sequence_parallel affects shapes, sequence parallelism enabled with tensor_model_parallel_size > 1, sequence-parallel with tensor_model_parallel_size>1] 配置下显现；The diff modifies warmup logic to ensure the prefill warmup batch's per-request query length exceeds decode_query_len, so it isn't misclassified as a uniform de

This diff FIXES (does not introduce) a warmup-shape-gap. At parent, warmup.py used a fixed prompt_len=2 for the prefill warmup step, while the decode-batch classifier uses uniform_decode_query_len = decode_query_len = 1 + num_speculative_steps (model_runner.py). When num_speculative_steps >= 1, decode_query_len >= 2, so a prefill warmup batch with per-request query length 2 would be indistinguishable from (or misclassified as) a uniform-decode batch. As a result, under speculative decoding the prefill attention/kernel path would never actually be exercised during warmup, causing runtime JIT/compile stalls on the first real prefill. The change sets prompt_len = 2 + num_spec_steps so the prefill query length strictly exceeds decode_query_len, guaranteeing the prefill path is warmed up. This matches the retrieved warmup-shape-gap prior (warmup misses shapes -> runtime stalls) but is corrective, not a regression.
findings.suggested_benchmark: Enable speculative decoding (num_speculative_tokens>=1), run warmup, then time first real prefill iteration to confirm no JIT/compile stall vs. steady state.

The diff adds an unconditional torch.accelerator.empty_cache() call inside WorkspaceManager._ensure_workspace_size, on the workspace-growth path invoked from get_simultaneous(). get_simultaneous is called from attention/MoE forward paths (flash_attn.py, flashmla_sparse.py, modular_kernel.py, sparse_attn_indexer.py). Although the growth branch is normally gated by the size check (current_size < required_bytes) and the workspace is expected to be locked after warmup, before locking (and during elastic-EP unlock/resize) each grow now triggers a full CUDA caching-allocator empty_cache(). empty_cache() forces a device synchronize and cudaFree of all cached free segments, which is expensive (100s of us to ms) and destroys allocator caching for the whole process — a global side effect to reclaim one segment. If any grow path fires while unlocked in a warm/repeated setting, this is a costly synchronization-inducing call on a per-resize basis.
findings.suggested_benchmark: Microbenchmark repeated get_simultaneous() growth calls (elastic-EP unlock->resize->lock cycles) with/without empty_cache to measure per-resize latency and its effect on subsequent allocator hit rate; also measure DBO steady-state latency.

findings.claim: The empty_cache() is placed between freeing the old workspace (self._current_workspaces[ubatch_id]=None; del) and the new torch.empty allocation. This does avoid the transient old+new coexistence spike (the historical transient-realloc-spike pattern), so the intent is a genuine peak-memory fix. However, calling empty_cache() then immediately torch.empty() means the freed segment is returned to CUDA and then re-requested via cudaMalloc, which can be slower than reusing a cached segment; the reclaim benefit only materializes when the new size cannot reuse the freed block. Consider only calling empty_cache() when the freed size differs enough to leave an unusable dead segment, rather than unconditionally.
findings.suggested_benchmark: Compare cudaMalloc/cudaFree counts and allocation latency during workspace grow with unconditional vs conditional empty_cache().

findings.claim: This diff is a performance FIX for the redundant-hot-path-work pattern, not a regression. At parent, both rocm_aiter_grouped_topk (rocm_aiter_fused_moe.py) and fused_topk_bias (fused_topk_bias_router.py) called e_score_correction_bias.to(gating_output.dtype) unconditionally on every forward pass / every MoE layer. When the bias dtype differs from the gating output dtype, that .to() allocates and copies a fresh bias tensor each iteration on the router hot path. The commit hoists the cast to a one-time step in DeepseekV2MoE.__init__ (mutating the shared nn.Parameter's .data via .to(out_dtype)), and the residual cast in _aiter_ops.py is now guarded with `if correction_bias.dtype != gating_output.dtype`, making it a no-op once pre-cast. This is the inverse of the historical redundant-hot-path-work leaf: it removes per-iteration tensor work. No new regression is introduced; guards (is_rocm_aiter_moe_enabled, non-None bias) keep it correctly scoped.
findings.suggested_benchmark: Micro-benchmark DeepSeek-V2/Kimi-K2 MoE routing (biased_grouped_topk) decode-latency on ROCm before/after, with bias dtype != gate out_dtype, to confirm the per-forward .to() copy is eliminated.

This commit is designed to *remove* blocking GPU->CPU syncs on the attention-metadata-build hot path, and it does so consistently: it introduces `seq_lens_cpu_upper_bound`, a CPU tensor maintained by an optimistic host-side mirror (`self.req_states.num_computed_tokens_np[idx_mapping_np] += num_scheduled_tokens` in gpu/model_runner.py; `num_computed_tokens_np` in states.py), and replaces the prior `.cpu()` / deprecated `seq_lens_cpu` reads that forced D2H syncs. The changed sites match the retrieved historical 'blocking-d2h-sync' pattern *positively* (i.e. the fix, not the regression): mla_attention.py dropped `common_attn_metadata.compute_num_computed_tokens().cpu()`; flashmla_sparse.py dropped `common_attn_metadata.seq_lens.cpu()`; flex_attention.py, indexer.py, and split_decodes_prefills_and_extends() stopped reading the deprecated `seq_lens_cpu` property (whose getter runs `self.seq_lens.to('cpu')` → blocking sync). No new device-value reads were introduced on the hot path.
findings.suggested_benchmark: Decode-phase throughput/latency (uniform-decode batch, e.g. serving benchmark with 128+ concurrent decode requests) comparing before/after to confirm the removed D2H syncs improve TTFT/ITL and CUDA-stream bubble time.

This diff FIXES (does not introduce) a redundant gate/router GEMM on the hot MoE path. In the parent, Qwen3MoeSparseMoeBlock constructs FusedMoE with gate=self.gate, so runner.is_internal_router() returns True (gate is not None) and the router runs INSIDE the FusedMoE. Yet the parent forward also explicitly computed `router_logits, _ = self.gate(hidden_states)` and passed it in, meaning the [num_tokens, hidden_size]x[hidden_size, num_experts] gate GEMM executed TWICE per MoE layer on every prefill/decode step. The new code branches on `self.experts.is_internal_router` and passes `router_logits=hidden_states` so the gate runs exactly once (aligning qwen3_moe with the already-correct qwen3_next/deepseek_v2/step3p5/exaone_moe pattern). No new regression is introduced; the else-branch is dead but branch-once on a constant, so no per-call overhead.
findings.suggested_benchmark: Qwen3-MoE (e.g. Qwen3-30B-A3B) throughput/latency; compare tokens/s and per-layer gate GEMM count before/after — expect one fewer ReplicatedLinear gate call per MoE layer.

The new per-group FP8 pattern registration in MLAAttnQuantFusionPass.__init__ nests 5 loops: `for quant_key in [128,64]` × `for col_major in [T,F]` × `for is_e8m0 in [T,F]` × `for tma_aligned in [F,T]` × `for layer in layers`. Each combination calls self.register(MLAAttnFp8GroupQuantPattern(...)), and register() traces both pattern and replacement via pm.register_replacement/fwd_only under enable_fake_mode — an expensive compile-time operation. This produces 2×2×2×2 = 16 registered patterns *per layer*. On a DeepSeek-V3-style model with ~60 MLA layers this is up to ~960 pattern registrations (or 16 if _USE_LAYERNAME breaks early after first layer). This inflates torch.compile / startup graph-pass build time. In practice for a given deployment only one flag combination (a single col_major/e8m0/tma_aligned tuple determined by the actual quant config) will ever match, so 15/16 of these traced patterns are wasted compilation work that never fires. This mirrors the historical 'wasted-compilation-work' cases where compilation runs but yields little payoff.
findings.suggested_benchmark: Measure model compile/startup time (pass registration phase of MLAAttnQuantFusionPass) for a block-FP8 MLA model (e.g. DeepSeek-V3) before/after this change; count patterns registered and time spent in register()/fwd_only tracing.

findings.claim: The flag combinations (has_col_major_scales, is_e8m0, is_tma_aligned) are runtime/config-derived properties of the quant scheme, yet all 8 combinations are pre-registered as distinct fusion patterns rather than deriving the single applicable tuple from the model/quant config at registration time. Deriving the concrete tuple once (as is done for the static/NVFP4 patterns which register a single pattern per layer) would avoid tracing 8× redundant patterns whose replacement subgraphs differ only in constant kwargs (quant_col_major/quant_scale_ue8m0/quant_tma_aligned) that could instead be resolved during matching.
findings.suggested_benchmark: Compare number of MatcherQuantFP8 pattern traces and total register() time when registering 1 config-derived pattern per layer vs. the 8-combination cartesian product.

findings.claim: The new GptOssReasoningParser.is_reasoning_end_streaming override defeats the very optimization it exists to provide. It is called from StructuredOutputManager.should_advance every decode step per structured-output request while reasoning is ongoing. When the sequence length n exceeds `window = pattern_len + len(delta_ids)`, it calls self.is_reasoning_end(input_ids[n - window:]) — good, bounded. BUT in the branch `if n <= window: return self.is_reasoning_end(input_ids)`, and more importantly the slice `input_ids[n - window:]` copies a window-sized slice each step, and `is_reasoning_end` still scans backwards over that region. That part is bounded and fine. The real regression risk is that the base-class BaseThinkingReasoningParser.is_reasoning_end_streaming only inspects `delta_ids` (O(delta)), whereas this GptOss override always materializes `delta_ids = tuple(delta_ids)` AND slices `input_ids[n - window:]` creating per-step allocation, and — critically — when n <= window it degrades to full is_reasoning_end(input_ids) which backward-scans the entire growing token list, i.e. O(n) per step -> O(n^2) over the sequence, the exact mechanism from the historical is_reasoning_end / should_advance cases. For gpt-oss reasoning prefixes shorter than `window` (~40+delta tokens) this is negligible, but if delta_ids is large (speculative decoding accepts a big chunk, as exercised by test_gptoss_is_reasoning_end_streaming_large_delta passing the entire sequence as delta), `window` grows with delta length and the slice/scan covers the whole sequence every step.
findings.suggested_benchmark: Structured-output decode-step microbench: call is_reasoning_end_streaming repeatedly on a growing input_ids (e.g. 1k..32k tokens) with delta_ids of size 1 vs size == num_speculative_tokens, measuring per-call latency vs sequence length to confirm it stays O(window) and does not scale with n.

findings.claim: Under speculative decoding, `window = pattern_len + len(delta_ids)` scales with the accepted-chunk size. In the extreme test case delta_ids equals the entire output, making window == full length so `input_ids[n-window:]` slices/scans the whole sequence. In production spec decode delta is bounded by num_speculative_tokens (small), so per-step cost stays O(pattern_len + num_spec_tokens) and is fine — but the tuple(delta_ids) materialization plus slice allocation add constant per-step overhead compared to the base parser which only does `end_token_id in delta_ids`. This is a mild redundant-allocation cost on a per-decode-step hot path, not a true quadratic blowup in normal operation.
findings.suggested_benchmark: Profile should_advance() allocation/latency per decode step with reasoning ongoing under xgrammar + gpt-oss reasoning parser, comparing to the base BaseThinkingReasoningParser streaming path.

findings.claim: This PR couples two KV-cache-sizing changes: (1) flips VLLM_MEMORY_PROFILER_ESTIMATE_CUDAGRAPHS default False->True (envs.py), which in gpu_worker.determine_available_memory() now subtracts cudagraph_memory_estimate from available_kv_cache_memory_bytes (previously 0); and (2) raises the default gpu_memory_utilization 0.90->0.92 (config/cache.py:54, entrypoints/llm.py) to add back ~0.02*total_memory of requested_memory as compensation. The compensation is a fixed +0.02 heuristic while the amount subtracted (cudagraph_memory_estimate) is workload-dependent (the code itself computes cg_util_delta = cudagraph_memory_estimate/total_mem). For models/configs where CUDA graph memory exceeds ~2% of total GPU memory (large models, full-cudagraph mode, many capture sizes), the net effect is a SMALLER KV cache than the old 0.90 default => fewer GPU blocks => reduced max concurrent sequences / prefix-cache room / throughput. This matches the historical kv-cache-capacity mechanism where the utilization fraction determines KV pool size and mis-sizing regresses throughput.
findings.suggested_benchmark: On a large model with full CUDA graph capture, compare num_gpu_blocks / max effective KV cache GiB and steady-state throughput at defaults before vs after this commit (parent gmu=0.90 estimate off vs new gmu=0.92 estimate on).

_startup_prefetch_weights reads the ENTIRE set of weight shards (*.safetensors, *.bin) into the OS page cache from the parent APIServer process. This is a best-effort warm-up, but it introduces duplicated I/O work: the parent reads all shards, then EngineCore in the child reads the same files again. On systems where the weights do NOT fully fit in the page cache (RAM < model size, e.g. tens/hundreds of GB), the parent's read is largely evicted before the child re-reads, so total wall time gets *worse* (double read + cache thrash) rather than better. The comment even claims '16MB block / 8 threads matches in-child prefetch' but there is no coordination — both readers run concurrently once the child forks, contending for disk bandwidth.
findings.suggested_benchmark: Cold-page-cache startup of `vllm serve` for a large model (weights >> RAM) vs (weights << RAM); measure time-to-first-token / engine load phase with and without the parent prefetch.

findings.claim: The parent-side prefetch also globs and reads tokenizer/config sidecars including a broad '*tokenizer*' pattern in addition to '*.json'. This can pull duplicate/large tokenizer artifacts (e.g. tokenizer.json which can be tens of MB) redundantly. Minor, but the overlapping glob patterns (*.json + *tokenizer* + tokenizer.model) can read the same file multiple times within one shard_paths list since sorted() does not dedupe, so a file matching both patterns is read twice by read_one.
findings.suggested_benchmark: Count number of open()/read passes over sidecar files during parent prefetch; verify no file (e.g. tokenizer.json) is read more than once.

This commit reverts PR #40331, removing the CLI-entry background prewarm that pre-spawned a forkserver control process preloaded with vllm.v1.engine.async_llm (and its transitive torch import) overlapped with APIServer argparse/config resolution. With this removed, the forkserver `ensure_running()`/module-preload cost (~3-5s) is no longer overlapped and, more importantly, `forkserver` is dropped entirely from the allowed VLLM_WORKER_MULTIPROC_METHOD choices in envs.py (fork/spawn only). EngineCore process launch reverts to the un-prewarmed path. This is the exact mechanism of the retrieved forkserver-preload prior, but here it is being removed rather than added, so it reintroduces (rather than newly introduces) the pre-optimization startup latency. Because this is an explicit revert (likely due to a race/hang in the async prewarm), the startup-time cost is a deliberate trade-off, not an accidental regression.
findings.suggested_benchmark: Measure `vllm serve <model>` wall-clock time-to-ready (cold page cache) before vs after this revert, comparing forkserver-prewarm vs plain fork/spawn engine launch.

findings.claim: `_bicubic_resize_and_normalize` is now decorated with `@torch.compile(dynamic=True)` and is invoked once per image, per thumbnail, per video, and per dynamic-resolution tile during multimodal preprocessing. This preprocessing runs on CPU tensors (input comes from `torch.from_numpy`/`np.asarray`, no device transfer). `torch.compile` incurs a first-call graph-capture/compile cost and per-call guard-evaluation overhead. Because the call arguments vary in ways that change guards — `size` is a concrete `(H,W)` tuple for resized images/thumbnails vs `None` for the no-resize video branch, and `norm_mean`/`norm_std` alternate between `None` and tensors — each distinct combination can trigger a fresh recompilation even under `dynamic=True` (dynamic only relaxes tensor-dim specialization, not the None-vs-tensor / present-vs-absent argument guards). This places compile-shaped work on the request preprocessing path and can dominate latency for small/variable inputs.
findings.suggested_benchmark: Micro-benchmark image+video preprocessing latency (single small image, single thumbnail, single short video, and a batch with varying image resolutions) comparing eager vs torch.compile of _bicubic_resize_and_normalize; count torch._dynamo recompilations across a stream of varied-resolution requests.

findings.claim: In `video_to_pixel_values`, the parent code had a fast path that skipped interpolation entirely when no resize was needed (`video_tensor = video_tensor / 255.0`, plain eager op). The new code always routes through the `torch.compile`-wrapped `_bicubic_resize_and_normalize` even when `size is None`, so the common no-resize video case now pays torch.compile guard/dispatch overhead (and possibly a separate compilation for the `size=None` branch) that it previously avoided.
findings.suggested_benchmark: Benchmark video_to_pixel_values latency for a video already at target size (size=None branch) comparing eager /255.0 path vs the compiled path.

findings.claim: This diff is a kernel-fusion optimization (matches historical 'kernel-fusion': multiple eager topk/softmax/one_hot/gather/mul ops merged into a single authored Triton kernel `_gemma4_routing_kernel`). Correctness-wise it looks beneficial. However, a latency regression risk exists: the kernel is launched with a 1-D grid of `(T,)` = one program per token, and hardcoded `num_warps=1`. For Gemma4's stated context length of 250K tokens (per the test), this launches up to 250,000 single-warp programs, each doing a full `tl.sort` over BLOCK_E=128 elements. For the large-T prefill/hot path this per-token-program, single-warp design can be substantially slower than a well-batched approach and may under-occupy the GPU, so verify it does not regress large-batch prefill latency versus the previous fully-vectorized torch path.
findings.suggested_benchmark: Benchmark gemma4_fused_routing_kernel_triton vs gemma4_routing_function_torch across T in {2048, 32768, 250000}, E=128, topk=8 on CUDA; compare end-to-end MoE-layer latency, not just kernel-in-isolation.

findings.claim: static rule 'tensor-to-cpu-forces-d2h-sync' matched antipattern: Moving a GPU tensor to CPU via .to('cpu')/.cpu() on a hot path forces a blocking device→host copy and CUDA synchronization. (matched: '.cpu()')

In EplbState.step() (the per-forward-pass hot path), the async-EPLB branch now calls self._all_ranks_result_ready(eplb_model_state) whenever eplb_model_state.rebalanced is True. Previously the collective (all_reduce) buffer-readiness check was doubly gated: it only ran when pending_global_ready_check was True AND ep_buffer_ready was set (`if eplb_model_state.pending_global_ready_check: all_ranks_buffer_ready = self._all_ranks_buffer_ready(...)`). The new code drops the pending_global_ready_check gate, so during the entire rebalance window `rebalanced` stays True across many forward passes and _all_ranks_result_ready issues a cross-rank all_reduce (on cpu_group or device_group) on *every* forward step. This adds a per-iteration collective + host-device sync on the inference critical path for the whole duration a rebalance is in flight, instead of only when a buffer is actually ready to consume.
findings.suggested_benchmark: Enable async EPLB with EP>1 and measure per-step forward latency during an active rebalance window (rebalanced==True) vs baseline; count all_reduce invocations per forward pass.

findings.claim: static rule 'all-gather-object-consumed-only-on-rank0' matched antipattern: Using all_gather_object to replicate a full serialized payload onto every rank when only rank 0 consumes the result, where gather_object to rank 0 would suffice. (matched: 'all_gather_object(')

static rule 'all-gather-then-local-shard-split' matched antipattern: All-gathering a tensor across the TP group and then immediately slicing/splitting it back down to only the local rank's shard, which throws away the just-gathered data and wastes the collective plus allocations. (matched: 'all_gather(')

init_cpu_memory_env unconditionally calls numa_set_strict(1) after setting the membind/interleave mask. Strict binding causes the kernel to fail allocations (OOM/SIGKILL) rather than falling back to other nodes when the bound node(s) run low on memory. Combined with numa_set_membind for the single-node case, this can degrade robustness/latency under memory pressure vs. the previous default first-touch policy that had no strict enforcement. Verify KV-cache sizing (now derived from per-node meminfo via get_memory_node_info) leaves headroom so strict binding does not trigger allocation failures.
findings.suggested_benchmark: CPU multi-NUMA serving throughput/latency under near-full memory node utilization with VLLM_CPU_KVCACHE_SPACE sized close to per-node MemTotal

findings.claim: The new init_cpu_memory_env in csrc/cpu/utils.cpp reintroduces the multi-NUMA memory-binding path. It correctly handles the historical pitfall: when node_ids.size() > 1 it uses numa_set_interleave_mask (interleave policy across nodes) and only uses numa_set_membind for a single node, rather than assuming a single NUMA node. This matches the fixed behavior of the historical cpu-affinity-numa cases (no regression introduced by the binding-policy choice itself).

In megatron_rl_inference_mode, the inference-side cuda_graph_scope was changed from a hardcoded empty list `[]` (which for local cuda_graph_impl meant full-layer capture per the previous comment) to `args.cuda_graph_scope`. This makes inference CUDA-graph capture scope entirely user-arg driven. If args.cuda_graph_scope does not include the appropriate inference scope (e.g. CudaGraphScope.full_iteration_inference, referenced at line ~2044), the inference decode path may no longer capture the full-layer graph it previously captured with `[]`, causing loss of CUDA-graph coverage during decode (per-op launch overhead / GPU bubbles). This matches the historical cudagraph-enablement pattern where full-iteration/full-layer inference capture is the intended optimization but can be silently not-turned-on. Verify args.cuda_graph_scope default contains the inference scope so decode graphs are still captured.
findings.suggested_benchmark: RL rollout decode step latency / GPU utilization with default args.cuda_graph_scope, comparing empty-list (prior) vs args-driven scope

此改动触及 cudagraph-incompatibility（compilation），该类问题历史上主要在 [TP-aware (cuda_graph_step_size rounded to TP size); dynamic inference backend, tensor-parallel (uses model_comm_pgs.tp); full-iteration CUDA graph, virtual pipeline parallelism (VPP/interleaved) + CUDA graphs (enable_cuda_graph, cuda_graph_scope != full_iteration)] 配置下显现；The diff fixes CUDA graph capture for the piecewise/wildcard LoRA case by adding has_lora to the BatchDescriptor. This affects the batch descriptor keying used

This diff is the FIX for the historical 'suboptimal-kernel-gate' / LoRA-graph-specialization pattern, not a regression. In the parent code, during PIECEWISE CUDA-graph capture the BatchDescriptor was built as BatchDescriptor(num_tokens=num_tokens) with has_lora defaulting to False. Since dispatch/replay keys graphs on the full BatchDescriptor (which now carries has_lora), a PIECEWISE capture done in a LoRA-enabled run would have been captured under the has_lora=False key. At runtime, a LoRA batch (has_lora=True) would fail to match this captured key and fall through to eager execution (or capture a duplicate/mismatched graph), losing CUDA-graph acceleration on the piecewise/LoRA decode path. Adding has_lora=has_lora makes the capture-time descriptor match the runtime dispatch key, restoring correct graph replay. No new performance regression is introduced by this change; it removes a graph-key mismatch that was causing missed CUDA-graph replays.
findings.suggested_benchmark: P/W (piecewise) CUDA-graph decode latency with LoRA adapters active: verify captured graphs are actually replayed (num_cudagraph_replayed > 0) rather than falling back to eager for has_lora=True batches.

When VLLM_ROCM_USE_AITER is enabled, this PR makes vLLM's CudaCommunicator swap its native custom-allreduce backend (ca_comm / CustomAllreduce) for AITER's CustomAllreduce by default. In __init__, the guard `if use_custom_allreduce and self.aiter_ar_comm is None and self.world_size > 1:` means once `self.aiter_ar_comm` is constructed, vLLM's own ca_comm is left None; in all_reduce() the new aiter_ar_comm branch is inserted before the ca_comm branch. This is a wholesale backend swap of the hot TP allreduce path: every small-message TP allreduce that previously used vLLM's tuned one/two-shot IPC custom allreduce now goes to AITER's kernel. If AITER's CustomAllreduce has different size gates or per-message latency characteristics than vLLM's (whose max sizes are device/world-size tuned via CUSTOM_ALL_REDUCE_MAX_SIZES), latency for the common decode-time small allreduce could regress relative to the prior default.
findings.suggested_benchmark: ROCm TP=2/4/8 decode-latency (small allreduce sizes) with VLLM_ROCM_USE_AITER=1, comparing VLLM_ROCM_USE_AITER_CUSTOM_AR=1 (new default) vs =0 (prior vLLM ca_comm path).

findings.claim: The new env VLLM_ROCM_USE_AITER_CUSTOM_AR defaults to True (envs.py) and is_custom_all_reduce_enabled() = _AITER_ENABLED and _CUSTOM_ALL_REDUCE_ENABLED. This static default silently opts every AITER-enabled ROCm deployment into the AITER custom-allreduce backend without an explicit choice. Because the default flips the allreduce backend for existing AITER users, any latency delta between AITER and vLLM's native custom allreduce is applied by default rather than opt-in. This is the 'static default chooses a path' mechanism; the fact that it's gated behind VLLM_ROCM_USE_AITER (default False) limits blast radius but AITER is commonly turned on in ROCm perf configs.
findings.suggested_benchmark: A/B end-to-end throughput/latency on MI300 with AITER enabled, toggling VLLM_ROCM_USE_AITER_CUSTOM_AR default True vs False.

findings.claim: Regression risk in the NEW gating: the W4A4 branch now unconditionally calls select_mxfp4_moe_backend(moe, activation_key=kMxfp4Dynamic), and select_mxfp4_moe_backend no longer silently returns NONE for ROCm/CUDA — it raises NotImplementedError when no native backend matches (unless --moe-backend emulation is passed). Previously the parent's `emulate` logic transparently fell back to Mxfp4MoeBackend.EMULATION when supports_mx()/AITER were unavailable. With AITER disabled (VLLM_ROCM_USE_AITER unset) and moe_backend='auto', AiterExperts.is_supported_config returns unsupported, EMULATION is now excluded from _get_priority_backends_for_gpt_oss(), and _filter_by_activation only re-admits EMULATION when a requested_activation_key is set — so an auto W4A4 config on ROCm will hard-fail instead of degrading to the emulation path it used before. This is a functional gate change: the previously-working (if slow) emulation fallback is no longer auto-selected, converting a slow-path into a startup error unless the user explicitly opts in. Verify the model_config path that reaches this branch and whether auto W4A4 was previously reachable via emulation.
findings.suggested_benchmark: On ROCm with AITER disabled and a w_mxfp4_a_mxfp4 checkpoint using default --moe-backend auto, confirm the server still starts (falls back to emulation) rather than raising NotImplementedError; compare against parent behavior.

findings.claim: This PR is the intended fix for the historical suboptimal-kernel-gate case: the parent QuarkOCP_MX_MoEMethod only ran select_mxfp4_moe_backend() for the w_mxfp4 (W4A16) and w_mxfp4_a_fp8 (W4A8) schemes, so w_mxfp4_a_mxfp4 (W4A4) had no native-backend branch. The parent's `emulate` gate (self.emulate = (not supports_mx() or scheme not in _AITER_NATIVE_OCP_MX_SCHEMES) and (mxfp4_backend is NONE or not use_rocm_aiter_moe)) plus the hand-rolled AITER path in process_weights_after_loading did wire up a native AITER shuffle path for W4A4, but not via the oracle/modular kernel. This diff adds a proper `elif self.ocp_mx_scheme == 'w_mxfp4_a_mxfp4'` branch calling select_mxfp4_moe_backend(moe, activation_key=kMxfp4Dynamic) and registers AITER_MXFP4_MXFP4 (CK kernel) as a native backend. This moves W4A4 onto the fast CK kernel rather than gating into emulation — a de-gating (improvement) matching the historical pattern's remediation, not a regression.
findings.suggested_benchmark: On MI355x/GFX950 with a w_mxfp4_a_mxfp4 (W4A4) Quark MoE model (e.g. amd/Qwen3.5-35B-A3B-MXFP4), compare decode/prefill MoE throughput with VLLM_ROCM_USE_AITER=1 (AITER_MXFP4_MXFP4 CK) vs --moe-backend emulation to confirm the native path is selected and faster.

findings.claim: Gemma4Config.verify_and_update_config now forces flash_attn_version=4 for all layers whenever is_fa_version_supported(4) and max_head_dim<=512. But get_flash_attn_version() (fa_utils.py) will still silently downgrade FA4 to FA2 on Blackwell (device major>=10) when head_size>128 and !=192 (TMEM capacity limits). For Gemma4 heterogeneous head dims where global_head_dim is in (256,512], the config-level gate says 'FA4 supported' (max_head_dim<=512) and pins flash_attn_version=4, yet the runtime kernel selector rejects FA4 for that head size and falls back to FA2 — reintroducing exactly the mixed/slow-path behavior the original TRITON_ATTN forcing was designed to avoid. The upper bound 512 in config is inconsistent with the effective FA4 head_size ceiling (128, plus 192) enforced in get_flash_attn_version, so the fast/uniform kernel path may not actually be taken.
findings.suggested_benchmark: Gemma4 variant with global_head_dim in (256,512] on SM100 (Blackwell): compare decode/prefill throughput and verify the actual fa_version selected at runtime vs the config-pinned value of 4.

findings.claim: gpu_model_runner now computes mm_prefix bidirectional ranges (req_doc_ranges) unconditionally for every step when self.is_mm_prefix_lm is True, iterating over all req_ids and their mm_features and building a per-request dict, moved to before attention-metadata build. This runs on the hot per-step path for PrefixLM multimodal models even for decode-only / text-only batches (compute_mm_prefix_range_tensor short-circuits to None only after the dict is fully built and an async H2D is set up). The prior placement also iterated, so this is not a new loop, but the range tensor is now computed inside each backend builder (flash_attn + triton) rather than once-and-shared via the removed _set_mm_prefix_range_for_metadata helper, so multi-backend/multi-group configs may recompute/H2D-transfer the same ranges more than once per step.
findings.suggested_benchmark: PrefixLM multimodal model with multiple attention groups; count H2D transfers of mm_prefix_range_tensor per decode step before/after this commit.

findings.claim: MarlinFP8ScaledMMLinearKernel is inserted into _POSSIBLE_FP8_BLOCK_KERNELS[CUDA] at priority position 4 (after FlashInfer/DeepGemm/Cutlass, before TritonFp8BlockScaledMMKernel). Because MarlinFP8ScaledMMLinearKernel.can_implement() unconditionally returns (True, None) and is_supported() returns True for GPUs with 7.5 <= compute_capability < 89 (e.g. Ampere A100/A10 cc80/86, Turing T4 cc75), Marlin will now be selected for FP8 block-scaled matmuls on those GPUs whenever the three higher-priority kernels are unavailable, shadowing the TritonFp8BlockScaledMMKernel that previously served this decode/prefill path. Marlin is a weight-only FP8 kernel explicitly designed 'for GPUs that lack FP8 hardware support'; where a native block-scaled path (Cutlass/Triton) exists it can be slower for FP8 block-scaled GEMM, causing a throughput regression on the hot linear-layer path. The is_supported() guard requiring VLLM_TEST_FORCE_FP8_MARLIN on cc>=89 mitigates the risk on Hopper/Ada, but Ampere-class GPUs are unguarded.
findings.suggested_benchmark: FP8 block-scaled (group_shape=(1,128)) linear-layer GEMM throughput on Ampere (A100/A10) with and without VLLM_DISABLED_KERNELS=MarlinFP8ScaledMMLinearKernel to compare Marlin vs Triton block-scaled selection.

findings.claim: Every ZeRO-3 allgather call now unconditionally imports the mori module and calls `_mori.allgather_into_tensor(...)` at the top of `TorchBackend.all_gather_into_tensor`, even when the SDMA fast-path is disabled (the default: DS_SDMA_ALLGATHER unset, or non-AMD/non-ROCm, or mori not installed). `allgather_into_tensor` -> `supports()` re-checks `_handle is None`, group identity, `input_tensor.numel() > _max_numel`, and dtype-map membership on EVERY collective. On the common baseline path (SDMA off), `_handle` is None so `supports()` returns False on the first check; the added cost is a per-call `from . import mori` (module attribute lookup) plus a function call + several branches. `all_gather_into_tensor` is the ZeRO-3 parameter-prefetch hot path invoked once per parameter bucket per fwd/bwd for every layer, so this is fixed per-collective CPU overhead injected into the critical prefetch-queuing loop that the design explicitly relies on keeping the CPU free (see _SdmaWork docstring). While small, it is paid unconditionally by ALL users regardless of hardware.
findings.suggested_benchmark: Micro-benchmark all_gather_into_tensor CPU dispatch overhead per call on a non-AMD/CUDA setup (SDMA disabled): compare parent vs PR for N=100k tiny allgathers, measure added per-call Python overhead from the unconditional mori import + supports() checks.

The custom-recipe early-exit fix in init_fp8_metadata (base.py: `if recipe.custom() and isinstance(recipe_state, CustomRecipeState): if recipe_state.recipe is recipe: return`) *restores* a caching guard that was missing. This is a performance improvement, not a regression: prior to the change, every forward with a custom recipe fell through the early-return and rebuilt all quantizers unnecessarily on each call. No regression introduced here; the identity check narrows re-init to only when the recipe object actually changed. Note the only residual risk: if a custom recipe object is mutated in-place while keeping the same identity, quantizers would go stale — but that is a correctness concern, not perf.

get_ub_is_fp8 is decorated with @torch.compiler.assume_constant_result and calls get_ub(...).is_fp8_ubuf() on the FP8 forward hot path (Linear/LayerNormLinear/LayerNormMLP forward, guarded by ub_overlap_rs_fprop/rs_dgrad/rs). Under eager (non-compiled) execution assume_constant_result adds no caching and the pybind11 is_fp8_ubuf() call still runs every forward — identical cost to the parent's get_ub(...).is_fp8_ubuf(). No new per-op overhead vs parent; under torch.compile it removes graph breaks (a win). Not a regression.

In DotProductAttention.forward, the parent code auto-detected pad_between_seqs for THD via torch.equal(cu_seqlens_q_padded[:-1], cu_seqlens_q[:-1]), which reads device tensors and forces a GPU->CPU sync every forward call. The diff replaces this with a sync-free identity/None check (`cu_seqlens_q_padded is cu_seqlens_q` and `is not None`). This is an IMPROVEMENT that removes the d2h sync -- but note it also RELAXES correctness: previously pad_between_seqs was only True when padded values actually differed; now it becomes True whenever any padded cu_seqlens is supplied (unless it is the identical object). If callers pass distinct-but-equal padded tensors, pad_between_seqs will now be True, potentially excluding FlashAttention v2/v4 and selecting a slower backend. Verify hot-path callers set the identical object when no inter-seq padding exists (the test runner in run_attention_with_cp.py was updated to do so, suggesting other callers may not).
findings.suggested_benchmark: Compare backend selection + per-step latency for THD training with distinct-but-equal cu_seqlens_padded vs identical-object; verify FlashAttention still selected and no d2h sync in forward.

NVFP4 SReLU grouped-MLP backward re-quantization overhead. The new NVFP4 branch in the SReLU-recompute path (backward_grouped_mlp.py, ~line 711-730) recomputes FC2 input in BF16 and then does a *full extra group-quantize* (`_group_quantize_for_grouped_mlp(fc2_x_bf16, ...)`) to produce columnwise FP4 data/scales/amax for the wgrad GEMM. The MXFP8 path (else-branch) does NOT do this: it reuses the `sfd_col_d_srelu_tensor` already returned by the fused dSReLU kernel and builds the GroupedTensor by viewing/permuting existing buffers (no re-quantize). So the NVFP4 path introduces an additional grouped-quantize kernel launch + full-tensor read/write over the recomputed FC2 activation in the backward hot path, negating the point of the fused dSReLU recompute (which was to avoid re-materializing/re-quantizing). This matches the historical 'kernel-fusion' pattern where a separately-run quantization step should be folded into the fused kernel rather than run as a standalone pass. Confirm whether cuDNN's grouped dSReLU FP4 wrapper can emit the columnwise FP4 scales directly (as MXFP8 does) to avoid the extra pass.
findings.suggested_benchmark: Backward pass of NVFP4 grouped MLP with ScaledSReLU (fc2 weight_requires_grad=True, recompute_input_from_dsrelu=True), compare step time vs MXFP8 SReLU path at same token/expert counts to isolate the extra group-quantize cost.

In the NVFP4 backward path, after the FC2 dgrad kernel produces `d_row_tensor`, the code does `fc1_dy_bf16 = ...view(...).contiguous()` and then feeds it into `_group_quantize_for_grouped_mlp`, which re-quantizes the bf16 grad-output from scratch (single-tensor `tex.quantize` + optional `swizzle_scales_for_gemm_`). The COLWISE-only scaling path is silently excluded from the fast specialized cast-only kernel. In the new gate, `scaling_type_has_specialized_support` is only true for ROWWISE (with cols%128==0 and grid-fit) or BIDIMENSIONAL; there is no COLWISE clause, so a pure COLWISE MXFP8 cast falls through to the generic TMA kernel. Previously it used a custom bulk_allocate extension that produce

The replacement `torch.empty(num_gemms, *shape, ...)` performs a single fused allocation just like `bulk_allocate`, and the Python-side list comprehension `[wgrad_packed[i] for i in range(num_gemms)]` creates num_gemms cheap views — this is actually LOWER CPU overhead than the pybind11 round-trip into the C++ `bulk_allocate` (which builds vectors of shapes/dtypes/alignments and calls from_blob per tensor). So the change is not expected to regress CPU-side allocation overhead; the only latent risk is the alignment/layout change noted separately. No redundant per-iteration work is introduced.

(matched: 'torch.cuda.synchronize()')

The new naive_topk_and_mask_smallk<K> replaces the baseline naive_topk_and_mask inner-element masking (which read the shared-memory topk_indices[] to skip already-selected experts) with a per-lane register array selected[K] plus an inner '#pragma unroll for j<k { masked |= (selected[j]==i) }' inside the strided element loop. Because this masking runs on EVERY element for EVERY of the K outer iterations, it is O(K^2 * E/32) comparison work per warp — the same asymptotic cost as the generic path, so the specialization does NOT reduce total FLOPs for the topk selection itself; the only genuine win is avoiding shared-memory reads for the mask. For the group_topk path each call uses topk/group_topk (small) but is invoked num_groups+1 times per token, so the register array and unrolled masking must not spill. Verify with a benchmark that the smallk path is actually faster than baseline for the common topk<=8 configs (the PR's own perf test uses topk=8) rather than merely equal or slower due to register pressure from selected[K].
findings.suggested_benchmark: TE_RUN_PERF_TESTS=1 test_fused_topk_router_perf_against_torch with topk in {1,2,4,8}; compare fused_ms against parent build to confirm the smallk specialization is a real speedup and not a neutral/negative change.

The default `Quantizer.make_empty` in quantized_tensor.py was rewritten to route every allocation through the C++ pybind entry `tex.create_empty_quantized_tensor`, whose first action is `convert_quantizer(quantizer)` (common.cpp). `convert_quantizer` calls `init_extension()` and then does a *linear scan* over `detail::custom_types_converters`, invoking a Python type-check callback (`check_quantizer_type(quantizer.ptr())`) for each registered quantizer type on every single call. The subclass-specific pure-Python `make_empty` implementations that previously allocated the buffers directly (Float8Quantizer, Float8BlockQuantizer, MXFP8Quantizer, NVFP4Quantizer) were deleted, so all of them now pay this per-call quantizer-conversion + type-scan overhead. `make_empty` is invoked on hot paths — e.g. distributed.py all-gather/reduce-scatter output allocation (lines ~1030/1141/1399/1573) and fused_adam.py optimizer state allocation (line ~427) — so this converts what was direct ATen allocation into a repeated Python↔C++ round trip with a per-type-registry scan each iteration.
findings.suggested_benchmark: Microbenchmark quantizer.make_empty(shape) in a tight loop (e.g. 10k iters) for Float8Quantizer/MXFP8Quantizer, comparing parent (pure-Python allocation) vs this commit (C++ create_empty_quantized_tensor path), and a distributed all-gather step that calls make_empty per iteration.

This diff is a fix (not a regression) for a suboptimal-kernel-gate bug in the FA3-vs-FA4 selection on SM90. The parent code at line 476 gated the 'disable FA4 to prefer FA3' branch on `use_flash_attention_4 and use_flash_attention_3`, where `use_flash_attention_3` is only a policy flag (default 1 / NVTE_FLASH_ATTN). It did NOT check `FlashAttentionUtils.v3_is_installed`. Consequently, on SM90 when FA3 was NOT actually installed but the policy flag was still True, the branch disabled FA4 (`use_flash_attention_4 = False`) even though no FA3 kernel exists, forcing a fallback to the slower FA2/cuDNN path — the fast FA4 kernel existed but the gating logic rejected it. The new code adds `and FlashAttentionUtils.v3_is_installed and ... FlashAttentionUtils.v4_is_installed`, so FA4 is only disabled when FA3 is genuinely available to take over. This RESTORES the fast path and does not introduce a regression.
findings.suggested_benchmark: On SM90 with FA4 installed but FA3 not installed, run get_attention_backend and assert use_flash_attention_4 remains selected; microbenchmark DPA fwd/bwd throughput vs the FA2/cuDNN fallback that the parent commit would have forced.

The new variable-shape grouped swizzle kernel (grouped_swizzle_scaling_variable_shape_kernel) recomputes the entire per-tensor block-partition on the device hot path. In the persistent-grid loop, for EVERY value of linear_block_id a block processes, it performs a full O(num_tensors) linear scan over m_array/k_array (device-memory reads) recomputing round_up_to_multiple, DIVUP, num_tiles_m/k, vec_load_size, grid_dim_x/y and a running block/scale base to discover tensor_id. Since each block iterates linear_block_id += gridDim.x, total device work per block is O(iterations * num_tensors) and the prefix-sum-style offsets are re-derived from scratch on every scheduler step instead of being precomputed once (e.g. a per-tensor block-offset / scale-offset prefix array built once). For large group counts this scan dominates the actual swizzle work.
findings.suggested_benchmark: Grouped MXFP8 swizzle with variable shapes over a large number of tensors (e.g. num_tensors=64/128/256, small per-tensor m,k) vs the uniform-shape path; measure kernel time scaling with num_tensors.

此改动触及 activation-recompute（memory-footprint），该类问题历史上主要在 [MoE (shared experts, TE grouped MLP), optionally MLA, context parallelism (CP>1) for CP path; recompute_granularity=full, recompute_method=uniform, recompute_num_layers=1 for memory path; also relevant to MoE/EP and TP, MoE / expert parallelism (GroupedMLP, TEGroupedMLP); also applies to MLA (mla_up_proj) and layernorms] 配置下显现；The diff checkpoints the rowwise quantized tensors (grouped_gemm_x / grouped_gemm_kernel) in the grouped-dense forward rule so that the te_grouped_quantize cust

In `_grouped_dense_fwd_rule`, the diff wraps the *forward-GEMM* inputs `grouped_gemm_x` (LHS rowwise) and `grouped_gemm_kernel` (RHS rowwise) with `.checkpoint(quantizer_set.x/.kernel)`, which under the hood calls `jax.ad_checkpoint.checkpoint_name(self, name=quantizer.checkpoint_name)`. This is the same mechanism as the historical 'activation-recompute' cases (`.checkpoint(quantizer_set.x/.kernel)` tagging low-precision quantized tensors). The intent (per the diff comment) is precisely a memory/remat trade: tag the rowwise-quantized tensors so a name-based remat save-policy makes them dead in the backward-scan remat block and lets the grouped-quantize FFI be DCE'd. If a user's remat `save_policy` is configured to *save* these checkpoint names instead of dropping them, the effect inverts: the rowwise low-precision tensors (grouped_gemm_x ~ (M,K), grouped_gemm_kernel ~ (G,N,K)) become saved residuals in addition to the colwise ctx tensors, increasing peak activation memory for grouped/MoE dense. Behavior is entirely policy-dependent and only active when `quantizer.checkpoint_name is not None` (opt-in); with the default `checkpoint_name=None` it is a strict no-op, so no regression on the default path.
findings.suggested_benchmark: Grouped/MoE dense (grouped_dense) fwd+bwd under jax.checkpoint with a name-based save_policy that includes the quantization checkpoint_name; measure peak HBM and step time vs parent, both with checkpoint_name set and unset.

This diff is the intended memory-leak FIX for the historical 'memory-leak-unpruned-state' pattern (Issue #2681/#2717: FP8 weight workspaces and columnwise/transpose caches accumulating across FSDP2 layers). Under is_fsdp2 it (a) disables the per-module _fp8_workspaces cache (cache_name -> None), (b) skips columnwise/transpose creation in forward, (c) does NOT save the FP8 workspace for non-quantized weights (wt_save=None), and (d) calls clear_columnwise_cache after the dgrad GEMM. The mechanism directly targets the unbounded columnwise-derived buffers that FSDP2 cannot deallocate. The diff does not introduce a new leak; it removes one.
findings.suggested_benchmark: run_fsdp2_mem_leak.py: assert no per-layer FP8/columnwise memory accumulation across N transformer layers over multiple iterations (fp8_init + FSDP2)

findings.claim: The new `is_fsdp2` property in base.py caches its result in `self._is_fsdp2` on first access, so `_get_module_fsdp_state` (which can be moderately expensive and raises/catches a RuntimeError when not under FSDP2) runs only once per module. Subsequent hot-path calls (forward: cache_name selection, and the tuple passed into the autograd Function; each iteration) are just attribute reads. This is correctly a one-time cost — NOT a per-iteration regression. Flagging only as a note: the cache is not invalidated if a module is wrapped with fully_shard AFTER first forward, but that is not a normal usage pattern.

findings.claim: split_overlap_rs 中新增的排序约束会串行化各 split GEMM，削弱 comm/GEMM 重叠。原本循环中所有 GEMM chunk 都提交到轮转的 _stream_compute[stream_id] 并只在起点等待 _start_compute，可以彼此并发/与 P2P send-recv 重叠；本次 diff 在 i>=1 时通过 cudaEventRecord(_start_compute, _stream_compute[(i-2)%N]) + cudaStreamWaitEvent 让第 i 个 GEMM 等待第 (i-2) 个 GEMM 完成，等于把 compute 流强制串成两两一组的链式依赖。这在启用 CUDA graph 时是为了修复错误排序的正确性需求，但在非 CG 或流水线场景下会引入额外的跨流同步等待，可能降低 GPU 利用率、增大 bubble。

findings.claim: In get_attention_backend's FP8 execution-type filter, the diff re-indents `use_flash_attention_2 = False` so it now sits INSIDE the `if use_flash_attention_2 and FlashAttentionUtils.is_installed:` guard. Parent (utils.py ~L472-473) unconditionally set `use_flash_attention_2 = False` under `if fp8 and fp8_meta['recipe'].fp8_dpa:`. After the change, FA2 is only disabled for FP8 attention when FA2 is *installed and already-enabled*; the semantics of the guard predicate now gate a state-clearing side effect. While this specific case is mostly benign (if FA2 isn't installed it won't run anyway), it is the same mechanism the historical [boolean-guard-misfire] priors warn about: a runtime predicate (here `FlashAttentionUtils.is_installed`) wrapping a fast/slow-path state assignment. Recommend keeping the `= False` assignment unconditional (dedented) as in parent to avoid a latent misfire where the FP8 fast-path exclusion depends on install-state.
findings.suggested_benchmark: Run get_attention_backend with fp8=True, fp8_meta recipe fp8_dpa=True across FA2-installed / not-installed to confirm backend selection is unchanged vs parent.

This diff LOWERS the declared CUDA-graph capability of TritonMLA from AttentionCGSupport.UNIFORM_BATCH (value 2) to UNIFORM_SINGLE_TOKEN_DECODE (value 1). Per the enum semantics (ALWAYS>UNIFORM_BATCH>UNIFORM_SINGLE_TOKEN_DECODE>NEVER) and cuda_graphs.md, UNIFORM_BATCH allows full-CUDA-graph capture/replay for uniform batches with query_len > 1 (e.g. spec-decode 'decodes' of 1+num_speculative_tokens), whereas UNIFORM_SINGLE_TOKEN_DECODE only permits full CUDA graphs for pure query_len==1 decode batches. As a result, on TritonMLA (used on ROCm) speculative-decode / uniform multi-token-decode batches will no longer be dispatched to full CUDA graphs and will fall back to piecewise CUDA graphs or eager attention, reintroducing per-step CPU kernel-launch overhead on the decode hot path. This is the inverse of the historical 'cudagraph-enablement' cases (CutlassMLA/Linear-attention) where raising the ClassVar turned capture ON to remove decode-step launch overhead — here the change turns a portion of it OFF. If TritonMLA on ROCm genuinely cannot safely capture query_len>1 uniform batches (a correctness constraint), this is a justified correctness-forces-slow-path fix; but if UNIFORM_BATCH was safe, this is an unnecessary capability downgrade with a spec-decode throughput cost. The PR title labels it a Bugfix, suggesting the former, but the perf impact on spec-decode paths should be confirmed/benchmarked.
findings.suggested_benchmark: Run DeepSeek/MLA model with speculative decoding (num_speculative_tokens>0) on ROCm with cudagraph_mode=FULL_AND_PIECEWISE; compare decode throughput / TPOT before vs after this change to measure whether uniform multi-token decode batches lost full-CUDA-graph capture.

findings.claim: In fused_topk_with_score_function_forward_kernel the TopkFunc (Naive vs Radix) is selected ONCE at launch time based solely on the outer `topk`, and is then applied uniformly to ALL topk_and_mask<TopkFunc> calls inside the kernel. In the group-topk branch, the actual effective K values are much smaller than the outer topk: the per-group selection uses K = topk/group_topk and the group-selection uses K = group_topk, both operating over small data sizes (group_size = num_experts/num_groups and num_groups respectively). Because radix_topk_and_mask always executes a fixed 8 radix passes + 2 gather passes (9-10 x O(E)) INDEPENDENT of K, forcing Radix for these tiny-K/small-E sub-selections (e.g. outer topk=16, group_topk=4 -> per-group K=4, num_groups=4 -> group-select K=4 over only 4 elements) is strictly slower than naive_topk_and_mask, whose cost for tiny K is ~O(K*E) with a very small constant. The cheap kernel (naive) exists but the gate rejects it for these sub-calls whenever the outer topk crosses the >=16 threshold.
findings.suggested_benchmark: test_fused_router.py with group_topk=4, num_groups=4, topk in {16,32}, num_experts in {128,1024}; compare fwd latency vs a variant that picks Naive for the group/per-group sub-selections regardless of outer topk

findings.claim: In the new AITER `hc_head` path (vllm/_aiter_ops.py, `rocm_aiter_ops.hc_head`), every call allocates and zero-fills padded scratch tensors — `full_fn = torch.zeros(hc_mult3, hc_mult*hidden_size, fp32)` (this is `hc_mult*(2+hc_mult)` rows, i.e. up to 6x larger than the `hc_mult`-row `fn` actually provided), plus `full_base = torch.zeros(hc_mult3)` and `full_scale = torch.zeros(3)` — then copies `fn`/`hc_base`/`hc_scale` into the leading slice. This is wasted allocation + zero-fill + copy work on what is a correctly-sized (small) input, executed on the hc_head hot path (compute_logits, called for MTP/draft as well as the main head). The `full_fn` allocation in particular is a large fp32 buffer (hc_mult3 * hc_mult * hidden_size) zeroed each call just to pad rows the AITER mhc_pre kernel then discards. This matches the redundant-buffer-copy prior. Preferably build the padded params once at weight-load / cache them, or add an AITER entrypoint that accepts the unpadded hc_head fn directly.
findings.suggested_benchmark: Microbenchmark rocm_aiter_ops.hc_head over repeated calls at typical hidden_size/hc_mult to measure per-call allocation+zero+copy overhead vs a cached-padded-params variant.

The diff REMOVES blocking D2H syncs on the all-TRTLLM path rather than introducing them. Previously `needs_seq_lens_cpu`/`needs_paged_kv_indices` were gated by `is_only_trtllm_decode` (num_prefills==0 AND decode_use_trtllm); the new gate uses `not all_uses_trtllm`, which is broader — it now also skips `seq_lens_cpu`/`paged_kv_indices` when BOTH prefill and decode use TRTLLM (num_prefills>0). Since TRTLLM paths consume GPU tensors directly, this avoids extra CPU-side seq_lens/paged_kv_indices materialization. Additionally, `cum_seq_lens_kv`/`paged_kv_indptr_prefill_gpu` is now computed via torch.cumsum on GPU instead of relying on a host-computed path, further reducing CPU sync. This is a perf IMPROVEMENT consistent with the blocking-d2h-sync history, not a regression.

findings.claim: This diff does NOT introduce a performance regression; it removes a redundant collective. `output_is_reduced()` is consumed by MoERunner._fused_output_is_reduced, which gates two paths: (1) `_maybe_reduce_final_output` performs a `tensor_model_parallel_all_reduce` on the combined MoE output only when NOT `_fused_output_is_reduced`; (2) `_maybe_reduce_shared_expert_output` reduces the shared-expert output separately when it IS reduced. The FlashInfer NVLink combine kernels (moe_alltoall.combine in one_sided, MnnvlMoe.mnnvl_moe_alltoallv_combine in two_sided) already reduce/combine expert outputs across EP ranks. With the parent value `False`, the runner issued an ADDITIONAL tensor_model_parallel_all_reduce over the already-reduced output — a double-reduce (both wrong numerically and an extra all-reduce collective on the hot path). Flipping to `True` eliminates that superfluous collective, so this is a perf improvement, not a regression.

In the new SP+modular-kernel branch of RoutedExpertsCapturer.capture(), the code issues get_tp_group().all_gather(topk_ids, dim=0) on the MoE routing hot path. capture() is called once per MoE layer per forward step (hook bound per FusedMoE layer). This introduces a per-layer, per-step collective communication (blocking NCCL all-gather + implicit device sync from get_forward_context state) that did NOT exist on this path before the refactor. Under SP the number of MoE layers times steps means N_layers all-gathers per decode step just to reconstruct routing indices for a debug/observability feature, adding latency and TP-group synchronization pressure on every forward. This is only gated by enable_return_routed_experts, but when enabled it is pure per-iteration overhead added to the critical path.
findings.suggested_benchmark: MoE model (e.g. DeepSeek/Qwen3-MoE) with enable_return_routed_experts + SP + TP>1: measure per-step decode latency vs disabled, isolating the per-layer all_gather cost.

findings.claim: The production change in AttnFuncWithCPAndKVAllGather.forward adds `torch.cuda.current_stream().wait_stream(flash_attn_streams[i-1])` before the `torch.maximum(max_logit, max_logit_per_step[i-1])` read (context_parallel.py:~3283). This is a correctness fix for a real read-after-write cross-stream race (stale max_logit) that the pool design in this PR exposed, and is guarded to be a no-op for i=1 (streams identical). The added wait only fires when actually reading from cp_stream (i=2), so any serialization cost is intrinsic to correctness — it introduces a small forced synchronization point that slightly reduces overlap between the default stream and cp_stream on the all_gather CP forward path, but this is required and only occurs once per forward. Not a regression to flag against a fast path; only note it as a minor added sync. This is only reachable when return_max_logit is true (a niche config), so hot-path exposure is limited.
findings.suggested_benchmark: CP all_gather forward with return_max_logit=True: measure per-forward wall time before/after the wait_stream insertion to confirm the added sync does not materially reduce compute/comm overlap.

findings.claim: static rule 'tensor-from-pylist-on-device' matched antipattern: Constructing a tensor from a Python list literal with a non-CPU device forces a CPU materialization plus a host-to-device copy on every call; use torch.zeros/ones/full/empty directly on the device instead. clip_qk() in megatron/core/optimizer/qk_clip.py issues a per-layer torch.distributed.all_reduce (ReduceOp.MAX over the DP+CP group) inside a Python loop over every transformer layer, then immediately calls .item() (log_max_attention_logit = max(..., torch.max(...).item())). This runs every optimizer step (every train_step when --qk-clip or --log-max-attention-logit is set). Two problems: (1) one small collective per layer instead of a single batched all_reduce over a stacked tensor across layers => N_layers small NCCL launches on the critical path each step; (2) the .item() forces a device->host sync after each all_reduce, serializing GPU work per layer. On deep models this adds measurable per-step latency. Recommend stacking current_max_attn_logits across layers into one tensor and doing a single all_reduce, and deferring/removing the per-layer .item() sync.
findings.suggested_benchmark: Per-step train_step latency with --qk-clip enabled on a deep (e.g. 60+ layer) model vs baseline; measure NCCL launch count and host-sync stalls via profiler.

findings.claim: static rule 'full-cuda-sync-in-overlap-path' matched antipattern: Calling the global torch.cuda.synchronize() (full device barrier) to order work against a side stream, which serializes all compute/comm and defeats overlap; stream.wait_stream should be used instead. (matched: 'torch.cuda.synchronize()')

The core behavioral change is a memory/perf tradeoff selected by the new opt-in flag `fsdp_db_use_persist_buf_on_alloc_fail` (default False). When a bucket does not fit the FixedPoolAllocator double-buffer set, the pre-change code always fell back to the dynamic backup_allocator (dynamic CUDA memory allocation, no NCCL user-buffer registration -> lower comm throughput). The new default (False) preserves exactly this existing behavior, so the diff does NOT introduce a regression by default; instead it OFFERS an opt-in path (True) that uses a persistent global-memory-buffer allocation to keep NCCL UB enabled at the cost of more memory. No historical mechanism (mismatched-collective-config / ipc-transport-overhead / suboptimal-kernel-gate) is newly triggered by the default path.
findings.suggested_benchmark: FSDP step-time with nccl_ub=True and a model whose bucket does not fit FixedPoolAllocator, comparing fsdp_db_use_persist_buf_on_alloc_fail=True vs False (comm throughput + peak memory).

此改动触及 low-precision-state（memory-footprint），该类问题历史上主要在 [interacts with CP/SP/TP padding, distributed optimizer (use_distributed_optimizer), distributed optimizer (ZeRO-style DP param sharding); test uses TP=4, DP] 配置下显现；This diff adds per-module TransformerEngine quantization config, allowing fine-grained selection of fp8/fp4/mxfp8/nvfp4 recipes per layer via fp8_autocast overr

Each TE linear forward (TELinear, TELayerNormColumnParallelLinear, TEGroupedLinear) now unconditionally wraps super().forward() in a `with quant_context:` block via _get_fp8_autocast_for_quant_params(). In the default/common case te_quant_params is None, so the function returns nullcontext() and the extra cost is only a Python function call plus context-manager enter/exit per linear per microbatch. This is a hot path (every attention/MLP linear, every layer, every step), but the overhead of nullcontext is small. It is NOT a hardcoded fp8_autocast(enabled=True) as in the historical case: when unconfigured no autocast/recipe object is constructed and precision is unchanged. So this does not by itself change the compute precision of layers. Worth a micro-benchmark to confirm the per-forward Python overhead is negligible at scale.
findings.suggested_benchmark: Microbenchmark GPT/Mamba forward step throughput with quant_recipe=None (default) before vs after, high layer count, to measure nullcontext + branch overhead per linear.

findings.claim: static rule 'inverted-cuda-gate-selects-native-kernel' matched antipattern: A fused/optimized backend kernel is gated behind a negated platform check (e.g. `not current_platform.is_cuda()`), so on that very platform the code falls through to the slow eager/native path. (matched: 'if not self.model_config.enforce_eager and not current_platform.is_rocm()')

static rule 'tensor-from-pylist-on-device' matched antipattern: Constructing a tensor from a Python list literal with a non-CPU device forces a CPU materialization plus a host-to-device copy on every call; use torch.zeros/ones/full/empty directly on the device instead. (matched: 'torch.tensor(\n                [self.total_tokens], dtype=cu_seqlens.dtype, device=')

In MoETransformerLayer._forward_mlp (transformer_layer.py, partial cudagraph path), a device synchronization was added after the router graph replay: `self._router_dtoh_event.record(); self._router_dtoh_event.synchronize()`. This is a CPU-side blocking sync (torch.cuda.Event.synchronize blocks the host until the event completes) inserted into the per-MoE-layer hot training forward path. Every MoE layer in every microbatch now stalls the CPU until the router's D2H copies finish, serializing CPU launch and GPU execution and creating pipeline bubbles/GPU-idle time. It defeats the async-launch benefit of the partial cudagraph design and scales linearly with the number of MoE layers.
findings.suggested_benchmark: Per-step training throughput for MoE model with cuda_graph_impl=local partial scope; measure host-side stall / GPU idle gaps around router->expert transition with/without the event.synchronize().

findings.claim: This diff disables the persistent (work-stealing) sparse-MLA kernel path for chunked-prefill continuations (query_len>1 AND seq_len>query_len). When `is_chunked_continuation.any()` is true for any request in the batch, `use_persistent` becomes False for the ENTIRE batch: get_mla_metadata_v1 is skipped and `work_meta_data=None` is passed, forcing the aiter forward to run the non-persistent (no load-balanced CU work-stealing) code path. This is a deliberate correctness fix (vllm#47042 numerical error compounding across chunked prefill), so it fits the 'correctness-forces-slow-path' pattern rather than an accidental regression. However, it is worth noting the granularity is per-batch, not per-request: a single chunked-prefill continuation request in a mixed batch drops the entire batch (including any decode tokens) off the faster persistent kernel, so throughput of otherwise-persistent-eligible tokens is degraded whenever they are co-batched with a chunked continuation.
findings.suggested_benchmark: DeepSeek-V3.2 sparse-MLA long-context decode+chunked-prefill mixed-batch throughput on MI300X: compare tokens/s with vs without a chunked-prefill continuation present in the batch

findings.claim: The added guard performs a per-build CPU-side computation: `common_attn_metadata.seq_lens_cpu[:num_reqs].numpy()` plus elementwise numpy comparisons and `.any()` on every build() call. This is cheap relative to the kernel launch it gates and sits on the metadata-build path (not inner attention loop), so it is not a hot-path concern, but the `.numpy()` materialization is redundant since seq_lens_cpu is already read for `clamped_seq_lens` just above — the two conversions could be shared.

The new FlashInferCuteDslNvFp4LinearKernel is inserted at the FRONT of _POSSIBLE_NVFP4_KERNELS[PlatformEnum.CUDA] (vllm/model_executor/kernels/linear/__init__.py), ahead of the previous default FlashInferCutlassNvFp4LinearKernel. Because its is_supported() only requires SM10x + has_flashinfer() and its can_implement() unconditionally returns (True, None), on all SM100 (sm_10x) hardware the auto-selected NVFP4 GEMM backend for EVERY nvfp4 linear layer silently changes to cute-dsl regardless of layer shape or batch size. If the cutedsl kernel is not uniformly faster than the cutlass backend across the full latency/throughput regime (e.g. small-batch decode vs large-batch prefill), this is a default-backend swap that can regress the common serving path with no per-shape gating. Prior 'fused-backend-swap' incidents show default backend re-ordering silently changing perf for the whole model.
findings.suggested_benchmark: NVFP4 linear/end-to-end latency+throughput on SM100 comparing auto (now cute-dsl) vs --linear-backend flashinfer_cutlass across small decode batch (1-4 tokens) and large prefill batch, per layer shape.

findings.claim: FlashInferCuteDslNvFp4LinearKernel does NOT override input_quant_key() (inherits base None) and its apply_weights() unconditionally re-quantizes the activation via scaled_fp4_quant(...backend='flashinfer-cutedsl') on the hot path. The kernel it displaces as default, FlashInferCutlassNvFp4LinearKernel, overrides input_quant_key()->kNvfp4Dynamic and consumes pre-quantized activations via as_quantized_activation(), letting the activation-quant be fused/hoisted out of the linear (skipping in-kernel quant when a fused producer supplies a QuantizedActivation). By making cutedsl the auto default on SM100 without exposing an input_quant_key, any fused-activation-quantization producer path is defeated and every layer redundantly re-quantizes its input each call, losing the quant-activation fusion that the cutlass path enables.
findings.suggested_benchmark: Compare per-layer activation-quant op count / kernel launches on SM100 nvfp4 model between cutedsl (in-kernel quant) and cutlass (fused/pre-quantized activation) paths under torch.compile fusion.

findings.claim: 此改动触及 speculative-decoding（inference-serving），该类问题历史上主要在 [TP with num_heads 16 or 128; MLA, async scheduling (spec decode); PP path handled separately, spec-decode (speculative decoding, TP=1 in example)] 配置下显现；This diff adds "Dynamic Speculative Decoding" (DSD), which dynamically tunes the number of speculative tokens K per batch-size range to preserve decode throughp

The added guard `and self.weight.dtype == torch.bfloat16` on `allow_dsv3_router_gemm` (gate_linear.py L74-77), combined with forcing fp32 gate weights for GLM-5.2 / configs with moe_router_dtype='float32' in deepseek_v2.py (_get_moe_router_dtype -> params_dtype=fp32, out_dtype=fp32), disables the fast DSV3 specialized router GEMM for these models. For GLM-5 (hidden=6144, experts=256) which was previously DSV3-kernel-eligible, the router now falls through to slower dispatch tiers (Tier 3 cuBLAS bf16→fp32 is also skipped because weight is now fp32, so it lands on Tier 4 F.linear in fp32). Additionally the fp32 gate weights double the router weight resident memory versus bf16. This is a deliberate correctness fix (fp32 routing), but reviewers should confirm the specialized-kernel loss is intended and only affects fp32-routing configs, not existing bf16 DSV3/GLM deployments.
findings.suggested_benchmark: Router GEMM latency for GLM-5/5.2 (hidden=6144, E=256) at M<=16 comparing DSV3 specialized kernel vs fp32 F.linear fallback; also measure gate weight memory bf16 vs fp32.

findings.claim: static rule 'tensor-on-gpu-before-cpu-sync' matched antipattern: Building a small metadata tensor directly on the GPU (device=self.device) that is later consumed by a CPU-synchronizing op like .item()/.tolist(), forcing a blocking device-to-host sync. (matched: 'torch.tensor(seg_addrs, dtype=torch.int64, device=self.device')

static rule 'unconditional-device-copy-may-alias-cpu' matched antipattern: A pre-allocated device tensor is filled via an unconditional `.copy_(..., non_blocking=True)` from a CPU staging tensor without checking whether the target device is CPU, causing a redundant CPU→CPU copy (and duplicate allocation) when running on CPU. (matched: '.copy_(self._ids_pinned[:n_blocks], non_blocking=True)')

此改动触及 prefix-state-caching（inference-serving），该类问题历史上主要在 [DCP/PCP asserted world_size==1 for hybrid/sliding-window paths, tensor-parallel (TP>1) with MLA; also PP-aware per comment, multi-engine / data-parallel or cross-restart (inconsistent lora_int_id across engines)] 配置下显现；The diff modifies KV-offloading lookup/scheduling logic to exclude a volatile trailing block for EAGLE/MTP draft-model attention groups. Although framed as a se

ThinkingTokenBudgetLogitsProcessor.apply() runs a Python for-loop over the whole batch (`for i in range(batch_size)`) plus a second Python generator scan (`any(state.get("in_end") ...)`) on every decode step, and this runs UNCONDITIONALLY whenever `self._state` is non-empty. Since `update_state` keeps an entry in `self._state` for every request that merely has `thinking_token_budget` set (not only those actively forcing end tokens), the per-step Python-level per-request work is paid even when no request is currently in end-mode. In contrast, sibling processors (MinP/MinTokens/LogitBias) gate their `apply()` on a cheap counter/dict-empty check and do their work vectorized. This is a needless per-step per-request Python cost on the sampling hot path.
findings.suggested_benchmark: Decode throughput (tokens/s) with reasoning_config enabled and a large batch (e.g. max_num_seqs=256) where all requests set thinking_token_budget but none are in end-mode; compare against baseline without the processor.

findings.claim: gpu_model_runner sets `logitsprocs_need_output_token_ids = ... or self.vllm_config.reasoning_config is not None`. This flag globally forces the runner to keep output_token_ids synced/materialized for ALL requests in the batch on every step whenever a reasoning_config is present at server level — even requests that did not set thinking_token_budget and thus have no state in the processor. Enabling reasoning-config server-wide therefore imposes the output-token-id bookkeeping cost on the whole batch unconditionally, not just on thinking-budget requests. Verify whether this triggers extra per-step device->host copies of sampled token ids in async scheduling.
findings.suggested_benchmark: Decode throughput with --reasoning-config set but zero requests using thinking_token_budget, vs. same workload without --reasoning-config, to isolate the always-on output_token_ids sync cost.
