findings.claim: In MHCPreOp.forward_hip and MHCPostOp.forward_hip (ROCm/HIP path), the AITER fused kernels (torch.ops.vllm.mhc_pre_aiter / mhc_post_aiter) are commented out, so the code now unconditionally falls back to the PyTorch-native mhc_kernels.mhc_pre_torch / mhc_post_torch even for the common hidden_size % 256 == 0 case where the fast fused kernel was previously selected. This is the fused-backend-swap mechanism in reverse: a prebuilt specialized/fused AITER backend is disabled in favor of the slower composite native implementation. MHCPreOp/MHCPostOp run per DeepseekV4 decoder layer (hot forward path), so throughput/latency on ROCm will regress at large token counts even though correctness improves. The fix is intentional (accuracy bug) but is a real perf regression until the AITER version is bumped.
findings.suggested_benchmark: DeepSeek V4 ROCm decode/prefill throughput at large batch/seqlen (hidden_size % 256 == 0), comparing mhc_pre/mhc_post fused-AITER vs torch fallback

findings.claim: 此改动触及 compile-cache-misconfig（compilation），该类问题历史上主要在 [cache keyed per rank_{rank}_{dp_rank}, affects per-rank cache dirs (rank_{rank}_{dp_rank}); multi-model scenarios: spec decode with EAGLE/Medusa draft head + backbone, or multimodal language_model/vision_model, affected path keys cache per tp world_size/rank] 配置下显现；除了纯粹的 flashinfer 版本 bump（Dockerfile/versions.json/requirements）和测试超时调整外，核心非文档改动在 kernel_warmup.py：它重新启用了 FlashInfer 的 persistent autotune 缓存（use_persistent_cach

This PR flips the FlashInfer autotune default from the always-on 'all ranks tune independently' path to the persistent-cache path (use_persistent_cache = True for all non-DeepEP backends), removing the previously hardcoded `_FLASHINFER_USE_PERSISTENT_CACHE = False`. The removed code carried an explicit warning that FlashInfer's persistent file cache had collisions (e.g. `use_8x4_sf_layout=True/False`) which 'causes invalid tactics to be chosen'. `use_8x4_sf_layout` is still branched on at runtime (vllm/utils/flashinfer.py:784, vllm/_custom_ops.py:1625) for trtllm FP8 GEMM paths. Re-enabling the persistent cache is only safe if the FlashInfer 0.6.13 bump actually fixes that cache-key collision; if it does not, this default flip resurrects the exact bug and would cause suboptimal/invalid autotune tactics to be loaded, degrading FP8/attention kernel throughput on Hopper/Blackwell. This is a default-value toggle routing to a different tactic-selection path, so it should be verified that 0.6.13 resolves the collision before merge.
findings.suggested_benchmark: On a Hopper/Blackwell FP8 model (e.g. an FP8 MoE) with a non-DeepEP all2all backend, run flashinfer_autotune twice across a restart so the persistent cache is populated then loaded; compare decode/prefill kernel latency and throughput against the parent (all-ranks-tune) path to confirm the persistent-cache path does not select slower/invalid tactics for both use_8x4_sf_layout=True (m<=32) and False cases.

The new TrtLlmBf16Experts._supports_routing_method (vllm/model_executor/layers/fused_moe/experts/trtllm_bf16_moe.py) drops RoutingMethodType.Renormalize and RoutingMethodType.RenormalizeNaive from the supported set (commented out due to a Qwen3.5 issue), whereas the parent's _supports_routing_method_bf16 in flashinfer_trtllm_moe.py explicitly included both. Renormalize is the common default routing method (config.py get_routing_method_type returns Renormalize for standard softmax+topk MoE). This narrows the runtime predicate so many BF16 MoE models on Blackwell now fail the TRTLLM support check and are routed to the slower TRITON fast-path fallback in select_unquantized_moe_backend. This is a runtime-predicate exclusion of the fast path (boolean-guard-misfire), affecting the hot MoE forward path.

findings.claim: In cpu_fused_moe.py, `_CPU_MOE_ACT_FN[MoEActivation.SILU]` was changed from a direct static-method reference (`SiluAndMul.forward_native`) to a lambda that instantiates a fresh `SiluAndMul(compile_native=False)` object on every invocation: `lambda x: SiluAndMul(compile_native=False).forward_native(x)`. This entry is called via `_CPU_MOE_ACT_FN[act](gate_up)` inside the per-expert loop of `cpu_fused_moe_torch` (once per active expert per forward). Each call now constructs an nn.Module/CustomOp (`__new__` does an op_registry_oot lookup; `__init__` calls `super().__init__()`, `current_platform.is_cuda_alike()/is_xpu()/is_cpu()` platform probes, and `dispatch_forward()` which invokes `get_cached_compilation_config()`, `enabled()`, and updates config custom-op sets) before running the trivial `F.silu(x[:d]) * x[d:]`. This is pure per-iteration host-side Python object-construction/dispatch overhead on the CPU MoE inference path that the previous static reference avoided entirely — and the comment directly above the dict explicitly states these entries were written to avoid instantiating CustomOp classes. The change contradicts that intent and adds recurring per-expert host overhead.
findings.suggested_benchmark: Microbenchmark cpu_fused_moe_torch forward with SILU activation across many experts/tokens on CPU, comparing per-forward latency before vs after the lambda change; also time repeated SiluAndMul(compile_native=False) instantiation vs a single cached instance.

In gpu_ubatch_wrapper.UBatchWrapper.__call__ the total token count is still hardcoded to 2 ubatches: `num_tokens = (ubatch_slices[0].token_slice.stop - ubatch_slices[0].token_slice.start) * 2` (parent line ~407, unchanged by the diff) and `_capture_ubatches` computes `num_tokens = ubatch_metadata[0].num_tokens + ubatch_metadata[1].num_tokens`. When ubatch_size > 2 (the new XBO path), this undercounts tokens, so the cudagraph cache is keyed on a wrong num_tokens and the full-cudagraph replay branch (`num_tokens in self.cudagraphs`) can miss/mis-match — forcing recapture or the non-graph eager ubatch path, degrading the compute/comm overlap the feature is supposed to provide. The N-way generalization is incomplete on the hottest execution path.
findings.suggested_benchmark: Run decode with --ubatch-size 4 + deepep_low_latency and FULL cudagraph; measure per-step latency / GPU util vs --enable-dbo (2-way) and check cudagraph capture/replay hit rate.

findings.claim: The CUTLASS MLA gate is narrowed: parent built MLA_ARCHS for '10.0a;10.1a;10.3a;12.0a;12.1a' (CUDA<13) and '10.0f;11.0f;12.0f' (CUDA>=13), but this commit removes the SM12x family from both branches (now '10.0a;10.3a' / '10.0f;11.0f'). Since the default X86 wheel arch list includes 12.0, SM120 (Blackwell RTX 50xx / GB20x) GPUs will no longer have the sm100_cutlass_mla_kernel compiled for them. If a 12.x device previously matched via the loose intersection (12.0a) and used the CUTLASS MLA fast path, it will now fall back to a slower MLA backend. The added comment claims runtime dispatch is gated in cutlass_mla.py, but the arch reduction still drops a kernel that the wheel arch list can request — verify the runtime dispatch actually excludes SM12x so this is intentional and not a lost fast path.
findings.suggested_benchmark: MLA attention (DeepSeek-V3) latency on an SM120 (Blackwell consumer, e.g. RTX 5090) device using the shipped CUDA-12.x/13 wheel

findings.claim: The grouped_mm_c3x_sm100 (CUTLASS MoE SM100) gate drops arches: parent used '10.0f;11.0f' (CUDA>=13) and '10.0a;10.1a;10.3a' (CUDA<13); this commit reduces them to '10.0f' and '10.0a;10.3a', removing 11.0f and 10.1a. On systems that request 11.0 (Thor, in CUDA_ARCH_AARCH64) or 10.1, the SM100 grouped-MM CUTLASS MoE kernel is no longer compiled. Combined with -DENABLE_CUTLASS_MOE_SM100 still being defined only when SCALED_MM_ARCHS is non-empty, a Thor/aarch64 target could lose the fast CUTLASS grouped-MoE path and fall back to a slower MoE implementation. Confirm the 11.0f drop is covered by family-specific 10.0f matching or is a deliberate size trim.
findings.suggested_benchmark: CUTLASS grouped-GEMM MoE (FP8) throughput on SM100/SM110 (Thor 11.0) targets built from the aarch64 wheel arch list

The old Fp8LinearOp computed an output token-padding (output_padding = 17) only for the 'torch' backend and only when NOT running under torch.compile (config.mode < CompilationMode.VLLM_COMPILE), because padding to batch>16 makes torch._scaled_mm faster but breaks dynamic shapes under compile. In the refactor, FP8ScaledMMLinearKernel.__init__ passes num_token_padding=self.get_output_padding() and the base get_output_padding() returns None unconditionally. Unless a torch-backend FP8 kernel subclass overrides get_output_padding() to re-implement the '17 when eager && torch backend' logic, the performance-oriented padding for the torch/_scaled_mm fallback path is silently dropped, degrading throughput on the eager torch-scaled_mm FP8 path (non-cutlass/non-rocm, e.g. capability<89 CUDA). Verify pytorch.py's *TorchFP8ScaledMMLinearKernel overrides get_output_padding to restore the pad-when-eager behavior.
findings.suggested_benchmark: Microbench torch._scaled_mm FP8 linear (torch backend, eager mode, batch 1-16) before/after; compare with per-tensor dynamic quant path on a GPU without cutlass-fp8 support.

findings.claim: This diff flips `fuse_allreduce_rms` from a static `False` to the runtime-gated `enable_allreduce_rms_fusion` at optimization levels O2/O3 (the O2 default). This is the *opposite* of the historical config-toggle-perf-feature regression pattern: instead of disabling a fast path, it *enables* the flashinfer AllReduce+RMSNorm fusion (a performance optimization) by default when TP>1, CUDA, Hopper+ (SM90), and flashinfer is installed. The gating predicate `enable_allreduce_rms_fusion` is conservative and well-scoped, so this is an intended speedup, not a regression. Worth noting only because it changes runtime behavior on the default (-O2) path for multi-GPU Hopper deployments and should be validated to not regress cases where the fused AllReduce is slower than NCCL AllReduce at small message sizes (the pass already gates via `flashinfer_max_size`).
findings.suggested_benchmark: TP=2/4/8 Hopper (H100) latency + throughput at both small and large batch/hidden sizes, -O2 default, comparing fuse_allreduce_rms on vs off

The pooling stage was NOT actually batched: after the (correctly batched) encoder call, the code loops per-image (`for orig_idx in range(total_images)`) / per-frame (`for i in range(total_frames)`), calling `vt.pooler(...)` once per item on `single_hidden.unsqueeze(0)`. This re-introduces exactly the per-item Python loop + N kernel launches that the PR claims to remove — the same anti-pattern as the historical serial-loop-over-BMM case (loop launching B kernels then torch.cat). Since pooling is uniform-shape within a resolution bucket (all items share `output_length`/`pooling_k2`), it could be applied over the whole batched `hidden_states` tensor in one pooler call. As written, encoder batching helps but the pooler remains O(N) launches, partially defeating the perf goal on large image/frame counts.
findings.suggested_benchmark: Gemma4 multimodal prefill with many images (e.g. 32 same-resolution images) and a long video (32 frames): compare per-item pooler loop vs single batched pooler call latency.

findings.claim: In OPTIMIZATION_LEVEL_01/02/03, kernel_config.enable_flashinfer_autotune is flipped from True to False. This static default change disables the FlashInfer autotuning warmup (flashinfer_autotune in vllm/model_executor/warmup/kernel_warmup.py), which benchmarks and caches the best kernel implementation per shape. As documented in flashinfer_autotune's own docstring, without autotuning FlashInfer 'will rely on heuristics, which may be significantly slower' for attention/MoE kernels on Hopper (SM 9.0) / Blackwell (SM 10.0). This is a runtime latency/throughput regression on the hot execution path for all default optimization levels (O2 is the default optimization_level).
findings.suggested_benchmark: End-to-end decode+prefill throughput/latency on a Hopper/Blackwell GPU with a FlashInfer-backed model at optimization_level O2, comparing enable_flashinfer_autotune=True vs False.

SiluAndMulWithClamp: at parent, ROCm (covered by is_cuda_alike()) bound the fused C++ kernel torch.ops._C.silu_and_mul_with_clamp. The diff adds an explicit `if current_platform.is_rocm(): self._forward_method = self.forward_native` branch, forcing ROCm onto the pure-PyTorch native path (three elementwise ops: clamp gate, clamp up, silu*mul) instead of the single fused kernel. This is a hot per-token MoE shared-expert activation path (deepseek_v4.py uses SiluAndMulWithClamp). Matches the historical suboptimal-kernel-gate mechanism (fast kernel exists but gating rejects it on ROCm) — the silu_and_mul_with_clamp kernel is registered under TORCH_EXTENSION_NAME which compiles for HIP as well, so the gate is a deliberate slowdown, not a missing kernel.
findings.suggested_benchmark: Microbench SiluAndMulWithClamp forward on ROCm (native vs fused C++ kernel) at MoE hidden sizes; end-to-end DeepSeek-V4 decode throughput on MI300.

findings.claim: DeepseekV4 O-projection: the diff routes ROCm to a BF16 reference path (rocm_inv_rope_einsum + wo_b, returning early) and skips the fused_inv_rope_fp8_quant + FP8 einsum path that CUDA uses. The comment 'Keep ROCm on the BF16 reference wo_a path util kernel ready' confirms the fast FP8 fused path exists but is intentionally not enabled on ROCm, running the heavier BF16 reference einsum instead. This is a new-model enablement suboptimal gate on a per-decode-step attention output path.
findings.suggested_benchmark: DeepSeek-V4 decode latency on ROCm comparing BF16 reference wo_a einsum vs FP8-quant fused path once available.

findings.claim: 此改动触及 speculative-decoding（inference-serving），该类问题历史上主要在 [TP with num_heads 16 or 128; MLA, async scheduling (spec decode); PP path handled separately, spec-decode (speculative decoding, TP=1 in example)] 配置下显现；The diff adds DFlash speculators config parsing and a draft model vocab mapping parameter (draft_id_to_target_id), wiring aux hidden state layer ids and mask to

The diff adds a static config toggle in VllmConfig.__post_init__: whenever `kv_transfer_config is not None` (i.e. any KV connector such as NIXL, LMCache, shared-storage, CPU offload is configured for disaggregated prefill/decode), it force-sets `scheduler_config.disable_hybrid_kv_cache_manager = True`. This unconditionally turns OFF the hybrid KV cache manager (HMA allocator), forcing a uniform (non-hybrid) KV cache allocation. For models with sliding-window attention or Mamba/hybrid attention, HMA allocates right-sized per-layer KV blocks — disabling it wastes KV memory (fewer usable blocks / smaller effective cache) and reduces throughput. This exactly matches the historical config-toggle-perf-feature pattern where a KV-connector-set predicate statically forces the slow uniform-KV path. Note: the disable is applied regardless of whether the connector actually supports HMA (blanket toggle, with only a TODO/warning to later make it connector-aware via `SupportsHMA`).
findings.suggested_benchmark: Run a sliding-window-attention model (e.g. Gemma-2/Mistral SWA) or a Mamba/hybrid model with `--kv-transfer-config` set (e.g. shared-storage or NIXL connector) and compare max KV cache blocks / achievable concurrent requests / throughput vs. running the same model without a connector where HMA stays enabled.

The gate function enable_mla_dual_rms_nor

static rule 'tensor-to-cpu-forces-d2h-sync' matched antipattern: Moving a GPU tensor to CPU via .to('cpu')/.cpu() on a hot path forces a blocking device→host copy and CUDA synchronization. (matched: '.cpu()')

In DFlashQwen3Model.precompute_and_store_context_kv, the per-layer RMSNorm on K is done with a Python loop `for i in range(L): ops.rms_norm(all_k_normed[i], ...)`, and later per-layer cache insert is another Python loop `for i in range(L): attn.impl.do_kv_cache_update(...)`. These per-layer loops run on the hot path for every drafting step. Unlike the KV projection and RoPE, which were correctly fused into one big op, the K-norm and cache-insert issue L separate kernel launches per drafting step. For models with many context layers this adds per-step launch overhead. Consider batching K-norm across layers (the layout [L, num_ctx, nkv, hd] is already contiguous) similar to how RoPE was fused via view to [L*num_ctx, kv].
findings.suggested_benchmark: spec-decode DFlash draft latency vs num context layers L

findings.claim: NVFP4 KV cache path forces the TRTLLM attention kernel to write FP8 output into a pre-allocated buffer (self._nvfp4_fp8_out) and then dequantizes back to model dtype via an extra out.to(output.dtype) copy in every prefill and decode step. This adds a per-step full-output-tensor D2D cast+copy on the hot attention path whenever output.dtype != FP8. The historical low-precision-state case flagged exactly this kind of narrowed-dtype-plus-repack overhead; here it is a fixed extra kernel launch + memory traffic proportional to num_tokens*num_heads*head_size on each forward.
findings.suggested_benchmark: Decode/prefill latency with FlashInfer TRTLLM nvfp4 KV cache vs fp8 KV cache; measure per-step attention time and extra copy overhead

findings.claim: In the main per-decode-step sampling hot path, _gumbel_sample_kernel now casts the ENTIRE vocab-wide logits block to FP64 ('logits = logits.to(tl.float64)') and performs the block-wide tl.max(logits, axis=0, return_indices=True) reduction in FP64. Only the scalar Gumbel noise transform (u -> -log(-log(u))) actually needs FP64 precision; widening all [num_tokens, vocab_size] logits and the argmax reduction to double introduces vocab-wide FP64 arithmetic on the hottest sampling path (called from sampler.py sample() and the eagle draft speculator every generation step). FP64 throughput is 1/2 to 1/32-1/64 of FP32 on most datacenter/consumer GPUs, so this can materially slow per-step sampling. A cheaper alternative is to build the FP64 uniform + gumbel noise (as tl_rand64 already does) but add it to FP32 logits (or keep the max reduction in FP32), rather than promoting the whole logits vector.
findings.suggested_benchmark: Microbenchmark _gumbel_sample_kernel / gumbel_sample over realistic [num_tokens, vocab_size] (e.g. 256 x 128k-256k) on A100/H100 and a consumer GPU, comparing FP32-reduction vs FP64-reduction wall time and sampler step latency in an end-to-end decode throughput run (temp>0).

findings.claim: gumbel_sample now allocates local_max as torch.float64 instead of torch.float32, doubling the per-call scratch buffer ([num_tokens, num_blocks]) and doubling the bytes read/written for the local_max store in-kernel and the subsequent host-side local_max.argmax(dim=-1). num_blocks is small (ceil(vocab/1024)), so absolute size is modest, but it is extra bandwidth/allocation on a per-step path and compounds the FP64 reduction cost above.
findings.suggested_benchmark: Measure allocator churn / bytes of local_max scratch (fp32 vs fp64) and local_max.argmax time across batch sizes.

findings.claim: 此改动触及 async-output-overlap（inference-serving），该类问题历史上主要在 [async scheduling enabled (use_async_scheduling); single-step overlap, Max-Throughput TP1 conc1024; Min-Latency TP8 conc8 (issue-measured), multiprocess executor (TP/PP with MultiprocExecutor); async scheduling / max_concurrent_batches>1 exercises the non_block Future path] 配置下显现；这个 diff 让异步调度（async scheduling）与 pipeline parallelism 完全兼容：移除了 PP 下对含 output placeholder 请求"不再调度下一步"的限制，并新增了通过 GPU broadcast 在最后一个 PP stage 广播 sampled token ids

The new EncoderCudaGraphManager greedy-packs images into the smallest fitting token budget and, when total tokens exceed all budgets, falls back to eager (encoder_eager_forward). If real workloads frequently produce image batches whose packed token count exceeds the largest captured budget (max_num_batched_tokens-derived), the CUDA graph replay path is skipped and every such encode runs eager. The auto-generated power-of-2 budget ladder (min 64 up to max_num_batched_tokens) can be coarse: an image just above a boundary is rounded up to the next (up to 2x larger) budget, replaying a graph that computes ~2x the needed tokens as wasted encoder compute. This mirrors the cudagraph-enablement pattern where capture is present but the fast path is not actually hit / carries overhead.
findings.suggested_benchmark: Encoder (ViT) throughput with cudagraph_mm_encoder=True vs eager, sweeping image sizes that fall just above budget boundaries and above the max budget, measuring encode latency and wasted-token ratio.

findings.claim: Feature is opt-in and off by default (cudagraph_mm_encoder=False), so no regression is introduced on default paths. When enabled, capture pre-runs encoder_cudagraph_forward for every token_budget level at startup; a coarse/large auto-generated budget ladder can materially increase startup/warmup time and pin GPU memory for many captured graphs. This is a compile/startup-time cost proportional to len(token_budgets).
findings.suggested_benchmark: Measure engine startup time and peak GPU memory with cudagraph_mm_encoder enabled across varying encoder_cudagraph_token_budgets ladder lengths.

The mo

enable_allreduce_rms_fusion now additionally requires `data_parallel_size == 1`, disabling the AR-RMS-Quant fusion pass entirely for any TP+DP combination. This is an intentional correctness workaround (issue #34458), but it is a genuine performance regression for TP>1 + DP>1 deployments that previously received the fused allreduce+RMSNorm path: those runs now fall back to unfused allreduce + separate RMSNorm, increasing per-layer latency. Flagged as a deliberate fusion-disablement (not-turned-on boundary) with real throughput cost until #34458 is fixed.
findings.suggested_benchmark: TP=2,DP=2 flashinfer model decode throughput before/after this commit

findings.claim: enable_allreduce_rms_fusion changed the capability gate from `has_device_capability(90)` (Hopper AND newer, i.e. >= sm90) to the disjunction `is_device_capability(100) or is_device_capability(90)` (exactly sm90 OR exactly sm100). This narrows enablement: any GPU with capability > sm100 (e.g. future sm110/sm120) or intermediate/other capabilities (e.g. sm89, sm86, or a hypothetical sm90 variant reporting different minor) will now FAIL the check and the AR+RMS fusion pass will be silently turned off, dropping a throughput/latency optimization on hardware that previously received it. This is the 'fusion authored by combining current ops is now not enabled' case (the not-turned-on boundary of the cudagraph-incompatibility family). Since `is_device_capability` uses exact-equality on to_int() (major*10+minor), a Hopper part with minor != 0 would also miss.
findings.suggested_benchmark: TP>1 flashinfer allreduce+RMS fusion throughput on sm90 and a >sm100 device (verify fusion pass still enabled)

findings.claim: 此改动触及 fused-backend-swap（kernel-efficiency），该类问题历史上主要在 [tensor-parallel with --sequence-parallel required when tp_size>1; MoE and MTP explicitly unsupported (asserts), MoE (token-wise weighted SwiGLU path, weighted_bias_swiglu_impl), inference dynamic batching (TP per-partition heads used)] 配置下显现；This refactors the CPU W8A8 scaled_mm path onto a oneDNN-based backend (new dnnl_helper.cpp/dnnl_kernels.cpp, updated scaled_mm/cpu.py, oneDNN bumped v3.8.1→v3.

This refactor is the FIX for the historical one-time-setup-on-hot-path pattern, not a regression. The parent's DNNLPrimitiveHelper::gemm_s8s8_jit (csrc/cpu/dnnl_helper.hpp, called per-GEMM from quant.cpp:684/705/712/768/811/818/931) rebuilt dnnl::matmul::primitive_desc + instantiated dnnl::matmul matmul(matmul_pd) on EVERY scaled_mm call, and on aarch64 also reordered/repacked the weight (dnnl::reorder(b_m, mat_weights_mem)) each call. The new W8A8MatMulPrimitiveHandler prepacks the weight ONCE in the constructor (prepack_weight) and caches built matmul primitives via DNNLPrimitiveCache (ClassMatmulCache + MSizeCache keyed on M size), so execute() only rebinds data handles and reuses the cached primitive. This directly removes the per-call setup on the hot path.
findings.suggested_benchmark: CPU W8A8 (int8) linear layer latency: repeated scaled_mm at fixed and varying M (batch/seq) to confirm no per-call primitive_desc/reorder rebuild; compare against parent.

findings.claim: static rule 'cuda-stream-in-init' matched antipattern: Allocating a fresh torch.cuda.Stream() inside __init__ (or per-object construction), so N objects create N CUDA streams instead of sharing one. (matched: 'torch.cuda.Stream()')

execute_in_parallel produces tensors on aux CUDA streams (aux_streams[i]) that are then consumed on the default stream, but no record_stream is applied to those aux-produced tensors. The GEMM outputs — kv_score (compressor), indexer_weights, indexer_kv_score — are allocated by the aux-stream kernels via the caching allocator, and after the done_events[i].wait() join the default stream reads them (e.g. kv_score fed into compressor(kv_score,...), indexer_weights/indexer_kv_score fed into the indexer). The event.wait() only enforces kernel ordering; it does NOT keep the aux-stream allocation alive against the caching allocator, which tracks liveness per producing stream. If the aux stream frees/reuses the block before the default stream's consumer kernel actually runs, this is a cross-stream use-after-free race — the same allocator-liveness footgun called out in the shared-experts dual-stream case where hidden_states.clone() ran on the aux stream without record_stream. The vllm codebase already documents this exact requirement in shared_experts.py (record_stream on cross-stream inputs).
findings.suggested_benchmark: DSV4 decode-heavy throughput run with CUDA_LAUNCH_BLOCKING=0 and allocator stress (many concurrent requests) to surface intermittent cross-stream corruption/NaNs on the compressor/indexer GEMM outputs

findings.claim: 此改动触及 cpu-affinity-numa（parallelism-scheduling），该类问题历史上主要在 [CPU tensor-parallel / multi-threaded OMP inference, CPU OMP threads / tensor-parallel CPU backend, affects OpenMP thread affinity per worker rank] 配置下显现；The commit explicitly refactors CPU affinity and memory management. It introduces init_cpu_memory_env which performs NUMA node memory binding (numa_parse_nodest

The refactor moves NUMA memory binding out of Python into a new native op `init_cpu_memory_env(node_ids)` (csrc/cpu/utils.cpp). Its multi-node branch correctly uses `numa_set_interleave_mask(mask)` while single-node uses `numa_set_membind(mask)`, matching the historical fix that avoided the single-node-only assumption (`numa_parse_nodestring(std::to_string(mem_node_id))` + TORCH_CHECK). This is the *correct* form of the historical `cpu-affinity-numa` pattern, not a regression: node_ids are concatenated into a comma-separated string and the multi-node case is explicitly handled, so allocations are interleaved rather than pinned to one node. No latency/throughput regression is introduced by the binding policy itself.
findings.suggested_benchmark: CPU multi-NUMA-node TP throughput benchmark comparing before/after refactor (e.g. Llama-3.2-3B tp=2 on a 2-socket host) to confirm interleave/membind placement preserves memory locality.

The new `scheduler_reserve_full_isl` gate (default True) changes admission semantics: a waiting request is only admitted if the entire input sequence length fits in the KV cache at once, rather than only the first chunk. Under chunked prefill / memory pressure this is a stricter gate that suppresses admission of new/large requests when free blocks are momentarily insufficient for the full ISL, even though they could otherwise make progress chunk-by-chunk. This is the same class as the retrieved batch-composition-mismatch case (a scheduler gate refusing to admit requests based on a stronger reservation condition), and can reduce throughput/increase latency for large-context workloads that previously used chunked prefill to trickle in. Because the default is True, the behavior/perf change is on by default. scheduler.py:722-735
findings.suggested_benchmark: Throughput/latency with long inputs (e.g. ISL 8k-32k) under tight KV cache (high max_num_seqs, small gpu_memory_utilization) with chunked prefill enabled, comparing scheduler_reserve_full_isl True vs False.

findings.claim: `can_fit_full_sequence` is called on every waiting-request scheduling iteration (default enabled) and internally calls `coordinator.get_num_blocks_to_allocate`, which iterates over all single-type KV cache managers. This duplicates work already performed by the subsequent `allocate_slots` call (which also calls get_num_blocks_to_allocate + remove_skipped_blocks), roughly doubling the per-admission block-accounting cost on the scheduler hot path. For hybrid/many-group models the per-manager loop is repeated. Consider computing the fit check once and reusing the result, or gating it behind actual memory pressure.
findings.suggested_benchmark: Scheduler step microbenchmark with many waiting requests and a hybrid KV cache model, measuring schedule() latency with the gate on vs off.

Caching avoids redundant repeated work on a frequen
findings.suggested_benchmark: gpt-perf — CPU-bound launch overhead; small-batch step time

findings.claim: The new parent-side `_startup_prefetch_weights` reads ALL weight shards (potentially tens of GB) into the OS page cache via 8 parallel threads doing full `f.read()` loops over safetensors/bin files. On any box where the model does NOT fit in page cache (weights >= available RAM), this is pure wasted duplicate I/O: the parent reads the whole file, but by the time EngineCore page-faults ~10-15s later the kernel has already evicted the earliest-read pages under memory pressure, so the child re-reads from disk anyway. Worse, the parent-side read can evict pages the child had already faulted in, producing a net startup regression on memory-constrained hosts. There is no memory/size guard or posix_fadvise(WILLNEED) hint to bound the working set.
findings.suggested_benchmark: Cold-page-cache serve startup time for a model whose weights exceed available RAM (e.g. 70B on a 64GB host) with and without the parent-side prefetch; measure time-to-first-load and total resident-set eviction.

findings.claim: Parent-side prefetch can contend with the engine's own in-child weight read rather than overlap with it. The comment claims the child page-faults ~10-15s later, but on fast startups (warm imports, forkserver prewarm already reducing spawn cost by this same PR) the child can begin reading the same shards while the 8-thread parent pool is still saturating storage bandwidth/IOPS. Two independent readers of the same large files on a single NVMe/queue produce read-amplification and can slow the child's load vs. the pre-change single-reader path. The `input_address` headless-worker guard only excludes disaggregated API-only workers, not the common single-node case.
findings.suggested_benchmark: Serve startup on fast local NVMe with warm imports: compare weight load phase duration with prefetch enabled vs disabled to detect read contention.

findings.suggested_benchmark: gpt-perf — config-gated regressions; run with feature toggled

findings.claim: supports_trtllm_attention() narrows its device gate from is_device_capability_family(100) (matches all SM 10.x, e.g. 100 and 103) to is_device_capability(100) (exact SM 10.0 only). This intentionally disables the faster TRTLLM attention kernel on SM103 (GB300 / Blackwell Ultra), where FlashInfer >=0.6.7 hangs — i.e. the fast path is being restricted for correctness/hang-avoidance, not by mistake. This is the inverse of the historical suboptimal-kernel-gate cases (which WIDENED exact-100 checks to family/>=100 to re-enable Blackwell kernels). On SM103, TRTLLM decode/prefill attention will now fall back to the generic FlashInfer path, which is a real throughput/latency reduction for that hardware — but it is a deliberate hang fix, not a regression bug. On SM100 (the validated hardware) behavior is unchanged. Confirm that a correctly-performing fallback exists for SM103 so it degrades gracefully rather than losing the fast kernel with no working alternative.
findings.suggested_benchmark: Attention decode/prefill throughput+latency on SM103 (GB300) with FlashInfer backend, kv_cache_dtype=auto, comparing TRTLLM path (family gate) vs FlashInfer fallback (exact gate); also verify no hang.

findings.claim: Enabling probabilistic rejection sampling requires caching the full draft logits: `draft_logits` is now allocated as a [max_num_reqs, num_speculative_steps, vocab_size] tensor in model_dtype (see RequestState wiring `model_dtype`/`cache_draft_logits` and `draft_logits_out=self.req_states.draft_logits`), and the gumbel kernel does an extra full [num_reqs, vocab_size] store per speculative step to populate it (`processed_logits_out`). Each draft step now writes the entire processed-logits tensor to HBM — an extra full read/write pass over [num_reqs, vocab_size] per step that did not exist before. On large vocab (e.g. 128K-256K) this materially increases both memory footprint (GBs) and memory-bandwidth per decode step. Note this is only on the non-strict path, but the extra store branch/stride args are threaded through the hot gumbel kernel unconditionally.
findings.suggested_benchmark: spec-decode decode-step latency and peak HBM with rejection_sample_method=probabilistic vs strict, vocab_size>=128K, num_speculative_steps>=3

findings.claim: The temperature-scaling division `logits = logits / temp` was moved OUT of the `if temp != 0.0` block in `_gumbel_sample_kernel` (gumbel.py). In the parent code, temperature was applied inside the `temp != 0.0` branch (i.e. only for stochastic sampling); temperature==0 (greedy) requests skipped it entirely. In the new code the division and the `processed_logits` store run whenever `APPLY_TEMPERATURE` is set regardless of temperature==0, adding an extra vocab-wide FP32 division pass plus (when caching) a full [vocab] write for every token — even for greedy requests that previously did zero temperature work. This is the same class of fused-elementwise-in-kernel cost as the referenced case, but here it is being *added* to the greedy path rather than saving a launch.
findings.suggested_benchmark: gumbel_sample microbenchmark with temperature=0 (greedy) requests, large vocab, comparing per-step latency before/after

static rule 'reassign-sorted-instead-of-inplace-sort' matched antipattern: Reassigning a local list variable to sorted(same_var, ...) allocates a new list when an in-place .sort() would suffice, wasting CPU/allocations in hot paths. (matched: 'capture_sizes = sorted(capture_sizes')

In the new naive_block_assignment branch of fused_experts_impl, `num_tokens_post_padded` is created with `torch.empty((1), dtype=torch.int32, device=topk_ids.device)` followed by `.fill_(max_num_tokens_padded)`. `max_num_tokens_padded = topk_ids.numel() * config['BLOCK_SIZE_M']` is a host Python int, so `.fill_()` on a CUDA tensor issues a host-scalar-to-device write; combined with the newly-allocated empty tensor this is per-call setup work on the MoE hot path (run every layer, every forward). The kernel then reads it via tl.load(num_tokens_post_padded_ptr) only to early-out per block. Since the value is a compile-time-known host scalar (num_tokens*BLOCK_SIZE_M), materializing it as a device tensor per call is avoidable; consider caching the device scalar or passing it as a kernel scalar arg. Related historically to constructing/filling device scalars on the hot path.
findings.suggested_benchmark: Decode-shape MoE (M=1..8, top_k<=2, E>=128) microbench comparing per-call host overhead of naive path vs. sorted path

findings.claim: static rule 'pin-memory-ignores-config-flag' matched antipattern: A pin_memory() call is executed unconditionally inside a generic offload branch without checking the dedicated pin-memory config flag, allocating page-locked host memory even when the user disabled pinning. (matched: 'x = torch.empty(1024).pin_memory(')

static rule 'tensor-from-pylist-on-device' matched antipattern: Constructing a tensor from a Python list literal with a non-CPU device forces a CPU materialization plus a host-to-device copy on every call; use torch.zeros/ones/full/empty directly on the device instead. (matched: 'torch.tensor(\n                [self.total_tokens], dtype=cu_seqlens.dtype, device=')

In megatron/core/transformer/moe/moe_utils.py permute(), the non-drop_and_pad branch was changed from a masked_select over a token-index tensor to a full argsort over the flattened routing_map: `flat_sorted = routing_map.reshape(-1).argsort(descending=True, stable=True)`. This is done to produce fixed-shape output for CUDA graph capture, but argsort over num_experts*num_tokens elements is O(N log N) and materially more expensive than the previous masked_select (roughly O(N)). Since this branch runs in the token-dispatch hot path (dispatch_postprocess) on every MoE layer forward, the change adds per-layer overhead that also applies to the eager/training path where CUDA graphs are not in use, not just the captured path.
findings.suggested_benchmark: Micro-benchmark permute() on a representative routing_map [num_tokens=8192, num_experts=64] comparing argsort-based path vs previous masked_select path, and an end-to-end MoE layer forward throughput (training, cuda_graph off) before/after.

findings.claim: The change to `_do_optimizer_sanity_check`/grad_accum_dtype removes the special-case that forced `grad_accum_dtype = torch.float32` for bf16 models without ZeRO (previously: `if model_dtype == torch.bfloat16 and not self.zero_optimization(): grad_accum_dtype = torch.float32`). Now grad_accum_dtype defaults to `model_dtype` (bf16). This is a *positive* change for bytes-on-wire (gradient allreduce for the new DDP-bf16 path now runs in bf16 instead of fp32, halving payload). However, it also changes numerical/accumulation behavior; and the DDP-bf16 wrapper (FP16_Optimizer with low_precision_dtype=bf16) now materializes fp32 master weights + fp32 flattened grad buffers per group (`grads_groups_flat` built with `data_type = self.fp32_groups_flat[i].dtype` = fp32), so per-step grad casting bf16->fp32 and an extra fp32 copy is introduced on this newly-enabled path. Confirm allreduce still uses communication_data_type (bf16) rather than the fp32 master grads to avoid inflating collective payload.
findings.suggested_benchmark: DDP (stage=0) bf16 training: measure gradient allreduce bytes-on-wire and per-step throughput vs prior fp32 grad_accum path

findings.claim: In FP16_Optimizer.step()/step_fused_adam(), the overflow-check and loss-scale update are now correctly gated behind `self.use_grad_scaling` (only true for fp16). For the newly-enabled bf16 DDP path (use_grad_scaling=False), the expensive overflow scan + distributed all-reduce of the overflow flag is skipped — this is a good gating decision (the opposite of the suboptimal-kernel-gate anti-pattern). Note however that `self.overflow_checker.has_overflow(fp16_params)` / `check_using_norm` is still executed before the gate in step()/step_fused_adam even when use_grad_scaling is False (the scan runs, only the scale update is skipped). For bf16 DDP this overflow scan is unnecessary work on the hot path and could be gated too.
findings.suggested_benchmark: bf16 DDP step(): profile time spent in overflow_checker.has_overflow scan per optimizer step with use_grad_scaling=False

findings.suggested_benchmark: gpt-perf — CPU-bound launch overhead; small-batch step time

findings.claim: register_z3_param now issues a per-parameter blocking allgather + CPU sync at registration time. In init_z3.py, register_z3_param is called once per model parameter in a loop (`for p in engine.module.parameters(): dc.register_z3_param(...)`). The added validation code in register_z3_param builds a single-element CUDA long tensor per parameter, allocates world_size output tensors, calls process_group->allgather(...)->wait() (a synchronous collective), and then in a loop copies each result back to CPU via `all_padded_counts[i].to(torch::kCPU).item<int64_t>()`. For models with thousands of parameters this multiplies startup collective+D2H-sync overhead by the parameter count. Each allgather is well-formed but the per-param collective and per-rank .item() CPU readbacks are largely redundant sanity checks (padded_per_rank is a deterministic function of ds_shape and world_size that is guaranteed equal on all ranks if shapes are already synchronized). This is a startup-time regression, not a per-step hot path, so severity is important rather than critical.
findings.suggested_benchmark: Measure init_z3()/model registration wall time for a large ZeRO-3 model (e.g. 1000s of params) on >=2 GPUs with DeepCompile enabled, before vs after this commit, isolating time spent in register_z3_param.

findings.claim: The per-parameter verification does world_size separate GPU->CPU transfers via `all_padded_counts[i].to(torch::kCPU).item<int64_t>()` inside a loop. Each .item() forces a device synchronization and a tiny D2H copy; doing this per rank per parameter serializes on the CPU. If the check is kept it could be done once (single tensor D2H of the whole all_padded_counts vector) rather than world_size individual synchronizing reads.
findings.suggested_benchmark: Micro-benchmark register_z3_param over N params measuring cumulative time in the CPU readback loop vs a single batched D2H copy.

findings.claim: 此改动触及 weight-load-strategy（io-startup），该类问题历史上主要在 [tensor-parallel + data-parallel (fully-parallel dist checkpoint load; effect appears when TP>1 so main replicas concentrate in one DP group), distributed (dist) checkpoint with data-parallel + context-parallel groups; benefit scales with DP size, distributed checkpointing with a parallelization ProcessGroup (data-parallel replica group); only active when args.ckpt_fully_parallel_save is set] 配置下显现；This diff routes non-Muon params through DistributedOptimizer and refactors the param all-gather / FP8 post-all-gather path so that chained optimizers (LayerWis

This PR replaces DeepCompile's custom ZeRO3 partitioner (which skipped saving gathered/downcasted param aliases for backward via a custom partition_fn and a wrapped `choose_saved_values_set`) with `min_cut_rematerialization_partition` driven by MUST_RECOMPUTE annotations added in `_recompute_param_aliases` (deepspeed/compile/partitioner.py). This is the inverse of the historical [skip-saved-tensor-backward] mechanism: instead of merely not stashing tensors that were unneeded, the new code forces recomputation (re-running the fused cast+allgather) in the backward pass. The code itself flags this with a TODO warning that `min_cut_rematerialization_partition` 'may recompute more nodes than required for ZeRO3'. The PR's own preliminary evaluation shows a compute-time regression on nearly every configuration (eager+autocast 551.92->571.24 ms +3.5%, eager+bf16 419.87->445.76 ms +6.2%, inductor+autocast 546.97->570.09 ms +4.2%), i.e. a memory-for-throughput trade-off where extra recomputation raises step latency. Memory drops on the eager path, but the min-cut heuristic can select a recompute set that is larger/different than the intended param-alias set, degrading throughput.
findings.suggested_benchmark: Compare DeepCompile ZeRO3 step time (fwd+bwd ms) and peak memory before/after this commit across eager+bf16, eager+autocast, inductor+bf16, inductor+autocast on a fixed model; verify the +3-6% latency increase and quantify recomputed-node count from min_cut vs the intended param-alias set.

findings.claim: In the inductor backend the change goes from patching `choose_saved_values_set` (which surgically remapped only param-alias saved values back to the ds param) to wrapping the whole partition with `get_wrapped_partitioner(True, ...)` inside `wrap_partition_fn` (deepspeed/compile/inductor.py). The MUST_RECOMPUTE annotations now feed torch's default inductor partitioner, whose min-cut cost model may choose to recompute additional intermediate nodes beyond the param aliases. Per the PR's own numbers the inductor+autocast path shows +4.2% time with no memory benefit (12.84->13.04 GB), suggesting the added recomputation on the inductor path buys no memory savings while costing compute.
findings.suggested_benchmark: Micro-benchmark inductor+autocast DeepCompile ZeRO3: measure bwd recompute overhead and peak memory; confirm whether wrapping the full partition_fn adds recomputation without reducing peak memory relative to the parent's choose_saved_values_set remapping.

findings.suggested_benchmark: gpt-perf — multi-node TP/PP; watch all-reduce/all-gather time

findings.claim: 此改动触及 prefix-state-caching（inference-serving），该类问题历史上主要在 [DCP/PCP asserted world_size==1 for hybrid/sliding-window paths, tensor-parallel (TP>1) with MLA; also PP-aware per comment, multi-engine / data-parallel or cross-restart (inconsistent lora_int_id across engines)] 配置下显现；The diff extends prefix-cache retention (state-snapshot caching) to Mamba/linear-attention KV cache groups, adding a reachable_block_mask that sparsifies which

In the newly-added EAGLE spec-decode gating logic in execute_model, `input_fits_in_drafter` is computed via `spec_decode_common_attn_metadata.seq_lens.max()`. `CommonAttentionMetadata.seq_lens` is the GPU device tensor (see vllm/v1/attention/backends/utils.py L57 `seq_lens: torch.Tensor`, distinct from the `seq_lens_cpu` field on L58; populated on device via `self.seq_lens.copy_to_gpu()`). Calling `.max()` on this device tensor and then comparing it against a Python int (`+ num_speculative_tokens <= effective_drafter_max_model_len`) forces an implicit GPU->CPU synchronization on every decode step in the EAGLE padded-batch hot path. This defeats the whole purpose of the padded-batch fast path, which is designed to run draft proposal immediately without waiting for bookkeeping/D2H copies (see the comment 'does not need to wait for bookkeeping to finish'). The correct field to read is `seq_lens_cpu.max()` (used elsewhere in the codebase, e.g. utils.py L161 `int(seq_lens_cpu.max())`), or the already-materialized host `max_seq_len` scalar on the metadata.
findings.suggested_benchmark: Decode-step latency (tokens/s and per-step time) for an EAGLE spec-decode model with disable_padded_drafter_batch=False; compare seq_lens.max() (device) vs seq_lens_cpu.max() (host) to isolate the D2H sync stall.

findings.claim: 此改动触及 cudagraph-incompatibility（compilation），该类问题历史上主要在 [TP-aware (cuda_graph_step_size rounded to TP size); dynamic inference backend, tensor-parallel (uses model_comm_pgs.tp); full-iteration CUDA graph, virtual pipeline parallelism (VPP/interleaved) + CUDA graphs (enable_cuda_graph, cuda_graph_scope != full_iteration)] 配置下显现；This commit replaces the previous large_context_topk kernel with a new "persistent" TopK scheduler for the DeepSeek-V3.2 DSA indexer decode path, explicitly adv

On SM90 (Hopper) the diff unconditionally disables FA4 whenever both FA3 and FA4 are available ('Disabling FlashAttention 4 to prefer FlashAttention 3 on SM90'). This hard preference for FA3 over FA4 on Hopper is a deliberate backend-swap gate. If FA4's SM90 forward path is faster for common non-MLA / standard-head-dim shapes, this gate forces the (potentially slower) FA3 kernel and leaves FA4 perf unreachable on Hopper. Matches the fused-backend-swap / suboptimal-kernel-gate history where a specialized fast backend is gated off in favor of a more 'mature' one. Confirm FA3>=FA4 perf assumption on SM90 for the shapes covered (the code itself only cites correctness limitations of FA4's SM90 *backward*, not forward, yet disables FA4 entirely including inference/forward).
findings.suggested_benchmark: Benchmark FA3 vs FA4 forward+backward on SM90 for standard head dims (64/128) across seqlens; compare tokens/s to confirm FA3 is not slower than FA4 for inference-only and non-MLA training.

findings.claim: static rule 'tensor-from-pylist-on-device' matched antipattern: Constructing a tensor from a Python list literal with a non-CPU device forces a CPU materialization plus a host-to-device copy on every call; use torch.zeros/ones/full/empty directly on the device instead. (matched: 'torch.tensor(\n                [0, num_actual_tokens], dtype=torch.int32, device=')

In the refactored build(), the new prefill pathway computes `max_q_len_prefill = int(query_lens_prefill_cpu.max().item())` where `query_lens_prefill_cpu` is derived from `qo_indptr_prefill_cpu` (a CPU tensor slice of `query_start_loc_cpu`). Because the source is CPU-resident, this `.max().item()` does NOT force a device->host sync — it matches the parent's behavior (`query_lens_prefill.max().item()` on `qo_indptr_cpu`). This is safe and does not introduce a new d2h sync. Noted for completeness given the historical FlashInfer plan() d2h-sync pattern.

此改动触及 buffer-sizing-layout（memory-management），该类问题历史上主要在 [MoE / expert-parallel, GroupedMLP with num_local_experts>=1, MoE with expert parallelism; recommended when num_local_experts > 1; dropless training (moe_expert_capacity_factor is None), MoE (EP/TP), affects both AllGather and AllToAll dispatchers and Grouped/TEGrouped MLP] 配置下显现；The diff changes the KV cache stride order / physical memory layout for MLA attention backends, switching from a cross-layer allocation permutation (1,0,2,3) to

This is a bugfix (Bugfix-tagged) that intentionally DISABLES the cross-layer/uniform KV cache layout for MLA attention backends. Previously MLACommonBackend.get_kv_cache_stride_order(include_num_layers_dimension=True) returned (1,0,2,3), which moved num_layers off the front and enabled the single contiguous cross-layer buffer in allocate_uniform_kv_caches()/use_uniform_kv_cache(). The diff changes it to identity (0,1,2,3) and updates use_uniform_kv_cache() to reject stride_order[0]==0. As a result, when a KV connector with prefer_cross_layer_blocks (e.g. offloading/NIXL) is used with an MLA model, KV transfers revert to per-layer swap_blocks/transfers instead of one contiguous per-block transfer for all layers. This matches the historical 'cross-layer KV cache layout' pattern (symptom: latency) but is the REVERSE direction: it removes the optimized layout for correctness. Since MLA kernels require contiguous per-layer views, the previous layout was incorrect for MLA, so this is a correctness-motivated revert of an optimization, not a newly-introduced regression.
findings.suggested_benchmark: Disaggregated prefill/decode or offloading KV-transfer throughput on a DeepSeek/MLA model with an offloading or NIXL connector; compare per-block cross-layer transfer count vs per-layer transfers before/after.

findings.claim: 此改动触及 scheduler-work-distribution（parallelism-scheduling），该类问题历史上主要在 [data-parallel group >1 (FullyParallelSaveStrategyWrapper); affects distributed checkpoint save, data-parallel (with context-parallel) group; benefits scale with DP size >1, data-parallel / distributed checkpointing (parallelization_group across DP ranks, group_size>1)] 配置下显现；The change modifies the DP coordinator's internal load-balancing logic: how request/step counts are tracked and how load stats are published to front-end procs

This PR is the load-balancing rework itself and primarily FIXES the routing-imbalance pattern from the priors rather than introducing it. In the parent, get_core_engine_for_request used idx=(client_index+i)%num_engines, so with multiple front-end API servers (num_servers>1) every client began its scan at the same client_index-derived offset and, on ties (empty engines), all clients funneled new requests to the same lowest-index engine -> GPU bubble/imbalance. The PR derives eng_start_index=(len(core_engines)*client_index)//client_count to spread client start offsets across the engine ring. This matches historical leaf 'scheduler-work-distribution' but as a corrective, not a regression. No new distribution regression is evident from the routing change.
findings.suggested_benchmark: Multi-client (num_servers>1) internal-DP serving throughput/per-engine utilization under bursty arrivals; measure request skew across DP engines.

findings.claim: The new guard `is_functorch_transforming()` is added at the top of `_backward_prologue_per_tensor`, which is registered as the per-tensor `preprocess_per_tensor_fn` and therefore invoked once per gradient tensor during every backward (a hot path). The function calls `getattr(torch._C, '_functorch', None)` and `getattr(_functorch, 'peek_interpreter_stack', None)` on every invocation. These attribute lookups + a C-level interpreter-stack peek run per-parameter on the normal `engine.backward()` path even when torch.func is never used. For large models with many parameters this adds a small but non-trivial constant overhead to each backward step. Consider caching the resolved `peek_interpreter_stack` callable at import time (resolve getattr once) so only the cheap `peek() is not None` check runs per tensor.
findings.suggested_benchmark: Micro-benchmark engine.backward() step time for a model with many parameters (ZeRO-1/2), comparing per-tensor hook overhead with vs without the getattr-based guard.

The new MIMO loss_func closely mirrors the reference pretrain_gpt loss_func and keeps all tensor arithmetic on-device: `num_tokens = mask.sum().to(torch.int)` and `loss_sum = masked.sum()` stay on GPU, and the logging dict uses `torch.stack((loss_sum.detach(), num_tokens.detach().float()))` (device-side stack), not a Python-list constructor. No `.item()`/`float()`/`assert <cuda-bool>` host reads are introduced. The schedule (schedules.py) consumes num_tokens via `output_tensor /= torch.clamp(num_tokens, min=1)` and `total_num_tokens += num_tokens`, all on-device, so no D2H sync is triggered per step. This distinguishes it from the historical [blocking-d2h-sync] case where `torch.tensor([gpu_scalar, gpu_scalar], device=...)` forced a device->host read each iteration.

findings.claim: CASE 2 of _apply_rotary_pos_emb_thd is reverted from a single-pass implementation (parent: build packed freqs once, call _apply_rotary_pos_emb_bshd ONCE on the whole packed tensor) back to a per-sequence loop that calls _apply_rotary_pos_emb_bshd(x.unsqueeze(1), ...) once for every packed sequence. Each _apply_rotary_pos_emb_bshd invocation materializes torch.cos(freqs)*mscale and torch.sin(freqs)*mscale (2 elementwise kernels), runs _rotate_half (chunk+cat), and does a final torch.cat — so the number of these elementwise/cat kernel launches now scales with the number of packed sequences B instead of being constant. This is the exact per-sequence-loop RoPE pattern the earlier optimization removed; the change is made for correctness (later packed sequences must not look like continuations of the first) but reintroduces the throughput cost on the unfused THD hot path (called per attention layer per forward via apply_rotary_pos_emb, e.g. absorbed_mla / dsa).
findings.suggested_benchmark: Microbench _apply_rotary_pos_emb_thd (unfused, freqs=max-seqlen format so CASE 2 is taken) with a packed batch of many short sequences (e.g. cu_seqlens for 32 sequences) vs the parent single-pass version; compare kernel-launch count and wall time.

The new runtime guard in transformer_engine/common/__init__.py adds a subprocess spawn (`ldconfig -p`) at module import time. `_is_cusolvermp_installed_in_system()` is the LEFT operand of the `and`, so it is always evaluated during import (whenever the top-level NVTE_PROJECT_BUILDING/NVTE_RELEASE_BUILD block runs), independent of whether the cuSolverMp PyPI packages are installed. This forks a child process and does string scanning over its stdout on every fresh `import transformer_engine`. It is guarded by @functools.lru_cache so the cost is one-time per process, but it still adds a fork+exec of the external `ldconfig` binary to the import-time critical path. Reordering so the cheaper `_is_package_installed(...)` check short-circuits first would avoid the subprocess in the common case where the cusolvermp wheels are absent.
findings.suggested_benchmark: Measure `python -c 'import transformer_engine.common'` cold import time with and without the ldconfig subprocess (e.g. time 100 iterations of a fresh subprocess import) to quantify the added startup overhead.

In qa/L2_jax_unittest/test.sh the `export XLA_FLAGS="${XLA_FLAGS} --xla_gpu_deterministic_ops"` line was moved to BEFORE the MNIST pytest invocation (previously it was set only after MNIST, just ahead of the encoder tests). As a result the MNIST training run now also executes under XLA deterministic-ops mode, which forces XLA to select deterministic (typically slower) GPU kernels — disabling faster non-deterministic reductions/atomics — purely to obtain run-to-run reproducibility for stable CI. This matches the 'correctness/determinism forces the slow path' mechanism: the intent is determinism, and the cost is extra CI wall-clock for the MNIST job. This is a deliberate test-stability trade-off and applies only to CI (not shipped runtime code), so impact is limited to CI duration.
findings.suggested_benchmark: Compare MNIST L2 pytest wall-clock time with vs without --xla_gpu_deterministic_ops to quantify the deterministic-kernel slowdown for the mnist example run.

findings.claim: In `MiniCPMVProcessingInfo.get_hf_processor` (minicpmv.py) and `MiniCPMOProcessingInfo.get_hf_processor` (minicpmo.py), a brand-new vendored processor object (`MiniCPMVProcessor` / `MiniCPMOProcessor`) is now constructed on every call. The underlying HF processor from `self.ctx.get_hf_processor(**kwargs)` is cached via `cached_processor_from_config`, but the new wrapper instantiation is NOT cached, so each `get_hf_processor` call re-runs `ProcessorMixin.__init__` and (in the MiniCPMO path) re-scans the image processor's `mean`/`std` numpy arrays converting them to lists. `get_hf_processor` is invoked during per-request multimodal preprocessing (processor.py:1111 / 1249). This adds per-request setup work that was previously served from the cached processor. Consider caching the vendored wrapper (e.g. functools.cache keyed on config/kwargs) to keep it a one-time-setup rather than repeated per request.

Gemma4MTP._get_full_lm_head_weight() performs a tensor_model_parallel_all_gather of the full lm_head weight (vocab_size x hidden_size) on EVERY call, and it is invoked once per draft step via compute_logits/get_top_tokens (the sparse centroid path). `_has_module` previously only ran `importlib.util.find_spec(module_name)` (a cheap disk/metadata lookup). The diff now additionally performs a full `importlib.import_module(module_name)` for every module whose spec is found. Because `_has_module` is the shared backend for all optional-dependency probes (`has_deep_gemm`, `has_deep_ep`, `has_aiter`, `has_triton_kernels`, `has_tilelang`, `has_fbgemm_gpu`, `has_cutedsl`, etc.), any of these now eagerly imports large native/GPU packages (deep_gemm, aiter, triton_kernels, fbgemm_gpu, cutlass) just to answer an availability check. Fully importing such modules executes their top-level side effects and loads shared libraries, which materially increases process startup/init time compared to the prior spec-only check. This partially defeats the intent of lazy/optional detection.
findings.suggested_benchmark: Measure cold-start / import time of a model init path that calls several has_* helpers (e.g. deep_gemm/aiter/fbgemm probes) on an environment where those packages are installed; compare parent vs this commit.

The change to _init_mrope_positions only replaces an assertion with a branch that, for the prompt_embeds-only case, builds `list(range(seq_len))` via `input_tokens = list(range(seq_len))`. While this is an O(L) allocation of a token-index list, _init_mrope_positions is a one-time per-request setup path (called during request init/prefill setup, not per decode step), so it does not repeat every iteration and does not match the hot-path/quadratic patterns from the priors. The list is only materialized on the prompt_embeds branch and is proportional to sequence length (bounded by max_model_len). No hot-path regression is introduced. The prompt_token_ids path is unchanged.

The new ApertusToolParser.extract_tool_calls calls `self.tool_call_regex.search(model_output)` with NO timeout. Every other tool parser in the codebase (pythonic, llama4_pythonic, llama, olmo3, lfm2) passes `timeout=envs.VLLM_TOOL_PARSE_REGEX_TIMEOUT_SECONDS` to its regex match precisely because untrusted/adversarial model output can trigger catastrophic backtracking (ReDoS) that hangs the request-handling thread indefinitely inside the `regex` engine. This new parser breaks that convention: the regex `<|tools_prefix|>(.*?)(?:<|tools_suffix|>|$)` with re.DOTALL is invoked on the full model_output on the hot serving path (extract_tool_calls per completion) without any guard, so pathological output can stall the thread. Add the `timeout=envs.VLLM_TOOL_PARSE_REGEX_TIMEOUT_SECONDS` argument (with a TimeoutError catch) as the other parsers do.
findings.suggested_benchmark: Feed extract_tool_calls an adversarial/large model_output string that maximizes regex backtracking (e.g. long text containing many near-matches of '<|tools_prefix|>') and measure wall-clock time; compare against parsers that pass VLLM_TOOL_PARSE_REGEX_TIMEOUT_SECONDS which cap at ~1s.

apply_structural_tag_constraint() is called on the per-request DefaultChatOutputProcessor::new() path (invoked once per chat completion request via new_chat_output_processor in backend/hf.rs). For strict/required/named tool calls it calls xgrammar_structural_tag::build_structural_tag(model, &tools, tool_choice, false) followed by tag.to_json_string() on every request. build_structural_tag compiles a grammar/structural-tag over all request tools' JSON schemas and then serializes it to a JSON string. This is compilation-like work whose input (the served model + the tool set) is often identical across requests, yet nothing here is cached — each request that enables tool parsing pays the full grammar-build + JSON-serialize cost inline before generation begins, adding one-time-style setup latency to the hot request path. Consider memoizing the built structural-tag by (model, tools hash, tool_choice) or caching on the backend when tools are stable.
findings.suggested_benchmark: Benchmark DefaultChatOutputProcessor::new / apply_structural_tag_constraint latency with tool_choice=required and a large tools list (e.g. 20+ tools with sizeable JSON schemas), comparing per-request cost vs. a cached structural tag.

This diff removes the `fastsafetensors` runtime dependency from `requirements/rocm.txt` (and cleans up the constraint reference in requirements/test/rocm.txt). This is the inverse of the historical `weight-load-strategy` case, which *added* fastsafetensors to ROCm to enable a GPU-accelerated faster safetensors weight-load path. The fastsafetensors loader is strictly opt-in (only reached when the user passes `--load-format fastsafetensors`; see default_loader.py `_get_weights_iterator` and weight_utils.py which imports it lazily behind a try/except with a PlaceholderModule fallback). Because the code path is guarded and the module is only imported when explicitly selected, removing the dep does NOT slow down the default ROCm weight-load path — it only means users who explicitly request `--load-format fastsafetensors` on a ROCm image built from this Dockerfile will hit the PlaceholderModule/ImportError instead of the GPU-direct fast path (i.e., they must now install it manually). No hot-path regression is introduced; the impact is a startup-time slowdown ONLY for users who had been relying on the pre-bundled fastsafetensors fast loader on ROCm. The removal is a deliberate packaging fix (git+ URL incompatible with uv), not a mistaken default toggle on a default code path.
findings.suggested_benchmark: On a ROCm build, measure model weight-load/startup time with `--load-format fastsafetensors` before vs after this change to confirm the fast loader is unavailable (expected to error out / fall back) rather than silently regressing the default `--load-format auto` path.

In _get_reqs_to_store (called every scheduler step via build_connector_meta for every running/cached request), the manager.touch call was changed from a bounded, prefix-limited touch `self.manager.touch(group_state.offload_keys[:num_blocks])` to `for group_state in req_status.group_states: self.manager.touch(group_state.offload_keys)`. This now touches the FULL offload_keys list for every group on every step, whereas before it touched only the first num_blocks entries and only for the single group. touch() (e.g. LRUCachePolicy.touch) does `for key in reversed(list(keys)): if key in self.blocks: self.blocks.move_to_end(key)` — O(len(offload_keys)) dict lookups + move_to_end per request per step. Since offload_keys grows monotonically with the sequence length, the per-step cost grows linearly with total context length and is repeated every decode step, giving O(context_len) work each step (O(context_len^2) accumulated over the generation). This is redundant hot-path work: only newly stored blocks need to be touched/refreshed, not the whole history each step.
findings.suggested_benchmark: Offloading-enabled decode throughput with long contexts (e.g. 8k-32k prompt/generation) measuring per-step scheduler build_connector_meta time as sequence length grows; compare touch() call cost vs baseline.

findings.claim: The store-eligibility loop was restructured so that on every scheduler step, for each request and each KV group, the code re-slices `group_state.offload_keys[start_block_idx:num_blocks]` and iterates the GPU block_ids twice (once to filter skipped blocks into new_offload_keys, then again in the src_block_ids assembly loop). In the common decode case only ~1 new block boundary is crossed per block_size tokens, so this per-group double pass runs frequently but on small ranges; the concern is that when start_block_idx does not advance (num_blocks <= start_block_idx handled) the filter loop still executes and now also unconditionally runs the full-list touch. Consider gating the touch/filter work on num_blocks > start_block_idx per group as the parent did with `num_new_blocks <= 0: continue`.
findings.suggested_benchmark: Micro-benchmark _get_reqs_to_store steady-state decode steps (no new blocks) counting per-request iterations over offload_keys.

findings.claim: The new InflightRequests introduces a single global Arc<Mutex<HashMap<String, HashMap<String,usize>>>> that is locked on the per-request submission hot path: every Llm::generate() calls inflight.track() (one lock/insert with two String clones + nested HashMap alloc), and every GenerateOutputStream drop calls RequestGuard::drop() (another lock). Since one mutex guards ALL concurrent in-flight requests, high request-submission/completion concurrency serializes track()/drop() on this lock. abort() (resolve) also takes the same lock. This mirrors the shared-mutable-state-contention pattern (threads serialize on contended shared data). Under normal loads the critical section is tiny so impact is likely modest, but the design centralizes all generate/drop traffic on a single lock. Consider sharding the map or using a lock-free structure if request throughput is high.
findings.suggested_benchmark: Concurrent generate()+drop throughput benchmark: spawn N tasks each doing generate() then dropping the stream, measure aggregate req/s and lock contention on InflightRequests::map as concurrency scales.

findings.claim: `_build_tool_maps(request.tools)` is invoked from inside the per-token streaming hot path: `extract_tool_calls_streaming` -> `_process_complete_block_streaming` (called for every completed `<function>...</function>` block on every streaming invocation) and `_process_partial_block_streaming` both begin with `_build_tool_maps(request.tools)`. `_build_tool_maps` rebuilds four collections from scratch every call — iterating all tools and constructing `name_to_tool`, `name_to_allowed_props` (set(props.keys())), `name_to_required` (set(required)) — which is init-shaped work that is identical for the whole request and does not depend on the streaming position. Because streaming re-invokes this method once per generated token (and once per block matched inside a single invocation), the tool maps are recomputed O(tokens × tools) times over a generation. This mirrors the historical redundant-hot-path-work pattern (property/getter re-invoked per loop iteration). Fix: compute the tool maps once in __init__ / on first invocation (or cache keyed by request) and reuse. Note `extract_tool_calls` (non-streaming) builds it once per call which is fine; the regression is on the streaming path.
findings.suggested_benchmark: Streaming a response containing several tool calls with a large `request.tools` list (e.g. 50+ tools each with many properties); measure per-token latency / total streaming time in extract_tool_calls_streaming vs a version that caches _build_tool_maps once.

The new POST /tokenize handler runs the CPU-bound tokenizer.encode() synchronously directly inside the async axum handler, blocking the tokio worker thread. In `tokenize_completion` (routes/tokenize.rs) `tokenizer.encode(&req.prompt, req.add_special_tokens)` is called inline on the async task; likewise the chat path calls `ChatLlm::tokenize_chat` which does `tokenizer.encode(&text, ...)` inline (chat/src/lib.rs). This mirrors the historical Rust/tokio analogue of gil-held-blocking-call: a large-prompt tokenize is a blocking CPU operation that stalls the async executor and delays all other requests sharing that worker thread. Notably, this codebase already established the correct pattern elsewhere: `multimodal::preprocess_images` explicitly wraps CPU-heavy work in `tokio::task::spawn_blocking` with the comment 'The processor work is CPU-heavy relative to request wiring, so it runs in a blocking task.' The new /tokenize path diverges from that convention and does not offload encode/decode to a blocking pool.
findings.suggested_benchmark: Concurrent load test: submit many /tokenize requests with very large prompts (e.g. 100k+ chars) while simultaneously hitting /v1/chat/completions; measure p99 latency and event-loop stall on the chat endpoint with vs without the tokenize load. Compare offloading encode via spawn_blocking.

findings.claim: The /detokenize handler similarly decodes token IDs synchronously inside the async handler (tokenizer.decode inline in the new detokenize fn). For large token arrays this is the same blocking-CPU-in-async-task pattern and should also be offloaded to spawn_blocking to avoid stalling the tokio worker under concurrent load.
findings.suggested_benchmark: Concurrent /detokenize with large token arrays measuring async runtime latency impact on co-located inference endpoints.

findings.claim: Both ChatMessage and DeltaMessage gain a `@model_serializer(mode="wrap")` custom serializer that runs on every serialization. DeltaMessage in particular is serialized once per streaming chunk (chat_completion_stream_generator calls chunk.model_dump_json(exclude_unset=True) at serving.py lines 533/569/751 for every delta token). The wrap serializer adds a Python-level callback (handler(self) + dict.get + dict.pop) around each dump, converting what was a pure Rust/pydantic-core serialization into one that invokes a Python function per message. On high-throughput streaming (many tokens/sec across many concurrent requests) this is per-token overhead on a hot path. Note: this matches the historical redundant-hot-path-work mechanism (work repeated every iteration), but the incremental cost is small and there is an existing precedent (ChatCompletionToolsParam._serialize), so severity is low.
findings.suggested_benchmark: Streaming chat completion throughput benchmark (tokens/sec) with many concurrent streaming requests, comparing per-chunk model_dump_json latency before/after adding the DeltaMessage wrap serializer.

此改动触及 warmup-shape-gap（compilation），该类问题历史上主要在 [context-parallel (cp>1) and tensor-parallel; sequence_parallel affects shapes, sequence parallelism enabled with tensor_model_parallel_size > 1, sequence-parallel with tensor_model_parallel_size>1] 配置下显现；This adds a monitor that detects Triton JIT compilation/autotuning happening during inference (post-warmup), which is precisely the observable symptom of a warm

`activate()` is unconditionally called at the end of `compile_or_warm_up_model` with no env-var/opt-out gate for the hook itself (only autotuning-print honors TRITON_PRINT_AUTOTUNING=0). Setting `knobs.autotuning.print = True` globally also changes Triton autotuning to always print, which can add per-autotune I/O overhead. Since activation happens post-warmup and the hook is only triggered on actual (undesired) compilations, this is low risk, but there is no way to disable the monitor without editing code. Consider gating behind an env flag defaulting off to avoid unconditionally mutating global Triton knobs in every worker.

The new RVV `interleave_save_16b` helper (csrc/cpu/cpu_types_riscv_impl.hpp) implements the K-interleaving via a scalar per-element loop (`for i in VEC_ELEM_NUM: packed[i] = values0[i] | (values1[i]<<16)`) after two `.save()` spills to stack buffers, whereas the x86 version uses vectorized interleave intrinsics. HOWEVER, in cpu_wna16.cpp `interleave_save` is only called under `if constexpr (isa == ISA::AMX)` — RISC-V exclusively uses the ISA::VEC path (plain .save()), so this scalar loop is effectively dead code on RISC-V and does not affect the runtime hot path. Not a real regression as wired today; flagged only as a latent slow-path if a RISC-V AMX-like tiled path is ever enabled.
findings.suggested_benchmark: WNA16 GEMM microbench on RISC-V (ISA::VEC) to confirm interleave_save is not exercised

HrmTextAttention allocates one PrefillPrefixLMAttention instance per (recurrence step, layer) — global_idx = step * num_layers_per_stack + layer_idx_in_stack — giving each recurrence step its own KV cache slot. Since HrmTextModel.forward runs nested H_cycles x L_cycles loops re-invoking the L/H stacks, the total number of distinct KV-cache-backed attention layers is num_layers_per_stack * (H_cycles*(L_cycles+1)) rather than num_layers_per_stack. This multiplies KV cache footprint by the recurrence depth, sharply reducing the number of concurrent sequences that fit in a fixed KV budget (lower max concurrency / throughput). With prefix caching also disabled (above), the memory pressure has no reuse mitigation.
findings.suggested_benchmark: Measure kv_cache_size_tokens / max concurrency reported at engine init for HRM-Text vs. a same-hidden-size single-pass decoder, and end-to-end throughput at high concurrency.

findings.claim: HrmTextForCausalLM introduces PrefillPrefixLMAttention.get_kv_cache_spec() which flips FullAttentionSpec.non_causal=True; the new engine-core logic (vllm/v1/engine/core.py) reads this flag and disables both prefix caching and chunked prefill for the whole engine when any such layer is present. This is functionally the same mechanism as the historical prefix-state-caching regressions (a spec/config flag force-disables prefix caching). For HRM-Text this is correctness-required (bidirectional prefill would be corrupted by shared-prefix reuse / chunking), so it is not a spurious regression, BUT the disable is engine-wide: with prefix caching turned off, any shared-prefix reuse across requests is lost, and chunked prefill is also disabled, hurting throughput/TTFT for large prompts. Confirm the disable is scoped only to HRM-Text deployments and does not leak to unrelated models sharing the engine core.
findings.suggested_benchmark: Throughput/TTFT with a long shared system prompt across many requests, comparing an HRM-Text run against a causal decoder of similar size; verify non-HRM models in the same process still get prefix caching.

findings.claim: get_safetensors_params_metadata now adds a new fallback branch that calls huggingface_hub.snapshot_download(..., local_files_only=True) whenever the Hub metadata fetch (try_get_safetensors_metadata) returns empty/None. This runs on the config-parse / quantization-setup startup path (callers: awq/awq_marlin/auto_gptq/cpu_wna16 quantization configs, MistralConfigParser, model_arch_config_convertor). Previously a failed Hub fetch just returned {} cheaply; now every miss triggers a local-cache snapshot resolution and a directory glob over *.safetensors, i.e. extra filesystem/index work on each startup where the fast metadata path fails. For a model like bloom-560m loaded from local cache this is one-time startup cost, not a per-request hot-path regression, and it is guarded (only fires after the primary path fails). Net effect is bounded startup-time overhead added to the load path, acceptable given it de-flakes offline/rate-limited scenarios.
findings.suggested_benchmark: Time engine init / config load for a locally-cached model with HF_HUB_OFFLINE and with a mocked Hub 429 to measure the added snapshot_download+glob cost on the fallback path.
